## BM-01 · disqualifier-scan — Disqualifier Scan — HCG

### Disqualifier Scan — HCG

HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Scan date: 2026-06-01.

Scope note: HCG is an Indian SEBI-LODR issuer reporting under Ind AS. The most recent *audited* annual report in the pool is FY2024-25 (year ended 31 Mar 2025; auditor's report signed 24 May 2025). FY26 results (year ended 31 Mar 2026) were announced 20 May 2026 and are covered by transcript and deck only — the FY26 audited annual report is not in the pool. The 3-year auditor lookback below therefore covers FY23, FY24 and FY25 (the audited years available), cross-checked against the FY26 transcript/deck for any late-breaking event. All disqualifier ratios use **consolidated** figures, because related-party transactions and "customers" that are HCG's own subsidiaries eliminate on consolidation.

#### 1. Disqualifier Check

| # | Disqualifier | Triggered (Y/N) | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Auditor qualification or going-concern note (last 3 years) | N | Unqualified ("true and fair view"), no going-concern modification, in both audited years on hand. FY25 standalone + consolidated opinions, B S R & Co. LLP, 24 May 2025 [FY24-25 Annual Report, Independent Auditor's Report, print p.216-217 (standalone) and p.304 (consolidated)]. FY24 standalone opinion also unqualified [FY23-24 Annual Report, Independent Auditor's Report, print p.201]. The only key audit matter is impairment of investments/goodwill — a standard estimate KAM, not a qualification. CARO has one minor adverse remark: clause i(c), title deeds of some properties held in names of merged entities [FY24-25 AR, Annexure A, print p.309]. |
| 2 | >50% promoter / insider shares pledged | N | No promoter-share pledge or encumbrance is disclosed anywhere in the pool. Promoter and promoter group hold 71.22%; no pledged figure shown [FY24-25 AR, "Shareholding pattern" chart, print p.64]. Word-by-word search of both annual reports found no "shares pledged" / "encumbered" / "non-disposal of promoter shares" statement; the only "pledge" / "non-disposal" references are the *Company* pledging its own investments in subsidiaries as borrowing collateral [FY24-25 AR, print p.236 ("non-disposal undertaking of its investment"); Notes 17/18]. Promoter is institutional (Aceso Company Pte Ltd / CVC Capital Partners Asia V L.P., transitioning to KKR) [FY24-25 AR, Note 44/43, print p.288/371]. Data limitation: the FY25 shareholding pattern predates the KKR control change; no post-changeover Reg-31 pledge disclosure is in the pool (see §3). |
| 3 | Related-party transactions >25% of revenue or expenses | N | Consolidated RPTs are a fraction of a percent of revenue. Management's own BRSR ratio: Share of RPTs in Sales = 0.0012% (FY25), 0.07% (FY24); Share of RPTs in Purchases = 0 [FY24-25 AR, BRSR "Open-ness of business", print p.192]. The consolidated RPT note explicitly eliminates intra-group transactions; remaining outside-party RPTs (KMP pay, CSR trust, GHA academy, Irelia Sports) total roughly Rs 200-250M against consolidated revenue from operations of Rs 22,181.36M and total expenses of Rs 22,014.71M [FY24-25 AR, Consolidated Note 43, print p.371-373; Consolidated P&L, print p.313]. ~1% of revenue and of expenses — far below 25%. |
| 4 | Auditor changed twice in last 3 years without disclosed reason | N | Same auditor across the audited years on hand: B S R & Co. LLP (FRN 101248W/W-100022) signed both FY25 [FY24-25 AR, print p.220/308] and FY24 [FY23-24 AR, Independent Auditor's Report, PDF p.266-267]. No auditor change, let alone two. |
| 5 | Material restatement (>5% of revenue or net income) in last 2 years | N | FY24 comparatives were restated, but only for a **business combination under common control** (slump-sale of the Nagpur oncology/hospital business from HCG NCHRI Oncology LLP), accounted under Appendix C to Ind AS 103 pooling-of-interests, which requires retrospective restatement from 01 Apr 2023 [FY24-25 AR, Note 45.1, print p.294; auditor "Other Matter", print p.218]. This is a mandatory pooling presentation, not a correction of error or misstatement; the auditor's opinion is "not modified in respect of this matter." No error/fraud restatement disclosed. |
| 6 | Active regulatory enforcement action on financial reporting | N | No enforcement action disclosed. BRSR Principle 1: Penalty/Fine, Settlement, Compounding fee all "N/A" (monetary and non-monetary); directors/KMPs/employees against whom any law-enforcement disciplinary action was taken for bribery/corruption = 0/0/0/0 for FY25 and FY24; corrective action on regulator matters = "Not applicable" [FY24-25 AR, BRSR Principle 1 Q2/Q5/Q7, print p.191-192]. The only "SEBI" references in the FY26 filings are routine Reg-30/Schedule III LODR cover-letter headings, not actions [FY26 transcript & deck cover pages]. |
| 7 | >40% of revenue from single customer with no long-term contract | N | HCG is a cancer-hospital network; revenue is from individual patients across 25 hospitals in 18 cities [FY26 transcript, prepared remarks/Q&A, PDF p.8], so single-customer concentration is structurally absent. The Capital IQ "customers" list is entirely HCG's own subsidiaries/LLPs (HCG Eko, HCG Manavata, Apex HCG, HCG NCHRI, etc.), which eliminate on consolidation [Capital IQ Customers export, rows 14-35]. BRSR: Sales to top 10 dealers/distributors as % of total sales = 0 [FY24-25 AR, BRSR "Open-ness of business", print p.192]. No external customer at/above 40%. |
| 8 | Negative operating cash flow in 3 of last 4 years (excl. growth-stage) | N | Operating cash flow is positive in every year, not negative. Consolidated "Cash from Ops." (Rs M): FY21 +1,205.21; FY22 +2,201.24; FY23 +2,515.91; FY24 +2,845.79; FY25 +3,171.09; FY26 (press release) +3,471.0 [Capital IQ Cash Flow export, "Cash from Ops." row, periods ending Mar-31-2021 to Mar-31-2026]. FY23/FY24/FY25 tie to the audited consolidated cash flow statements [FY24-25 AR Consolidated Cash Flow, print p.314; FY23-24 AR Consolidated Cash Flow, PDF p.272]. Zero negative-CFO years in the last 4 (or 6). Not an early-stage business — HCG is profitable (consolidated PAT FY25 Rs 488.34M) and cash-generative. |

#### 2. Triggered Disqualifiers — Detail

No disqualifier triggered.

Two items were examined closely because they superficially resemble triggers, and both were cleared on primary evidence:

- **The FY24 restatement (Disqualifier 5)** is a pooling-of-interests restatement for a common-control business combination (the Nagpur slump-sale), mandated by Appendix C to Ind AS 103, not a correction of an accounting error or misstatement. The auditor's "Other Matter" paragraph states the opinion is "not modified" in respect of it [FY24-25 AR, print p.218; Note 45.1, print p.294]. A separate FY25 acquisition (Vizag/VHCRPL, 51% acquired Oct 2024) is normal purchase accounting with goodwill of Rs 2,070.14M, also not an error restatement [FY24-25 AR, Note 45.1, print p.373-374].

- **The promoter changeover (Disqualifier 2 context)** — between the FY25 annual report (Sep 2025) and now, control has been moving from CVC/Aceso to KKR, and founder Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar is reclassified as "Promoter and Non-Executive Chairman" [FY24-25 AR, print p.64 ("collaboration with KKR"); FY26 transcript, PDF p.8 ("promoter changeover and management change"); FY26 deck, PDF p.6]. This is a change of PE control plus a founder reclassification, not a pledge, enforcement, or audit event. No promoter-share pledge is disclosed in any document in the pool.

#### 3. Verdict-Lock Signal

- **Any disqualifier triggered:** N
- **If Y, names:** None
- **Action:** No disqualifier triggers. The synthesizer is NOT required to lock the verdict at "Low-quality business — avoid deeper work" on the basis of this scan. Deeper business-model work may proceed.

Data limitation to carry forward (does not change any verdict, flagged for honesty): the most recent *audited* shareholding/pledge disclosure on hand is the FY25 position (promoter 71.22%, no pledge disclosed). The pool does not contain the FY26 audited annual report or a post-KKR-changeover detailed Reg-31 shareholding pattern. Disqualifier 2 requires a named, sourced fact of >50% promoter shares pledged; no such fact exists in any available document, so it is N. Highest-value confirming item if it becomes available: the post-changeover Reg-31(1) shareholding pattern / FY26 corporate governance report promoter-encumbrance line.

## BM-02 · business-identity — Business Identity — HCG

### Business Identity — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Reporting currency INR. FY ends 31 March. Latest annual filing: Integrated Annual Report FY2024-25. Latest results: FY2025-26 (year ended 31 March 2026).*

#### 1. What The Company Actually Does

HCG runs a chain of cancer hospitals in India. It owns and operates comprehensive cancer centres that put diagnosis and the three treatment modalities — radiation oncology, medical oncology (chemotherapy), and surgical oncology — under one roof at each site, supported by molecular pathology, PET-CT imaging and genomic testing [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A "Our business" / Oncology, p.163]. As of 31 March 2025 the network was 22 comprehensive cancer centres (one of them in Nairobi, Kenya) plus 4 multispecialty hospitals, spanning 10 Indian states and 19 cities, with about 2,500 beds and a network of 400+ oncologists [FY24-25 Annual Report, "Geographic presence", p.14-15]. The customers are cancer patients and their families, who pay either out of pocket, through private insurance / third-party administrators, through corporate tie-ups, or through government schemes; a portion of revenue comes from medical-value-travel patients from Africa, the Middle East, SAARC and CIS countries [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.166; Q4/FY26 investor presentation, May 2026, payor-mix slide]. The company also runs Triesta Sciences, an in-house oncology diagnostics and genomics lab business (1 reference lab plus 12 diagnostic labs) that serves HCG and third-party hospitals, and Milann, a fertility / IVF chain (6 centres) [FY24-25 Annual Report, "Our specialised branches", p.10-11]. What the patient is paying to solve is a high-stakes, time-sensitive medical problem — getting an accurate cancer diagnosis and the full course of treatment in one place — in a country where roughly 70% of cancers present at an advanced stage and comprehensive oncology capacity is concentrated in a few metros [FY24-25 Annual Report, "HCG: Pioneering the future of cancer care", p.20-21]. Note: in May 2026 HCG agreed to sell 100% of the Milann fertility business to exit what it calls a "non-core" segment, so the fertility arm is on its way out of the company [Q4/FY26 investor presentation, May 2026, "Milann Divestment Update" slide].

#### 2. How The Company Makes Money

This is a hospital operator. Revenue is essentially a capacity-utilisation-times-price business at each centre:

- **Oncology hospitals (the core, largest piece):** `Revenue ≈ operational beds × occupancy × realization per patient + outpatient/diagnostic/pharmacy revenue`. Management tracks bed occupancy (58% as of FY26) plus a blended "centre utilisation" metric covering chemo-bed, operating-theatre and radiation-machine utilisation, and average revenue per patient [Q4/FY26 earnings call, May 20 2026, prepared remarks + Q&A; FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.32]. Within oncology, the FY26 revenue split by modality was medical oncology ~38%, surgical oncology ~21%, outpatient oncology services (OPD, diagnostics, pharmacy) ~16%, and radiation ~14%, with the balance non-oncology [Q4/FY26 investor presentation, May 2026, modality-mix slide].
- **Diagnostics (Triesta):** `Revenue ≈ tests performed × price per test`, across routine pathology, molecular and genomic (next-generation-sequencing) assays, sold both inside HCG and to outside hospitals [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A "Precision Diagnostics", p.166].
- **Fertility (Milann — being divested):** `Revenue ≈ IVF/ART cycles × price per cycle`; 1,287 successful IVF cycles in FY25 [FY24-25 Annual Report, "Our specialised branches", p.10-11].

Consolidated revenue from operations was ₹22,228 million in FY25 and ₹25,454 million in FY26, up about 15% year on year [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.167; Q4/FY26 investor presentation, May 2026, results slide].

What drives volume: number of operating centres, occupancy/utilisation at each centre, patient registrations, oncologist roster, and acquisitions (e.g., the Vizag/VHCRPL hospital consolidated from October 2024 added ₹501.7 million of revenue) [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.167]. What drives price (realization): case mix toward higher-acuity surgical and radiation work, and payor mix — cash and insurance/TPA patients pay more than government-scheme patients, and management is deliberately shifting toward the former (FY26 payor mix: cash + TPA ~56%, government ~33%, medical-value-travel ~2%, corporates/others ~9%) [Q4/FY26 investor presentation, May 2026, payor-mix slide; Q4/FY26 earnings call, May 20 2026, Q&A]. What drives margin: centre maturity. HCG classifies centres into monthly-revenue "buckets," and as a centre moves from a lower bucket to a higher one (after the fixed cost of beds and machines is already built out), its margin expands; FY26 Adjusted EBITDA was ₹4,711 million at an 18.5% margin (up ~68 bps), and pre-tax ROCE improved to 14% [Q4/FY26 investor presentation, May 2026, profitability slide; Q4/FY26 earnings call, May 20 2026, prepared remarks].

#### 3. Business Type Classification

Asset-heavy, single-specialty (oncology) hospital chain in India, monetised through bed/equipment utilisation and patient-level realization, with a centre-maturity ramp driving margins.

#### 4. What Drives Variance

When revenue moves, the usual causes are volume (occupancy and utilisation across chemo beds, operating theatres and radiation machines), new-centre ramp-up, and acquisitions — newer "emerging" centres grew far faster than mature ones (FY25: emerging centres +32% vs the network's lower-teens growth), so the mix of young vs mature centres swings the growth rate [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.152]. When margin moves, the cause is mostly mix — payor mix (cash/insurance vs government, which moved ~100 bps favourably over the prior two quarters) and case mix toward higher-realization surgical and radiation work — layered on the operating leverage of centres climbing into higher revenue buckets [Q4/FY26 earnings call, May 20 2026, Q&A]. Secondary swing factors are input costs (clinician/consultant fees, drug and consumables formulary, pay-per-use radiation-equipment charges) and the loss-making or sub-scale centres (notably some Mumbai assets in the sub-₹5-crore/month bucket) that drag blended margin until they ramp [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.167; Q4/FY26 earnings call, May 20 2026, Q&A]. FX is a minor factor, limited to the Kenya operation and international medical-tourism revenue [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.166].

---

## BM-03 · segment-map — Segment Map — HCG

### Segment Map — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787). All figures INR million (₹ Mn) unless stated. Audited segment/revenue disclosures from the FY2024-25 Integrated Annual Report (year ended 31 March 2025); FY2025-26 figures (year ended 31 March 2026) are from the company's Q4/FY26 investor deck and earnings call, as the FY26 annual report is not in the data pool.*

---

#### Headline

HCG reports **one** operating segment under Ind AS 108: **Medical and Healthcare Services** (cancer care). It is effectively a single-segment business — by the audited revenue-by-type split, ~93% of FY25 revenue is income from medical (hospital) services [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 22 (Consolidated)]. The "segments" that matter economically are not reported segments; they are business lines and maturity cohorts the company discloses only in its investor deck. The decisive structural fact: the **Milann fertility business is being divested** (signed May 2026), leaving an almost-pure oncology platform.

---

#### 1. Segment Table

There is only one reported operating segment. The table below shows (i) the single reported segment, and (ii) the economically meaningful business lines / cohorts HCG discloses outside the formal segment note. Rows (ii) are management/deck disclosures, not audited segment accounting — flagged accordingly.

##### (i) Reported operating segment (Ind AS 108)

| Segment | What It Does | Revenue Share | Profit Share | Margin Quality | Capital Intensity | Cyclicality | Main Risk |
|---|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Medical and Healthcare Services | Comprehensive cancer-care hospital network (medical/surgical/radiation oncology, diagnostics, nuclear medicine) + pharmacy + fertility (being divested) | 100% | 100% | Mid — FY26 reported EBITDA margin 18.3%, but PAT margin 0.5% after high D&A and finance cost | High — ₹13,928 Mn PP&E + ₹6,484 Mn right-of-use assets vs ₹25,454 Mn revenue (FY26) | Low–Mid — cancer care is largely non-deferrable demand | Single-line concentration; centre ramp-up risk; ₹9,104 Mn gross debt + ₹8,250 Mn lease liabilities |

Citations: single-segment statement — [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 36 (Consolidated), "The Company's sole operating segment is therefore 'Medical and Healthcare Services'"]. FY26 P&L (revenue ₹25,454.1 Mn; reported EBITDA ₹4,657.9 Mn / 18.3%; PAT ₹137.6 Mn / 0.5%) — [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Q4 & FY26 Consolidated Profit & Loss Account"]. PP&E / right-of-use / debt — [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Consolidated Balance Sheet" (Mar-26)].

##### (ii) Economically meaningful sub-splits (deck disclosure only — NOT audited segments)

**A. By business line (FY26 revenue, ₹ Mn):**

| Business line | What It Does | Revenue (FY26) | Revenue Share | Profit Share | Margin Quality | Capital Intensity | Cyclicality | Main Risk |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|---|---|
| Oncology hospital network (India) | 24 India cancer centres; the core platform | ~24,110 | ~94.7% of total | ~98.5% of Adj. EBITDA (see §2) | Mid — 18.7% Adj. EBITDA margin excl. fertility (FY26) | High | Low–Mid | Emerging-centre drag (see cohort table) |
| International (Kenya) | Nairobi oncology operations | 740 | ~2.9% | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | High | Mid | Single-country FX/political exposure; small base |
| Milann (fertility / IVF) | IVF and reproductive medicine; **being divested** | 605 | ~2.4% | ~1.5% of Adj. EBITDA (Adj. EBITDA ~₹69 Mn, see §2) | Low — goodwill on it impaired ₹319 Mn in FY26 | Mid | Mid | Exiting the business; deal close risk |

Citation: South+West+East = 9,950 + 11,330 + 2,830 = 24,110; Kenya 740 (grew 71% YoY from 433); Milann 605 (from 578). Sum = 25,455 ≈ total 25,454 — [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, region-wise slides "South / West / East" and "International and Milann"]. Goodwill impairment of fertility ₹319 Mn — [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, P&L note (1)].

**B. By geographic cluster — same oncology business, different regions (FY26 revenue, ₹ Mn):**

| Cluster | Revenue (FY26) | Share of India network | Utilisation (FY26) | Cyclicality | Main Risk |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| West (Gujarat, Maharashtra, Rajasthan, MP) | 11,330 | 45% | 50% | Low–Mid | Newer beds still ramping (lowest utilisation) |
| South (Karnataka, AP, Telangana, incl. Bangalore COE) | 9,950 | 39% | 68% | Low–Mid | Medical-value-travel softness |
| East (West Bengal, Odisha, Jharkhand) | 2,830 | 11% | 57% | Low–Mid | Kolkata case-mix / clinician transition |

Citation: cluster revenue ₹9,950 / ₹11,330 / ₹2,830 and HCG-overall ₹25,454; utilisation 68% / 50% / 57% (HCG overall 58%); cluster share % (South 39%, West 45%, East 11% — shares of the India hospital base) — [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Hospital Utilization and Growth Potential in Existing Centres" and region-wise slides]. These are geographic groupings of one business, not separate economic segments.

**C. By centre maturity — the most economically meaningful split (FY26, excl. Milann):**

| Cohort (revenue/month) | # Centres FY26 | Revenue Share | Centre EBITDA Margin¹ | ROCE² (inverted: negative = value destruction) | Main Risk |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Mature: >₹100 Mn/mo | 4 | 44% | 26% | 27% | Saturation / capacity limits |
| Ramping: ₹50–100 Mn/mo | 14 | 50% | 18% | 9% | Slow progression to >₹100 Mn bucket |
| Emerging: <₹50 Mn/mo | 6 | 6% | 5% | **(22)%** | Cash burn; turnaround uncertain |
| Total (excl. Milann) | 25 | 100% | 19% | 14% | — |

¹ Centre-level EBITDA margin before corporate cost allocation. ² ROCE = EBIT (pre-Ind AS, post corporate cost) / average net capital employed; a NEGATIVE ROCE means the cohort destroys capital — this column is inverted (higher = better, negative = worst). Citation: [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve Profitability and Return on Capital"].

**By revenue type (audited, FY25 consolidated — the only disaggregation in the filing):**
Income from medical services ₹20,563.17 (92.70%); Sale of medical and non-medical items / pharmacy ₹1,445.27 (6.52%); Other operating ₹172.92 (0.78%); Total ₹22,181.36 — [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 22 (Consolidated)].

**By geography (audited, FY25 consolidated):** India ₹21,748.22 (98.0%); Outside India ₹433.14 (2.0%); Total ₹22,181.36 — [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 36 (Consolidated), Geographical information].

---

#### 2. Dominant Segment

**The oncology hospital network is the company — there is no second segment of consequence.** On the audited single-segment basis, Medical and Healthcare Services is 100% of revenue and profit [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 36 (Consolidated)]. Stripping out the only material non-oncology line, the FY26 deck shows total revenue ₹25,454 Mn vs revenue excluding fertility ₹24,850 Mn, implying Milann ≈ ₹604 Mn (~2.4% of revenue); and total Adjusted EBITDA ₹4,711 Mn vs ₹4,642 Mn excluding fertility, implying Milann Adjusted EBITDA ≈ ₹69 Mn (~1.5% of group Adjusted EBITDA) [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Financial Highlights: FY26"]. So oncology contributes roughly 97.6% of revenue and 98.5% of Adjusted EBITDA.

This is a **single-segment business (>85% from one segment)** — I am not manufacturing sub-segments. The relevant internal split that drives value is not a product line but **centre maturity**: 4 mature centres (>₹100 Mn/month) generate 44% of revenue at 27% ROCE, while the 6 emerging centres (6% of revenue) run at **−22% ROCE** [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve Profitability and Return on Capital"]. Where the value sits, and where it leaks, is a maturity story inside one segment — that is the read downstream `unit-economics` and `competitive-map` should carry, not a multi-segment story.

Profit is disclosed only at the consolidated/group level (one segment), so the dominant-segment call rests on revenue and management's "excluding fertility" Adjusted EBITDA bridge, not on audited segment profit — see §3.

---

#### 3. Segment Disclosure Quality

Segment definition is **consistent year-over-year**: both the FY24 and FY25 annual reports state the identical single operating segment, "Medical and Healthcare Services," under Ind AS 108 [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 36 (Consolidated); FY23-24 Annual Report, Note 36 (Consolidated)]. There is **no audited segment-level profit disclosure** — because the company runs as one segment, the filing gives only (a) revenue by type (medical services vs pharmacy vs other) and (b) revenue/non-current assets by India vs Outside India [FY24-25 Annual Report, Notes 22 and 36 (Consolidated)]. There is **no "Other" or unallocated-Corporate bucket** in the segment note (a single segment makes one moot), so the >10% "Other"-bucket concern does not arise.

The material disclosure gap is the opposite of an "Other" bucket: the economically important splits (business line, geographic cluster, centre-maturity cohort, modality mix, payor mix) exist **only in the investor deck**, not in audited segment accounting, and many are presented "excluding fertility" and on a "pre-Ind AS" basis — i.e., not reconciled to the audited statements. Treat cohort ROCE and centre-level EBITDA margins as **management figures (deck), not audited**. A reconciliation note: the FY26 deck reports FY25 total revenue as ₹22,228.5 Mn, which equals the AR's Note 22 figure of ₹22,181.36 Mn **plus** income from government grant ₹47.14 Mn — consistent, just a presentation difference [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 22/24; Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, P&L].

**Segment definition is about to change.** In May 2026 HCG signed the divestment of 100% of BACC Healthcare Private Limited (the Milann fertility brand) at an enterprise value of ₹632 Mn, expected to close in Q1 FY27; the fertility business's assets (₹667.1 Mn) and liabilities (₹344.4 Mn) are classified as held for sale at 31 March 2026, and FY26 carried a ₹319 Mn goodwill impairment on it [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Milann Divestment Update", Consolidated Balance Sheet note (1), and P&L note (1); May 20 2026 earnings call transcript, prepared remarks]. *Inference, not from filings:* post-close, FY27 disclosures should show an even purer oncology business (~97.5%+ → effectively 100% oncology), with International (Kenya) the only non-India, non-oncology-adjacent line remaining. Downstream agents should not expect a new reportable segment to appear; they should expect the existing single segment to get cleaner.

---

## BM-04 · unit-economics — Unit Economics — HCG

### Unit Economics — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787). Figures INR (₹) unless stated. ARPP = Average Revenue Per Patient (per in-patient, IP). Primary per-unit data is from the Q4/FY26 investor deck (May 19 2026) and the May 20 2026 earnings call; FY25 baseline cross-checks from the FY24-25 Integrated Annual Report. The FY26 annual report is not in the data pool, so FY26 per-unit figures are deck/management figures, not audited.*

Upstream segment-map was present and used. Per segment-map, HCG is effectively a **single-segment oncology hospital business** (~93–98% of revenue/EBITDA), and the value-driving split is **centre maturity**, not a product line. That directs the unit choice below.

---

#### 1. Natural Unit

**The natural economic unit for HCG's oncology hospital network is the cancer centre (hospital), measured on a per-month revenue / maturity-cohort basis; the underlying revenue driver beneath the centre is the in-patient treated (priced as ARPP).**

A centre is the unit HCG actually builds, capitalises, and ramps, and the unit at which it discloses EBITDA margin and ROCE. Two finer units sit inside it and are also disclosed: the **bed** (the capacity HCG buys — ~₹45 lakh to build) and the **in-patient (ARPP × volume)** (the revenue HCG earns). I build the table primarily at the **centre** level because that is where value is created or destroyed, and add the bed and per-patient lines because the cost-to-build and revenue-per-unit are only meaningful there. Secondary unit types not built here: the International (Kenya) operation and the Milann fertility unit (being divested) — both immaterial (<3% of revenue each).

---

#### 2. Unit Economics Table

Centre cohorts (FY26, excl. Milann). ROCE column is **inverted in interpretation: a negative value means the cohort destroys capital** (higher = better, negative = worst).

| Unit Economic | Value | Period | Direction vs Prior Year | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue per unit — **per in-patient (ARPP), network** | ₹84,261 | FY26 | **Improving** (+3% YoY; FY25 ₹81,924, excl. fertility) | [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Financial Highlights: FY26" — ARPP excl. fertility 81,924→84,261; "HCG Key Highlights" — FY26 grew on ~12% volume + ~3% ARPP] |
| Volume per network (IP patients) | 294,912 (excl. fertility); ~295k incl. | FY26 | **Improving** (+12% YoY; FY25 264,278 excl. fertility) | [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Financial Highlights: FY26" and "FY26 Operational and Financial Metrics" (295k volume, +12%)] |
| Gross margin per unit | Not disclosed (no per-unit COGS/consumables breakdown in deck or filing) | — | Unknown | [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026; FY24-25 Annual Report — no per-centre or per-patient gross-margin line] |
| Contribution margin per unit — **centre EBITDA margin (pre-corporate allocation), by cohort** | Mature >₹100Mn/mo: **26%** · Ramping ₹50–100Mn/mo: **18%** · Emerging <₹50Mn/mo: **5%** · Total (excl. Milann): **19%** | FY26 | Mixed / not cleanly comparable — group margin improving (+68bps to 18.5%), but cohort-level margins shifted because centres migrated up buckets (composition change), per management | [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve Profitability and Return on Capital"; May 20 2026 call, Mattoo: cohort margin moves driven by "centres moved from third bucket to the second one and second to the first one"] |
| Capital return per unit — **centre ROCE (pre-IndAS, post corporate cost), by cohort** *(inverted: negative = value destruction)* | Mature: **27%** · Ramping: **9%** · Emerging: **(22)%** · Total (excl. Milann): **14%** | FY26 | **Improving** at group level (+87bps; FY25 ~13% → FY26 14%) | [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve Profitability and Return on Capital" and "Financial Highlights: FY26" (ROCE 13%→14%, +87bps); footnote: ROCE = EBIT (pre-IndAS, post corporate cost) / average net capital employed, excl. fertility] |
| Cost to acquire / build the unit — **per bed (brownfield)** | **~₹45 lakh per bed** (~₹90–100 crore for 200 beds) | FY26 guidance (next 24 months) | New disclosure (no prior-year per-bed figure in pool) | [May 20 2026 call, Mattoo: "average cost is about INR45 lakh per bed… INR90-100 crore for 200 beds"] |
| Cost to build the unit — **per centre** | Not disclosed as a single per-centre figure; FY25 network CAPEX ₹2,056 Mn; plan to add ~1,000 beds by FY30 (400 greenfield + 600 brownfield) | FY25 actual / FY30 plan | Unknown (greenfield per-bed cost not separately disclosed) | [FY24-25 Annual Report, Business Model "Manufactured Capital" — CAPEX ₹2,056 Mn; May 20 2026 call, Mattoo: ~1,000 beds by FY30, 400 greenfield + 600 brownfield] |
| Payback period / unit lifetime | **Not disclosed** as a number. Qualitative only: a centre moves through emerging (<₹50Mn/mo, −22% ROCE) → ramping (₹50–100Mn/mo, +9%) → mature (>₹100Mn/mo, +27%); hospital assets are long-lived. Management guides brownfield to "faster ramp-up and better returns" than greenfield, but gives no years-to-breakeven or payback figure. | FY26 | Unknown | [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve…"; May 20 2026 call, Mattoo: near-term emphasis on brownfield for "faster ramp-up and better returns"] |

Supporting capacity / throughput metrics (network level, for context): capacity beds ~2,605 (FY26; ~60 beds reclassified up); FY26 blended **centre utilisation 58%** (South 68% / West 50% / East 57%) — note management states this is a *blended* metric across chemo-bed, OT and radiation-machine use, **not** bed occupancy [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "FY26 Operational and Financial Metrics" and "Hospital Utilization…"; May 20 2026 call, Mattoo: utilisation "factors in… chemo beds utilization, the OT utilization, Radiation machine utilization… a blended metric"]. For comparison, the FY25 Annual Report reported true **in-patient bed occupancy 58%** and **LINAC capacity utilisation 60%** [FY24-25 Annual Report, Business Model "Manufactured Capital" / "Outputs"]. Per-cluster FY26 ARPP: West ₹94,067, South ₹79,889, East ₹65,759 [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, region-wise slides].

---

#### 3. Value Creation Read

**Creates value — but only above the maturity threshold; a new centre destroys capital for years before it does.** The cleanest evidence is the cohort ROCE ladder: a mature centre earns **27% pre-tax ROCE** and a ramping centre **9%**, while an emerging centre runs at **−22% ROCE** — i.e., a freshly built centre destroys capital until it scales [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve Profitability and Return on Capital"]. So the unit creates value *conditional on reaching maturity*, and the whole investment case is whether the 14 ramping centres (50% of revenue, 9% ROCE) climb into the >₹100Mn/27% bucket — which management says is already happening (centres migrated up buckets in FY26, and group ROCE rose 13%→14%) [same deck; May 20 2026 call]. At the network level the math is positive and improving: 14% blended ROCE on a 19% centre-EBITDA-margin base, with ARPP +3% and volume +12%.

The single most valuable missing disclosure is a **per-centre payback / years-to-mature curve** (or a vintage/cohort-by-opening-year table). With cost-to-build (~₹45 lakh/bed brownfield) disclosed but no time-to-breakeven and no contribution margin × lifetime, the formal "contribution × lifetime > acquisition cost" test cannot be closed from disclosure — the read rests on the cohort ROCE snapshot plus management's qualitative ramp claim, not on a computed payback.

---

#### 4. Sensitivity

The input that would most change the read is **utilisation / ramp speed of the ₹50–100Mn cohort** — equivalently, the time a new centre spends in the value-destroying (<₹50Mn, −22% ROCE) phase. A 20% move in utilisation flows almost directly to contribution margin because the cost base of a built-out hospital is largely fixed (beds, LINACs, clinicians, and — post-IndAS — rent); the cohort table itself shows the leverage, with EBITDA margin jumping from 5% (emerging) to 18% (ramping) to 26% (mature) as throughput rises, far outpacing the ~3% annual ARPP move. ARPP is the more *stable* lever (only +2–3% YoY, and East's ARPP actually fell ~1% to ₹65,759 on Kolkata case-mix), so a 20% ARPP swing is historically unlikely; a 20% utilisation swing is both more achievable (West sits at 50% vs South's 68%, on the company's own blended metric) and far more consequential. Acquisition cost per bed (~₹45 lakh) is the least volatile input. Net: ramp/utilisation of the ramping cohort is the swing factor that decides whether the unit math compounds or stalls.

---

## BM-05 · customer-geography — Customer And Geography Map — HCG

### Customer And Geography Map — HCG

HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787) — India-headquartered cancer-care hospital network (comprehensive cancer centres, 3 multispecialty hospitals, and the "Milann" fertility chain). "Customers" here are patients; "payers" are who actually settles the bill. Authoritative figures are from the FY24-25 Integrated Annual Report (audited, year ended 31 Mar 2025); the most recent period (FY26, year ended 31 Mar 2026) is covered only by the investor deck and earnings transcript dated May 2026 (the FY26 audited annual report is not yet in the data pool).

#### 1. Customer Map

HCG does not have named B2B customers. Revenue comes from a widespread base of individual patients, classified by who pays. The audited financial statements state plainly that no single customer is material. The Capital IQ "Customers" export in the pool contains no named customer data, consistent with a patient-funded hospital model.

| Customer Type | Importance (% of revenue if disclosed) | Long-term Contract? (Y/N/Not disclosed) | Evidence | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cash + TPA (self-pay patients + private insurance settled via Third-Party Administrators) | ~56% of revenue (FY26) | N — walk-in / referral model; uninsured patients settle in cash or by credit card on discharge date | `FY26 Investor Deck (Q4/FY26 results), p.6 (Payor mix, FY26)`; `FY24-25 Annual Report, Consolidated Note 40 (Credit risk), p.367` | Discretionary, price-sensitive demand; cash patients exposed to household affordability and insurance penetration |
| Government health schemes (e.g. Ayushman Bharat / state / CGHS-type scheme business) | ~33% of revenue (FY26) | Not disclosed (scheme-empanelment based, rate-card driven; no fixed-volume contract disclosed) | `FY26 Investor Deck, p.6 (Payor mix, FY26)`; strategy text cites "corporate, government or private insurance" payer options, `FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.71` | Scheme tariff/rate revisions and payment-cycle delays sit outside HCG's control; a third of revenue rides on government scheme economics |
| Medical Value Travel (MVT) — international medical-tourism patients | ~2% of revenue (FY26) | N | `FY26 Investor Deck, p.6 (Payor mix, FY26)`; FY26 transcript notes "softer medical value tourism" partly offset South cluster, `Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks` | Small; sensitive to visa/geopolitics and forex |
| Others | ~9% of revenue (FY26) | Not disclosed | `FY26 Investor Deck, p.6 (Payor mix, FY26)` | Residual; not characterised |
| Any single customer | <10% of revenue (FY25 and FY24) — none individually material | Not applicable | `FY24-25 Annual Report, Consolidated Note 40, p.367` and `Standalone Note 40, p.284`: "No single customer accounted for more than 10% of the revenue as of 31 March 2025 and 31 March 2024. There is no significant concentration of credit risk." | None at the single-customer level |

Note: Payor-mix percentages are FY26 figures from the investor deck (a management/Level-3 source). HCG does not publish a payer-mix split in the audited FY25 financial statements; the audited statements confirm only that no single customer exceeds 10% and that the base is "widespread." The ~56% + ~33% + ~2% + ~9% figures sum to ~100% as disclosed.

#### 2. Geography Map

Two views are disclosed. (a) The audited financial statements (Note 36) split revenue only by country of domicile: India vs Outside India. (b) The investor section of the same annual report provides a finer FY25 state-level / metro split. Both are shown; the audited country split governs.

**(a) Audited country split — Note 36, FY25 (₹ million):** India ₹21,748.22 / Outside India ₹433.14 / Total ₹22,181.36.

| Geography | % of Revenue | Trend (Growing / Stable / Declining / Unknown) | Evidence | Risk |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| India (country of domicile) | 98.0% (FY25); 99.1% (FY24) | Stable (slight decline as international scales off a tiny base) | `FY24-25 Annual Report, Consolidated Note 36 (Segment information — Geographical), p.358` | Single-country exposure: India healthcare regulation, scheme tariffs, pricing controls, FX on USD/EUR borrowings |
| Outside India (single international centre, Kenya) | 2.0% (FY25); 0.9% (FY24) | Growing fast off a small base — international revenue +~71% YoY in FY26 | `FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 36, p.358`; `FY26 Investor Deck, p.6`; "22 comprehensive cancer centres (including our international centre in Kenya)", `FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.71` | Single overseas country (Kenya); small enough to be immaterial today |

**(b) FY25 state-level revenue split (investor section, Manufactured Capital, printed p.47):** Karnataka 30%; Gujarat and Rajasthan 28%; Andhra Pradesh and East 17%; Maharashtra 14%; Others 11% (footnote: "Includes Kenya"). Metro cities 52% / Non-metro cities 48%.

| Geography (FY25 state grouping) | % of Revenue | Trend | Evidence | Risk |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| Karnataka (South; Bengaluru hub / Centre of Excellence) | 30% | Stable / growing (South cluster +~13% YoY FY26) | `FY24-25 Annual Report, p.47 (City-wise revenue break-up)`; `FY26 Investor Deck, p.6` | Largest single state; home market and flagship hub |
| Gujarat and Rajasthan (West) | 28% | Growing (West +~14% YoY FY26, led by Ahmedabad) | `FY24-25 Annual Report, p.47`; `FY26 Investor Deck, p.6`; `Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks` | West is the current growth driver |
| Andhra Pradesh and East | 17% | Growing (East +~11% YoY FY26; MGCHRI Vizag 51% acquisition, 196 beds) | `FY24-25 Annual Report, p.47`; MGCHRI detail `MD&A, p.72`; `FY26 Investor Deck, p.6` | Newer cluster; Kolkata ARPP noted as soft in Q4 FY26 |
| Maharashtra (West) | 14% | Stable/growing | `FY24-25 Annual Report, p.47` | — |
| Others (incl. Kenya, plus smaller centres) | 11% | Growing (international portion +~71% YoY FY26) | `FY24-25 Annual Report, p.47 (footnote: Includes Kenya)` | Residual + the only overseas exposure |

State-level shares sum to 100% (30+28+17+14+11). The state split (b) is a management/investor-section view (Level 3 within an audited document); the country split (a) is the audited segment note (Level 5) and is the binding disclosure. The two are consistent: no single Indian state exceeds ~30%, but the country of domicile (India) is ~98%.

#### 3. Concentration Flags

Inverted lens: "Triggered = Y" means more concentration / more risk (worse).

| Concentration Flag | Triggered (Y/N) | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| One customer >20% of revenue | N | Audited: "No single customer accounted for more than 10% of the revenue as of 31 March 2025 and 31 March 2024." `FY24-25 Annual Report, Consolidated Note 40, p.367` and `Standalone Note 40, p.284` |
| Top 3 customers >40% of revenue | N | No single customer >10% (audited), so the top 3 cannot reach 40%. `FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 40, p.367`. The customer base is described as "widespread." |
| One geography >50% of revenue | Y (at country level) / N (at state level) | India = ~98% of revenue at the audited country-of-domicile level (`Note 36, p.358`) — clears 50% decisively. At the finer state level, the largest single state is Karnataka at 30%, below 50% (`p.47`). The audited country split governs, so this flag is triggered. |
| One customer or geography >30% with no long-term contract disclosed | Y | Two paths trigger this: (1) India is ~98% of revenue (`Note 36, p.358`) with no offsetting long-term contract — revenue is per-episode patient care, not contracted. (2) Government health schemes are ~33% of FY26 revenue (`FY26 Investor Deck, p.6`) on scheme-empanelment terms with no disclosed fixed-volume/long-term contract. |

#### 4. Read

HCG is concentrated on geography, not on customers. On the customer side it is the opposite of concentrated — no single customer exceeds 10% of revenue (audited, both consolidated and standalone, FY25 and FY24), because revenue is millions of individual cancer/fertility patients. The real concentration is geographic: ~98% of revenue is earned in one country (India), and within India four state groupings — Karnataka 30%, Gujarat+Rajasthan 28%, AP+East 17%, Maharashtra 14% — account for ~89% of FY25 revenue, with the only overseas presence a single Kenya centre at roughly 2%. This concentration is naked, not contractually secured: hospital revenue is per-episode and walk-in/referral driven, with uninsured patients settling in cash on discharge and no long-term volume contracts disclosed. The single biggest dependency the synthesizer should carry forward is HCG's near-total reliance on the Indian market, sharpened by a ~33% revenue dependence on government health-scheme payers (FY26) whose tariffs and payment cycles HCG does not control.

---

## BM-06 · value-chain — Value Chain Position — HCG

### Value Chain Position — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Reporting currency INR, FY ends 31 March. Evidence base: FY24-25 Integrated Annual Report (filed Sep 2025); Q4/FY26 investor presentation (May 19, 2026); Q4/FY26 earnings call (May 20, 2026); Q3/FY26 earnings call (Feb 9, 2026); Capital IQ Suppliers / Customers relationship files. Upstream input: 02_business-identity.md (read).*

HCG is a single-specialty (oncology) hospital operator. In value-chain terms it is a **service provider** that sits between two groups it does not control: upstream input providers (drug/consumable distributors, imported-equipment makers, and — most importantly — the oncologists who bring patients in) and downstream payors (individual cancer patients, insurers/TPAs, corporates, and government health schemes). Because HCG runs an "asset-light" model — leased real estate, pay-per-use radiation/imaging equipment, and a largely visiting (fee-for-service) clinician roster — it has deliberately pushed two of its most important inputs off its own balance sheet and into supplier hands [FY24-25 Annual Report, "Asset-light model", p.46-47; "Total employees" panel, p.~28].

#### 1. Stages Occupied

| Value Chain Stage | Company Role (1 sentence) | Bargaining Power vs Upstream | Bargaining Power vs Downstream | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Service provider — comprehensive cancer treatment | Owns/operates 22 comprehensive cancer centres + 4 multispecialty hospitals (~2,500 beds), delivering radiation, medical and surgical oncology under one roof. | **Mid** — buys from a 15,000+ supplier base (no single-vendor lock disclosed), but drugs/consumables are a rising 25.7% of revenue and key equipment is imported. | **Mid** — sets list prices to cash/insurance patients (~65% of mix) but is a price-taker to government schemes (~33%) and faces "pricing sensitivity around cancer care." | [FY24-25 AR, "Geographic presence", p.14-15; "Cost of consumption", p.169; Q4/FY26 deck, payor-mix slide; Q3/FY26 call, Feb 9 2026, Q&A] |
| Clinician/specialist platform (the demand engine) | Aggregates 400+ oncologists (114 resident, 3,388 visiting) who are paid largely via fee-for-service "medical consultancy charges" and performance-linked incentives. | **Weak** — clinicians are the largest single cost (medical consultancy charges ₹4,816.7m in FY25) and HCG calls itself "highly dependent on key clinicians"; volume growth is explicitly attributed to "new clinician additions." | n/a (clinicians are an input, not a customer) | [FY24-25 AR, "Total employees" panel, p.~28; Note 30 Other expenses, p.~228; Risk factor (e), p.~150; Q4/FY26 call, prepared remarks] |
| In-house diagnostics (Triesta) | Runs 1 reference lab + 12 diagnostic labs selling pathology/genomic tests to HCG and third-party hospitals. | **Mid** — buys reagents/sequencing consumables from specialist vendors; not separately quantified. | **Mid** — third-party hospital test pricing is negotiated; not separately disclosed. | [FY24-25 AR, MD&A "Precision Diagnostics", p.166] |
| Real estate / equipment user (asset-light) | Operates hospitals "mostly on a lease basis" and uses radiation/imaging equipment on a "pay per use basis." | **Mid** — converts fixed capex into variable rent/usage fees (rent ₹269.4m, pay-per-use radiation inside Other expenses), trading ownership for flexibility; lessors/equipment owners take the residual-value risk. | n/a | [FY24-25 AR, "Asset-light model", p.46-47; Note 30 Other expenses, p.~228; MD&A "Other expenses", p.169] |

Bargaining power bands:
- **Strong:** Company sets price, dictates terms, has alternatives
- **Mid:** Negotiated outcomes, no extreme leverage either way
- **Weak:** Price-taker, terms imposed, few alternatives

Note on data: the Capital IQ "Suppliers" and "Customers" files in the pool list only HCG's own subsidiaries/LLPs (e.g., HCG Eko Oncology LLP, Apex HCG Oncology Hospitals LLP) as both "supplier" and "customer." There is **no external supplier or customer concentration disclosed** in those vendor files — consistent with a business whose customers are tens of thousands of individual patients and whose inputs are spread across a 15,000+ supplier base [Capital IQ Suppliers file; Capital IQ Customers file; FY24-25 AR, "Total supplier base: 15000+", p.29/63].

#### 2. Input Cost Pass-Through

HCG does **not** demonstrate clean, contractual pass-through of input costs; the filings show input costs rising faster than HCG offsets them. Cost of consumption (drugs and medical/non-medical consumables) rose to ₹5,805.99m in FY25 from ₹4,754.17m in FY24, lifting its share of total revenue to 25.72% from 24.64% — a ~108 bps drag, meaning consumable inflation outran realization on a like-for-like basis [FY24-25 AR, "Cost of consumption", p.169]. Two structural constraints sit on the upstream side: (1) HCG dispenses drugs that fall under India's Drugs (Prices Control) Order, 2013 (DPCO), so the government caps prices on scheduled essential cancer drugs — HCG cannot freely re-price the regulated portion of its pharmacy [FY24-25 AR, regulatory-compliance schedules, p.~178 and p.~250]; and (2) a large share of cost is medical consultancy charges (₹4,816.7m in FY25, the single biggest line in Other expenses) plus pay-per-use radiation-equipment fees, both of which rise with clinician hiring and volume rather than being passed through to patients [FY24-25 AR, Note 30, p.~228; MD&A "Other expenses", p.169]. The company's own framing is that realization grows roughly with "medical inflation" of about 5% plus mix, not via an input-indexed escalator: when asked to bridge 15% revenue growth, management split it into ~10% volume and ~5% ARPP, with ARPP improvement coming from replacing low-margin work with higher-margin work over time [Q3/FY26 call, Feb 9 2026, Q&A; Q4/FY26 call, May 20 2026, Q&A]. No contractual cost-escalator or indexed-pricing clause with patients or payors is disclosed in the pool.

#### 3. Customer Pricing Power

HCG has partial, mix-dependent pricing power, not broad pricing power. On the cash/insurance/corporate side (~65% of oncology revenue, or ~56% excluding corporates per the business-identity read) it sets list prices and has lifted average revenue per patient (ARPP) — ARPP was ~₹84,000 in Q3 FY26, up ~5% year on year, and FY26 saw a 3% improvement in average revenue per patient — but management is explicit that this comes from case mix (more high-acuity surgical/radiation work) and payor mix, not from raising sticker prices, and it cites "pricing sensitivity around cancer care" as a reason it guides only ~5% ARPP growth [Q3/FY26 call, Feb 9 2026, prepared remarks + Q&A; Q4/FY26 call, May 20 2026, prepared remarks]. On the government-scheme side (~33% of revenue), HCG is a price-taker: scheme tariffs are set by the payor, and a transition in the Odisha state government scheme cut one cluster's ARPP by 3% year on year in Q3 FY26 — direct evidence that HCG absorbs scheme repricing rather than dictating it [Q3/FY26 call, Feb 9 2026, prepared remarks]. The clearest signal on pricing-power limits is behavioural: across the last 24 months HCG chose to *pare down low-margin business* and accept slower revenue growth rather than push price to defend volume, telling analysts the low-margin-to-high-margin transition would "normalize over the next couple of quarters" — a company with strong pricing power would raise price, not exit the work [Q4/FY26 call, May 20 2026, prepared remarks + Q&A]. There is no disclosed instance in the pool of HCG taking a broad list-price increase and holding volume; the volume reaction it does cite (paring low-margin cases) is a volume *sacrifice* it chose, which means pricing power on the patient side is real but bounded.

#### 4. Economic Control Verdict

**Mixed.**

HCG sets prices to the majority cash/insurance/corporate payor base (~65% of oncology revenue) and has many alternatives on the supplier side (15,000+ suppliers, no disclosed single-vendor dependence), which gives it real economic control on parts of both ends [Q4/FY26 deck, payor-mix slide; FY24-25 AR, "Total supplier base: 15000+", p.29]. But it is squeezed on the two relationships that matter most for its economics: roughly a third of revenue is government-scheme work where the payor sets the tariff (Odisha scheme repricing cut a cluster's ARPP 3%), and its single largest cost is the oncologists themselves, whom the company concedes it is "highly dependent on" and pays largely on a fee-for-service basis — so the people who generate the revenue capture a large, growing slice of it (medical consultancy charges ₹4,816.7m in FY25) [Q3/FY26 call, Feb 9 2026; FY24-25 AR, Risk factor (e), p.~150; Note 30, p.~228]. Rising consumable costs (25.7% of revenue, up ~108 bps) and DPCO drug-price caps further limit upstream control. Strong on one side, weak on the other — this is a mixed position, not full control.

#### 5. The Single Biggest Bargaining Risk

The clinician relationship: if HCG's high-volume oncologists (the 3,388 visiting/400+ specialist roster it depends on for patient flow) negotiated a larger share of economics or left for a competitor, both revenue and the largest cost line would move against HCG at once — it is the one value-chain link the company itself flags as a dependency [FY24-25 AR, Risk factor (e) "highly dependent on... key clinicians", p.~150; Note 30 medical consultancy charges ₹4,816.7m, p.~228; Q4/FY26 call, May 20 2026, growth attributed to "new clinician additions"].

---

## BM-07 · business-quality — Business Quality — HCG

### Business Quality — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). All figures INR million (₹ Mn) unless stated. Audited base is the FY24-25 Integrated Annual Report (year ended 31 Mar 2025). FY26 (year ended 31 Mar 2026) figures are from the Q4/FY26 investor deck (May 2026) and the May 20 2026 earnings call; the FY26 audited annual report is not in the data pool. Six-year financial history (FY21–FY26) is from the Capital IQ Financials export. Upstream segment-map and customer-geography outputs both present — no degraded-confidence flag needed.*

Direction note: rows are marked higher = better, except the five reverse-mapped factors where **low intensity / dependence / cyclicality earns a HIGH score** (capital intensity, competitive intensity, regulatory dependence, commodity dependence, cyclicality). Bands per `CLAUDE.md`: 0–20 very weak, 21–40 weak, 41–60 mixed/average, 61–80 strong, 81–100 very strong.

---

#### 1. Quality Factor Table

| Quality Factor | Score /100 | Evidence | Comment |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Pricing power *(higher = better)* | 48 | Mgmt guides ~5% medical-inflation pricing inside a 15% revenue target; West ARPP +~3% YoY, East/Kolkata ARPP fell on case-mix [Q4/FY26 transcript, Q&A (Devang Patel) and prepared remarks; Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, region-wise slides]. ~33% of FY26 revenue is government-scheme business on rate cards HCG does not set [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, p.6 payor mix]. Risk factor: "government policies which may affect the pricing of our medical services" [FY24-25 Annual Report, Risk Factors, p.~315 of text/§ "results of operations"]. | Real but limited. Net pricing is roughly medical inflation (~5%) plus mix/up-coding; the rest of ARPP gains come from swapping low-margin for high-margin work, not from raising prices on a fixed basket. A third of revenue sits on tariffs set by others. Mixed/average. |
| Repeat / recurring revenue *(higher = better)* | 52 | Oncology care is multi-month: medical-oncology (chemo cycles) is the single largest modality at 38% of FY26 revenue, plus surgical 21%, OP-onco 16%, radiation 14% [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "FY26 Revenue Mix: By Specialty and Payor"]. No subscription, contract, or annuity revenue; income is "income from medical services" per episode, 92.70% of FY25 revenue [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 22 (Consolidated)]. | A single cancer patient generates a multi-cycle revenue stream over months to years (diagnosis → surgery/chemo/radiation → surveillance), which is repeat-by-treatment-course. But it is not recurring in the contractual sense, and a cured/lost patient does not renew. Episodic, treatment-anchored. Mixed. |
| Customer stickiness *(higher = better)* | 56 | Treatment is consultant-led and continuity-critical; the deck attributes Kolkata ARPP softness to "clinical transitions" / clinician movement — i.e., patients follow clinicians and case-mix moves with them [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, East slide; Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks]. 20 NABH-accredited centres + Bengaluru Centre of Excellence anchor referral networks [FY24-25 Annual Report, p.~2155 (NABH accredited: 20) and COE references]. No single customer >10% of revenue (audited) [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 40 (Consolidated), p.367]. | Once treatment starts, switching mid-course is clinically costly, so stickiness within an episode is high; the network/COE referral model and accreditation create pull. But stickiness is to the treating clinician as much as to HCG — clinician exits move patients (Kolkata) — and there is no contractual lock-in. Mixed, tilting up. |
| Margin stability *(higher = better)* | 67 | Total-revenue EBITDA margin: FY22 12.8%, FY23 14.0%, FY24 14.0%, FY25 14.1%, FY26 18.3% reported / 18.5% adjusted [Capital IQ Financials export, Income Statement, FY21–FY26; Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, P&L]. FY26 adjusted EBITDA margin rose only ~70bps YoY on a comparable basis — "operating leverage, better utilization, throughput optimization" — not a step-change [Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks]. FY21 was the COVID exception (6.5% EBITDA margin, ₹1,935 Mn net loss). | EBITDA margin held in a tight ~13–14% band for four straight years, then improved gradually — high operating-margin stability for a hospital roll-out. The headline 14%→18% gap is mostly a definitional/Ind-AS-and-adjustment difference (incl. a ₹566 Mn SBC reversal in FY26), not volatility. Note: PAT margin is thin and noisy (0.5%–3.8%) because D&A and ₹1,766 Mn interest sit below EBITDA — margin stability is at the operating line, not the bottom line. Strong at EBITDA. |
| Capital intensity *(low intensity = high score)* | 28 | Capex/sales climbed 3.5% (FY21) → 7.9% → 9.7% → 9.4% → 11.5% (FY26); capex exceeded D&A from FY24 (1.10x, 1.02x, 1.20x) [Capital IQ Financials export, Cash Flow, FY21–FY26]. PP&E ₹13,928 Mn + right-of-use ₹6,484 Mn vs ₹25,454 Mn revenue [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, Consolidated Balance Sheet]. CFO−capex FCF margin fell to 2.2% in FY26; group ROCE ~5% (Capital IQ basis) / 14% (deck pre-Ind AS basis) [Capital IQ Financials export, Ratios; Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve Profitability and Return on Capital"]. Plan to add ~200 beds over two years [Q4/FY26 transcript, Q&A]. | A new cancer centre needs land/lease, buildings, linacs, PET-CT and imported equipment before it earns; the 6 emerging centres run at −22% ROCE while they ramp [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, cohort table]. Heavy, front-loaded, and still expanding. Reverse-mapped: high intensity → low score. Weak. |
| Competitive intensity *(low intensity = high score)* | 42 | HCG's own risk factor: "We face intense competition from other healthcare facilities. If we are unable to compete effectively, our business … may be materially and adversely affected" [FY24-25 Annual Report, Risk Factors, p.~315 of text, item (d)]. Multispecialty chains, standalone oncology centres, and government hospitals all compete; medical-value-travel softness shows demand is contestable [Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks]. | HCG is among India's larger dedicated oncology networks (24 India centres) and high-end radiation/diagnostics raise the entry bar, which lifts this above the weak band — but it states intense competition itself, and rivals are well-funded. Reverse-mapped: moderate-to-high intensity → low-mixed score. Mixed/average, lower half. (Moat itself is the `moat` agent's call.) |
| Regulatory dependence *(low dependence = high score)* | 40 | Operates under the Atomic Energy Act 1962 & Radiation Protection Rules 2004 (AERB licenses/audits radiation oncology), the Clinical Establishments (Registration) Acts incl. state versions, and NABH/NABL accreditation [FY24-25 Annual Report, Secretarial Audit, p.~5516 and p.~7102; quality section p.~11640–11700]. ~33% of FY26 revenue is government-scheme (Ayushman Bharat / state / CGHS-type), exposed to tariff revisions and payment-cycle delays [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, p.6; FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A]. Pricing of medical services explicitly subject to "government policies" [FY24-25 Annual Report, Risk Factors]. | Not a price-controlled utility, but heavily licensed (radiation, clinical-establishment, drug/consumables empanelment) and one-third dependent on government payers whose rate cards and payment timing HCG does not control. Reverse-mapped: meaningful dependence → low score. Weak. |
| Commodity dependence *(low dependence = high score)* | 70 | Filings acknowledge "commodity price risk" only generically and route it to the financial-risk note [FY24-25 Annual Report, BRSR p.~8239 and p.~9468–9470]. Main variable inputs are drugs and consumables (consumable items rose ₹1,051.82 Mn YoY) [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.~11138] and largely pass through to the bill; main capital inputs are imported linacs/PET-CT/MRI (an FX/import exposure, not a commodity feedstock) [FY24-25 Annual Report, p.~6913–7010]. | No crude/metal/energy feedstock drives the cost line; the cost base is clinical labour + consumables + equipment depreciation. Commodity sensitivity is low; the real input exposure is skilled-clinician cost and imported-equipment FX. Reverse-mapped: low dependence → high score. Strong. |
| Cyclicality *(low cyclicality = high score)* | 72 | Cancer demand is structurally rising and largely non-deferrable: India incidence projected to ~1.77 million new cases by 2030, "there is no room" [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.~1266; incidence section p.~9907–9955, p.~10026–10163]. Revenue compounded ~20% p.a. FY21→FY26 (₹10,134 Mn → ₹25,454 Mn) straight through the post-COVID period [Capital IQ Financials export, Income Statement]. Discretionary/cyclical pockets are small: medical-value-travel ~2% of revenue, hit by Middle-East conflict in Q4 FY26 [Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks]. | Oncology is one of the least economically cyclical healthcare niches — patients do not defer cancer treatment in a downturn. The cyclical edges are MVT (~2%) and the affordability of cash/uninsured patients in a weak economy. FY21 weakness was a COVID-access shock, not a demand cycle. Reverse-mapped: low cyclicality → high score. Strong. |
| Disclosure quality *(higher = better)* | 57 | Audited financials are clean and consistent: same single Ind AS 108 segment ("Medical and Healthcare Services") in FY24 and FY25 [FY24-25 Annual Report, Note 36; FY23-24 Annual Report, Note 36], revenue-by-type and India/Outside-India splits disclosed [Notes 22, 36], held-for-sale fertility assets/liabilities and the ₹319 Mn goodwill impairment disclosed [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026; FY26 to be confirmed in audited AR]. BUT the economically decisive splits — centre-maturity cohorts, cohort ROCE, ARPP, payor mix, cluster utilisation — exist only in the deck, "excl. fertility" and "pre-Ind AS," not reconciled to audited statements [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Path to Improve Profitability and Return on Capital", p.6]. | Statutory disclosure is adequate and consistent; the gap is that the numbers a buyer most needs (where capital is destroyed vs created across 25 centres) are management figures, not audited, and the FY26 audited annual report is not yet in the pool. Treat cohort ROCE/centre EBITDA as Level-3. Mixed, upper half. |

---

#### 2. Aggregate Quality Score

**Aggregate business quality: 52 / 100 (Mixed/Average).**

This is not a strict average of the rows; it is judgment-weighted. Two factors dominate the read in opposite directions and roughly offset: the demand side is high quality — low cyclicality (72) and low commodity dependence (70) on top of a structurally growing, non-deferrable oncology market with stable ~13–14% EBITDA margins (67) — while the capital and return side is weak — heavy and still-rising capital intensity (28) that has pushed group ROCE to ~5% and FCF margin to ~2%, with a third of revenue dependent on government tariffs (regulatory dependence 40). Secondary factors (pricing power 48, stickiness 56, recurring 52, competition 42, disclosure 57) cluster in the mixed band and pull the aggregate toward the middle rather than the extremes. The single fact that most caps the score is that a genuinely defensive, growing demand profile is being monetised through a capital-hungry roll-out that does not yet earn its cost of capital at the group level.

---

#### 3. Strongest Factor & Weakest Factor

| | Factor | Score | Why |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Strongest | Cyclicality *(low = high score)* | 72 | Cancer treatment is non-deferrable and demand is structurally rising (India incidence to ~1.77M by 2030); revenue compounded ~20% p.a. FY21→FY26 through the post-COVID period, with only ~2% (MVT) genuinely cyclical [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A p.~1266 and incidence section; Capital IQ Financials export, Income Statement]. |
| Weakest | Capital intensity *(low = high score)* | 28 | Capex/sales rose to 11.5% and capex now exceeds D&A (1.2x); FCF margin fell to 2.2% and group ROCE is ~5%, with 6 emerging centres at −22% ROCE and ~200 more beds planned [Capital IQ Financials export, Cash Flow & Ratios; Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, cohort table and Q&A]. |

---

#### 4. Read

HCG is a **structurally-growing, capital-intensive, single-line oncology platform with utility-like demand but sub-utility returns** — closest to a long-duration infrastructure build-out than to a durable compounder today. The demand is defensive and non-cyclical and operating EBITDA margins are stable, but the business consumes almost all of its operating cash flow in expansion capex and below-EBITDA finance/D&A charges, so it has not yet converted a good market into good group returns on capital. It is not a commodity or macro bet in disguise; it is a maturity-curve bet inside one segment. The single quality factor a buyer should watch over the next 24 months is **whether capital intensity converts into rising returns** — specifically whether the 14 ramping centres migrate into the >₹100 Mn/month, ~26%-margin / 27%-ROCE bucket and the 6 emerging centres turn ROCE-positive, because that migration (not pricing, which is roughly capped at ~5% medical inflation plus mix) is what would lift this from mixed quality toward genuinely high quality.

---

## BM-08 · competitive-map — Competitive Map — HCG

### Competitive Map — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787). Competitor scale figures are from a Capital IQ comparable-analysis export (USD, as-of 2026-06-01) unless stated; USD figures are restated to INR at the rate implied by HCG's own line in that export (HCG LTM revenue USD 268.7m ≈ FY26 INR 25,454m → ≈ ₹94.7/USD). Upstream inputs (02_business-identity, 03_segment-map) both present.*

---

#### 1. Dominant Segment

**India comprehensive cancer care (oncology hospital network)** — ~97.6% of FY26 revenue and ~98.5% of Adjusted EBITDA; a single-segment business, with value driven by centre maturity rather than a product mix [03_segment-map, §2; Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026, "Financial Highlights: FY26"]. HCG calls itself "the largest provider of cancer care in India" and "India's foremost pure-play oncology powerhouse" [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A p.146 ("Our business / Oncology") and p.20]. Competitors below must compete in **this** segment, not in hospitals generally.

---

#### 2. Named Competitors

A structural note on sourcing: HCG's filings do **not** name corporate competitors in a competition section — they speak generically of "our competitors" and contrast HCG against "multi-speciality hospitals" that "have one department for oncology but lack comprehensive cancer care centres," naming only Tata Memorial as an R&D peer [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A p.147 ("HCG key differentiators"), p.146, p.149 ("Risks and concerns")]. The named set below is therefore built from (i) the Capital IQ comparable-analysis export in the data pool and (ii) dated industry/trade sources, filtered to the oncology segment. There are two distinct rival types: **multi-specialty majors with large oncology departments** and **standalone single-specialty cancer chains** (HCG's own category).

##### Competitor A — Apollo Hospitals (Apollo Cancer Centres / Apollo Proton Cancer Centre)

- **Ticker / listing:** NSE/BSE: APOLLOHOSP (Apollo Hospitals Enterprise Ltd). Apollo Cancer Centres is its oncology vertical; not separately listed.
- **Where they compete:** India comprehensive oncology — the largest dedicated cancer footprint among the multi-specialty majors. 14 dedicated cancer centres, 240+ oncologists, 1,000+ beds, plus a proton-therapy facility (Apollo Proton Cancer Centre, Chennai). Direct catchment overlap with HCG in Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Chennai [Web: apollohospitals.com "Apollo Cancer Centers Overview," 2026-06-01 (indicative, unverified)].
- **Scale:** Apollo Hospitals group revenue is far larger than HCG (group is the largest listed hospital chain in India by revenue; ~74 hospitals, 10,400+ beds), so the oncology vertical alone is plausibly of comparable-or-larger scale than HCG — but Apollo does not disclose a clean stand-alone oncology revenue line [Web: apollohospitals.com, 2026-06-01 (indicative, unverified)]. *Inference, not from filings:* oncology is a minority of Apollo's group revenue, so segment-level scale is not precisely comparable from disclosure.
- **Source named in:** HCG's own filing names Apollo as a prior employer of two HCG executives (CEO Meghraj Gore; SVP-Sales) — an industry reference, not a competition statement [FY24-25 Annual Report, p.37, p.87-88]. Named as a direct HCG competitor in trade/industry profiles [Web: tracxn.com HCG competitor list, 2026 (indicative, unverified)].
- **One-line read:** The deepest-pocketed oncology rival; competes on brand, proton therapy and metro density, but oncology sits inside a multi-specialty group rather than being the whole company as it is for HCG.

##### Competitor B — American Oncology Institute (AOI / Cancer Treatment Services International)

- **Ticker / listing:** Private. Backed by CTSI / Asia Healthcare Holdings; CTSI was acquired by Varian (now Siemens Healthineers) [Web: medtechdive.com "Varian ups presence in India with $283M acquisition"; tracxn.com AOI profile, 2026 (indicative, unverified)].
- **Where they compete:** This is HCG's **closest like-for-like rival** — a standalone, single-specialty comprehensive-oncology chain (medical, radiation, surgical oncology under one roof), the same model as HCG. ~18 oncology hospitals across South Asia, weighted to South and Central India / Tier-2 cities — overlapping HCG's non-metro expansion thesis [Web: americanoncology.com "About," 2026; healthcareradius.in profile (indicative, unverified)].
- **Scale:** Revenue not publicly disclosed (private). Network of ~18 oncology hospitals vs HCG's 22-24 comprehensive cancer centres → comparable in centre count, though HCG is materially larger by revenue (HCG FY26 revenue ₹25,454m) [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, May 2026; Web sources above (indicative, unverified)]. Qualifies as a credible competitor on the "clear segment-specific rival" criterion (same single-specialty model, overlapping geography) even though its revenue is not disclosed.
- **Source named in:** Named as a top HCG competitor in trade/industry profiles [Web: tracxn.com HCG competitor list, 2026 (indicative, unverified)].
- **One-line read:** The only rival running HCG's exact playbook — dedicated cancer chain expanding into Tier-2 cities — and the one most likely to contest HCG's emerging-centre catchments directly.

##### Competitor C — Max Healthcare Institute

- **Ticker / listing:** BSE: 543220 / NSE: MAXHEALTH.
- **Where they compete:** Listed multi-specialty chain in which oncology is a named top specialty; competes with HCG in North India and metros [Capital IQ comparable export, Business Description, 2026-06-01: "specialties, including … oncology"]. Catchment overlap is lower than Apollo/AOI (Max is North-India weighted vs HCG's South/West tilt).
- **Scale:** LTM revenue ≈ USD 884m (≈ ₹83.7bn); LTM EBITDA ≈ USD 237m; EBITDA margin 26.8%; market cap ≈ USD 9,641m — the largest comp in the set, roughly 3.3x HCG's revenue and ~6.5x its EBITDA margin-dollars [Capital IQ comparable export, Financial Data + Operating Statistics, 2026-06-01]. At ~3.3x HCG revenue it sits just outside the ~5x scale band on the small side, i.e., comparable enough to be a credible peer.
- **Source named in:** Capital IQ relevancy-ranked comparable set for HCG; also named in HCG trade-press competitor lists [Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01; Web: tracxn.com, 2026 (indicative, unverified)].
- **One-line read:** A higher-margin, much larger multi-specialty operator whose oncology line competes at the premium/metro end — useful as the downstream margin benchmark, less so as a head-to-head catchment rival.

**Broader listed comp bloc (named, for market-structure context, not profiled):** Fortis Healthcare (LTM rev ≈ ₹91bn), Narayana Hrudayalaya (≈ ₹79bn, runs an oncology line), Aster DM Healthcare (≈ ₹46bn), Global Health/Medanta (≈ ₹44bn, "cardiac and cancer care"), KIMS (≈ ₹39bn). All carry oncology departments but none is a pure-play cancer chain [Capital IQ comparable export, Financial Data + Business Description, 2026-06-01].

---

#### 3. Competitive Position

**Position vs peers: Holding-to-gaining in oncology, but on a proxy, not on disclosed segment share.** No competitor or HCG discloses an audited India-oncology market-share series, so a clean share read is not available. The supporting proxies: HCG states an explicit ambition to reach "45%+ market share" in its home Bengaluru market and describes itself as "a natural industry consolidator," with FY26 group revenue +15% YoY (₹22,228m → ₹25,454m) and emerging centres growing far faster than the network (FY25 emerging-centre growth +32%) [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A p.29; 02_business-identity §2; 03_segment-map §2]. On scale within the *listed* peer set, HCG is the smallest by revenue (≈ ₹25bn vs Max ≈ ₹84bn, Fortis ≈ ₹91bn) but the only pure-play oncology operator, so it is not losing oncology share to those names so much as occupying a different niche [Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01]. Against its true like-for-like rival (AOI, ~18 centres), HCG is larger by revenue and centre count. The honest read: HCG is the share leader in standalone comprehensive cancer care and is adding capacity, but the absence of a disclosed oncology-share dataset caps confidence in any "gaining" claim.

---

#### 4. Competitive Shape

**Fragmented overall, with a small consolidated cluster of dedicated cancer chains.** Indian cancer care is structurally fragmented: HCG's own filing notes comprehensive cancer-care centres are concentrated in metros (≈30% in the top-8 metros, ≈10% in other state capitals, ≈60% in the rest of India), the industry is projected to grow ~12% CAGR, and the sector is "witnessing increased consolidation even among the larger players," with HCG positioning as the consolidator via bolt-on acquisitions [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A p.28, p.146, p.149, p.29]. No single player approaches majority national share, and oncology demand sits across thousands of multi-specialty hospitals plus government institutions (Tata Memorial, regional cancer centres). The narrower segment that actually matters — **organised, multi-modality dedicated cancer chains** — is far more concentrated: effectively HCG (leader, 22-24 centres), Apollo Cancer Centres (14 centres), and AOI (~18 centres), i.e., a 3-player lead group, with the listed multi-specialty majors (Max, Fortis, Narayana, Aster, Medanta) contesting the premium/metro slice [FY24-25 Annual Report p.146-147; Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01; Web sources, 2026-06-01 (indicative, unverified)]. HHI / hard top-N share data: not disclosed in the data pool.

---

#### 5. Caveat

Three limitations to flag for downstream `moat`:
1. **No company-named competitor list.** HCG's filings name no corporate competitor in a competition section (only Tata Memorial as an R&D peer). The named set rests on a Capital IQ comparable export (Tier-5 vendor data) plus dated, unverified web/trade sources — not on the company's own disclosure.
2. **Segment-level scale is partly unavailable.** The closest like-for-like rival (AOI) is private and does not disclose revenue; the largest oncology rival (Apollo) does not break out a stand-alone oncology revenue line. So head-to-head oncology economics cannot be fully sized from disclosure — the listed comp set (Max, Fortis, Narayana, Aster, Medanta, KIMS) is group-level multi-specialty, only partially oncology.
3. **No disclosed market-share dataset.** The position read in §3 is proxy-based (HCG's stated Bengaluru ambition, revenue growth, centre count), not a measured share series. What would resolve it: an audited or third-party India-oncology revenue/share dataset (e.g., a Capital IQ/industry oncology-specific market-size report), or segment-level oncology revenue from Apollo and a revenue figure for AOI.

---

## BM-09 · moat — Moat — HCG

### Moat — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787). Single-segment India comprehensive cancer-care (oncology hospital) operator. Named competitors inherited from `08_competitive-map.md` (present). Competitor economics from a Capital IQ comparable-analysis export (USD, as-of 2026-06-01, S&P relevancy-ranked); HCG's own multi-year returns from a Capital IQ Financials export (INR, FY21–FY26). USD→INR uses HCG's own export line (LTM rev USD 268.7m ≈ FY26 INR 25,454m → ≈ ₹94.7/USD). All scores 0–100, higher = better, per `CLAUDE.md` §12 / MODULE_RULES.*

---

#### 1. Named Competitors

(Inherited verbatim from `08_competitive-map.md` §2 — no new competitors introduced.)

- **Apollo Hospitals (Apollo Cancer Centres / Apollo Proton)** — NSE/BSE: APOLLOHOSP. Deepest-pocketed oncology rival; 14 dedicated cancer centres + proton therapy; oncology sits inside a far-larger multi-specialty group, no clean stand-alone oncology revenue line [08_competitive-map §2].
- **American Oncology Institute (AOI / CTSI)** — Private; backed by CTSI/Asia Healthcare Holdings (CTSI acquired by Varian/Siemens Healthineers). HCG's closest like-for-like rival: standalone single-specialty cancer chain, ~18 hospitals, Tier-2 weighted [08_competitive-map §2].
- **Max Healthcare Institute** — BSE: 543220 / NSE: MAXHEALTH. Larger, higher-margin multi-specialty operator with a named oncology line; North-India weighted; the margin/scale benchmark in the listed set [08_competitive-map §2].
- **Broader listed bloc (context only, not head-to-head catchment rivals):** Fortis, Narayana Hrudayalaya, Aster DM, Global Health/Medanta, KIMS — all carry oncology departments; none is a pure-play cancer chain [08_competitive-map §2].

---

#### 2. Moat Sources

A moat = something making it hard for the competitors above to take HCG's profits. Scored against those named rivals, not an abstract industry.

| Possible Moat | Present? (Y/N) | Evidence | Strength /100 |
|---|---|---|---:|
| Brand | Partial (Y) | HCG markets itself as "the largest provider of cancer care in India" under the "HCG" brand and targets "45%+ market share" in home Bengaluru; claims "leadership positions in 16 out of 19 cities" [FY24-25 AR, p.163 (printed), p.22 (printed)]. But brand has NOT converted to pricing power: FY26 EBIT margin 8.7% sits at the bottom of the listed peer set (median 16.8%) [Capital IQ comparable export, Operating Statistics, 2026-06-01]. Brand recognition without margin/return premium = weak economic moat. | 38 |
| Cost advantage | N | HCG claims a "competitive cost structure" via a centralised drug/consumables formulary and scale [FY24-25 AR, p.163 (printed)]. This is a parity claim, not a cost edge: HCG's EBIT margin (8.7%) and net margin (0.5%) are the lowest in the 11-company comp set (medians 16.8% / 12.0%) [Capital IQ comparable export, Operating Statistics, 2026-06-01]. No evidence of a structural cost advantage over the named rivals. | 20 |
