| Distribution | Partial (Y) | 22 comprehensive cancer centres across ten Indian states + 1 in Kenya, plus a hub-and-spoke day-care / referral model and "largest tumour board in India" feeding referrals [FY24-25 AR, p.165, p.164, p.147 (printed)]. Wider dedicated-oncology footprint than AOI (~18) and Apollo Cancer Centres (14). Real, but it is a footprint, not an exclusive channel — rivals are building the same Tier-2 day-care networks. | 45 |
| Scale | Partial (Y) | Largest *dedicated* oncology chain by centre count/revenue and "highest number of oncologists (400+)" [FY24-25 AR, p.164 (printed); 08_competitive-map §3]. But against the listed peer set HCG is the *smallest* operator by revenue (LTM USD 268.7m vs Max USD 883.8m, Fortis USD 963.5m) and its scale has not produced peer-level margins or returns [Capital IQ comparable export, Financial Data + Operating Statistics, 2026-06-01]. Scale leadership is real only within the narrow pure-play niche; it does not yet defend profits. | 40 |
| Technology / IP | Partial (Y) | Documented "first-in-India" deployments: 128-slice PET-CT, high-intensity flattening-filter-free radiotherapy, robotic radiosurgery, computer-assisted tumour-navigation surgery; CyberKnife, TomoTherapy, MR-LINAC, Da Vinci across the network; Triesta Sciences genomics lab (NABL + CAP accredited) and ~970 published research papers [FY24-25 AR, p.163, p.164, p.72, p.96 (printed)]. Genuine clinical/technical depth, but the equipment is third-party (Varian/Siemens, Intuitive, Accuray) and purchasable by Apollo/Max; no proprietary patents disclosed in the data pool. Defensible as accumulated know-how, not as exclusive IP. | 48 |
| Licenses / regulation | N | Hospitals need NABH/NABL accreditation and standard medical licensing, which HCG holds [FY24-25 AR, p.159, p.171 (printed)] — but these are table-stakes available to every rival, not a scarce licence that excludes competitors. Regulation cuts the *other* way: HCG flags government "price control or capping of margins on medicines" and third-party-payer disallowances as profit risks [FY24-25 AR, p.166 (printed)]. No licensing moat. | 22 |
| Network effects | N | Not proven from available data. There is a referral/tumour-board flywheel (more oncologists → more complex cases → more referrals → talent attraction) described qualitatively [FY24-25 AR, p.72 ("India's strongest community of Oncology experts"), p.147 (printed)], but no evidence it produces increasing returns that lock out AOI/Apollo. Closer to a talent-cluster economy than a true network effect. | 28 |
| Switching costs | Partial (Y) | Cancer is a chronic, multi-year, multi-modality journey with long-term follow-up, so an in-treatment patient is sticky to their centre/oncologist [FY24-25 AR, p.21, p.71 (printed)]. But the lock-in is patient-level and episode-bounded, not contractual or recurring revenue; new patients choose freely, and HCG itself names patient acquisition / brand-building as a continuous spend [FY24-25 AR, p.72 (printed)]. Modest, real, but not a profit-defending contract base. | 35 |
| Natural resource access | N | Not applicable to an oncology hospital operator; no evidence in data pool. | 0 |
| Location advantage | Partial (Y) | The Bengaluru KR Road "Centre of Excellence" is a genuinely entrenched flagship (operating since 1989) and HCG targets 45%+ local share [FY24-25 AR, p.165, p.22 (printed)]. But it is only "about 3% of our business" [FY24-25 AR, p.71 (printed)], and the broader network is in contested metros with direct Apollo/Max/AOI overlap [08_competitive-map §1-2]. Location strength is concentrated in one small-revenue node, not network-wide. | 32 |

Summary: several *partial* sources, none rising to a strong, profit-defending moat on its own. The recurring failure: every claimed advantage (brand, scale, technology, distribution) co-exists with bottom-of-peer margins and below-cost-of-capital returns, so the advantages are not converting into defended economics.

---

#### 3. Competitive Economics

Margins are LTM from the Capital IQ comparable export (as-of 2026-06-01), which corresponds to HCG's FY26 (Mar-2026). **ROIC note:** the comparable export does NOT carry a return-on-capital column for the peers, so peer ROIC is marked "Not disclosed in export" rather than invented. HCG's own Return on Capital (Capital IQ definition: EBIT-based return on total capital) is available as a six-year series from the Financials export and is shown for HCG only; the comparative capital-efficiency read uses EBIT margin + the export's Credit Health "Operational" rank (1 = strongest, 4 = weakest) as labeled proxies.

| Company / Competitor | Gross Margin | EBIT Margin | ROIC | Period | Source |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| **HCG (HealthCare Global)** | 36.4%¹ | 8.7% | 4.6% (FY26); 4.5% FY25; 5.0% FY24² | LTM ≈ FY26 (Mar-2026) | Capital IQ comparable export, Operating Stats, 2026-06-01; ROC from Capital IQ Financials, Ratios, FY21–FY26 |
| Competitor 1 — Apollo Hospitals | Not disclosed (no stand-alone oncology line; not in this comp export) | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | — | 08_competitive-map §2 (Apollo not carried in the HCG relevancy-ranked export) |
| Competitor 2 — AOI (American Oncology Institute) | Not disclosed (private) | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | — | 08_competitive-map §2 |
| Competitor 3 — Max Healthcare | 62.4% | 21.5% | Not disclosed in export (Credit Health Operational = 1, strongest) | LTM (Dec-2025) | Capital IQ comparable export, Operating Stats + Credit Health, 2026-06-01 |
| Listed-bloc context — Narayana Hrudayalaya | 44.0% | 14.8% | Not disclosed in export (Operational = 3) | LTM | Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01 |
| Listed-bloc context — Fortis Healthcare | 41.6% | 17.9% | Not disclosed in export (Operational = 3) | LTM | Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01 |
| Listed-bloc context — Aster DM | 58.6% | 13.7% | Not disclosed in export (Operational = 2) | LTM | Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01 |
| Listed-bloc context — Medanta (Global Health) | 52.5% | 15.8% | Not disclosed in export (Operational = 2) | LTM | Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01 |
| **Comp-set median (10 listed peers)** | 55.5% | 16.8% | — | LTM | Capital IQ comparable export, Operating Stats, 2026-06-01 |

¹ HCG's FY26 gross margin fell from 53.6% (FY25) to 36.4% chiefly because of a COGS reclassification, not a margin collapse: COGS rose ₹10,319m → ₹16,200m (+₹5,881m) while "other operating expense" fell ₹7,188m → ₹4,597m (−₹2,591m); operating income still rose ₹1,780m → ₹2,216m and EBITDA margin rose to 18.3% [Capital IQ Financials, Income Statement + Ratios, FY25–FY26]. So the gross-margin gap vs peers is partly an accounting-classification artifact; **the EBIT-margin and ROIC gaps below are not.**

² HCG Return on Capital is single-digit and roughly flat across the cycle: FY21 −1.2%, FY22 2.8%, FY23 4.7%, FY24 5.0%, FY25 4.5%, FY26 4.6% [Capital IQ Financials, Ratios, FY21–FY26]. Return on Equity FY26 1.9%; net margin 0.5% [same source]. These sit well below any plausible India cost of capital (~12–13%; *Inference, not from filings*), i.e., HCG has not earned its cost of capital in any of the last six years.

Leverage / credit context (relevant because high leverage is the opposite of a moat — it makes profits easier to take): HCG carries the heaviest balance sheet in the set — Net Debt/EBITDA 2.6x, Total Debt/Capital 55.2% (comp median 28.5%) — and the weakest Credit Health scores: Overall 4, Operational 4, Solvency 4 (all the weakest band; 1 = strongest), versus Max Overall 3 / Operational 1, Medanta Overall 1 [Capital IQ comparable export, Operating Stats + Credit Health Panel, 2026-06-01].

---

#### 4. Where The Company Sits

**HCG sits at the BOTTOM of its named/listed peers on capital efficiency: lowest EBIT margin (8.7% vs 16.8% comp median), lowest net margin (0.5% vs 12.0%), highest leverage (Total Debt/Capital 55.2% vs 28.5%), and the weakest Credit Health operational and solvency rank (4 of 4) in the 11-company set** [Capital IQ comparable export, Operating Stats + Credit Health Panel, 2026-06-01]. Its own six-year Return on Capital (4.5–5.0% recently) has not reached double digits or its cost of capital [Capital IQ Financials, Ratios, FY21–FY26]. Head-to-head economics versus its two truest oncology rivals (Apollo's oncology vertical, private AOI) cannot be sized — neither discloses stand-alone oncology margins — so the "bottom of peers" verdict is anchored on the listed multi-specialty set, not on a pure-oncology comparison.

---

#### 5. Moat Verdict

**Narrow moat — and economically unproven so far.**

The strongest defensible source is **Technology / clinical depth (strength 48)** — a documented record of first-in-India treatment modalities, the largest oncologist base and tumour board, a NABL/CAP-accredited genomics lab, and ~970 research papers [FY24-25 AR, p.163-164, p.96, p.72 (printed)]. That accumulated know-how, combined with the largest dedicated-oncology footprint, plausibly makes it hard for AOI and Apollo's oncology vertical to *out-clinical* HCG quickly. But the moat is narrow and not yet visible in the numbers: HCG earns the lowest EBIT margin and net margin in its listed peer set and a single-digit Return on Capital below its cost of capital, while carrying the highest leverage [Capital IQ comparable export, 2026-06-01; Capital IQ Financials, Ratios, FY21–FY26] — the signature of an advantage that has not converted into defended profits. The durability test over the next 5 years: HCG must turn its scale/clinical lead into a sustained EBIT-margin and ROC step-up (management's own stated "margin enhancement and ROCE expansion" roadmap [FY24-25 AR, p.22 (printed)]) — specifically Return on Capital crossing ~12% and EBIT margin closing toward the ~16% peer median — *without* the gap simply reflecting equipment any well-capitalised rival (Max, Apollo) can buy. Until margins and returns rise toward peer levels, the moat is a clinical-reputation edge, not a profit-protecting one.

*Banned-phrase note: the AR's own "best-in-class," "industry-leading," and "leadership positions" language is the company's marketing and is cited as a company assertion, not adopted as this agent's conclusion.*

---

## BM-10 · external-dependency — External Dependency Check — HCG

### External Dependency Check — HCG

HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787) — India-focused cancer-care ("oncology") hospital chain. 100% of turnover is Medical and Healthcare (NIC 8610) [FY24-25 Annual Report, BRSR Section A, p.180]. ~25 hospitals across 18 Indian cities plus one cancer centre in Kenya (50% JV) [FY24-25 Annual Report, MD&A, p.146–148; Q4/FY26 transcript, 20-May-2026, prepared remarks].

**Score direction note: this module's score is INVERTED — higher = WORSE (more dangerous external dependence).** All Dependency-Level cells below are also read as higher = worse.

#### 1. Dependency Table

| External Variable | Dependency Level (Low / Mid / High) | Why It Matters | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Commodity prices | **Low** | Drugs/consumables ("cost of consumption") are 25.72% of total revenue, but they are sourced goods under regulated drug pricing, not exchange-traded commodities. The financial-risk note discloses FX and interest-rate market risk only — no commodity price sensitivity is quantified, despite the risk committee nominally listing "commodity risk." | Cost of consumption 25.72% of revenue [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.169]; market-risk note discloses FX + interest rate only, no commodity figure [FY24-25 AR, Note 40 (Consolidated), p.351–352]; committee remit lists commodity risk [FY24-25 AR, p.114] |
| Interest rates | **Mid** | Variable-rate borrowings of INR 6,791.84m; a +1% rate move cuts PBT by INR 67.92m. Total debt INR 10,022.31m, D/E 1.09, EBITDA/interest coverage 2.74x (down from 3.19x), finance costs INR 1,545.61m (+42% YoY). Coverage is thinning while the company funds a ~1,000-bed expansion. This is the largest disclosed market-risk sensitivity — but management actively manages it (rights-issue proceeds used to repay long-term debt; average cost ~8%). | Interest-rate sensitivity: +1% = −INR 67.92m PBT on INR 6,791.84m variable debt [FY24-25 AR, Note 40 (Consolidated), p.369]; D/E 1.09, EBITDA/interest 2.74x, finance costs +42% to INR 1,545.61m [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.173, p.169]; ~8% avg cost, debt repaid post rights issue [Q4/FY26 transcript, 20-May-2026, Q&A p.8] |
| FX | **Low** | Exchange risk "arises mainly from foreign currency borrowings." Net unhedged FX liability only INR 456.15m (US$455m + EUR 1m), and a +1% Rs/USD move impacts PBT by just INR 4.55m. All debt is INR-denominated except INR 214.34m (US$/Euro equipment-vendor loans + the Kenya term loan), ~2% of total borrowings. HCG is a rupee-revenue, rupee-cost business. | Net FX liability INR 456.15m; +1% Rs/USD = −INR 4.55m PBT [FY24-25 AR, Note 40 (Consolidated), p.368–369]; only INR 214.34m of debt non-INR [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.173] |
| Freight / logistics rates | n/a — dropped | Not a meaningful cost line for a hospital operator; no freight/logistics sensitivity disclosed and the business is not goods-distribution. | No freight/logistics risk disclosed in Note 40 or MD&A risk section [FY24-25 AR, Note 40, p.350–352; MD&A "Risks and concerns", p.166] |
| Government policy | **High** | Management names this first among risks: "Government intervention, by way of an active regulatory regime, be it in terms of price control or capping of margins on medicines has been stepped up. State and Central Healthcare coverage schemes are also impacting industry margins." Drug pricing is set under the Drugs (Prices Control) Order, 2013 (DPCO). Government schemes (Ayushman Bharat/PM-JAY, CGHS, ECHS, state schemes) shape volumes and reimbursement rates; HCG centres are enrolled in CGHS, ECHS and state programs. This variable hits both revenue per patient and margin and is outside management control. | "Government intervention … price control or capping of margins … impacting industry margins" [FY24-25 AR, MD&A "Risks and concerns", p.149, p.166]; DPCO [FY24-25 AR, p.75, p.98]; enrolled in CGHS/ECHS/state schemes [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.164] |
| Regulation | **High** | Hospital operations depend on a dense licensing/accreditation regime, any of which can interrupt operations or raise cost: Clinical Establishments Act 2010, Pharmacy Act, Drugs & Cosmetics Act, Radiation Protection / AERB radiation-surveillance rules and X-Ray safety code (critical for LINAC radiation and PET-CT oncology equipment), PNDT Act, Transplantation of Organs Act, Narcotic Drugs Act, Bio-Medical Waste rules, plus NABH/NABL/CAP accreditation. Management lists "licensing of our centres and hospitals and the licensing of our medical staff … delays/failure in receiving government approvals" as a results driver. | Statutory framework incl. DPCO, Clinical Establishments Act, Radiation Surveillance, X-Ray Safety, PNDT, Transplantation, Narcotic, BMW rules [FY24-25 AR, Secretarial Audit / compliance list, p.75, p.98, p.115]; NABH/NABL/CAP external audits [FY24-25 AR, p.159, p.171]; licensing risk [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.166 risk (a)] |
| Weather | n/a — dropped | No seasonality, monsoon or weather-driven demand language anywhere in the filing. Oncology demand is condition-driven, not weather-driven. | No weather/seasonal demand disclosure in MD&A or risk section [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.166–167]; only historical COVID references appear, not weather |
| Geopolitics | **Low** | Overwhelmingly domestic India. The only cross-border exposures are the small Kenya JV (non-current investment INR 43.09m; "still a smaller part of the portfolio") and inbound medical tourism from Africa/Middle East/SAARC/CIS into Indian hospitals — a revenue tailwind, not a controlling dependency. | Kenya investment INR 43.09m [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.154, p.171]; "smaller part of the portfolio" [Q4/FY26 transcript, 20-May-2026, prepared remarks, line 162]; medical tourism inbound [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.164] |
| Consumer cycle | **Mid** | Oncology treatment is largely non-discretionary, so core demand is more defensive than cyclical-consumer. But payment is consumer-/payer-sensitive: patients are self-pay or covered via third-party payers (insurance, TPAs, government schemes). Affordability and insurance penetration drive realisation and the doubtful-debt cycle — management warns "if we do not receive payments on time from our payers … provisions for disallowances reduce our revenue … and provisions for doubtful trade receivables increase our expenses." Expected-credit-loss allowance grew to INR 807.88m (from INR 700.91m). | Self-pay vs third-party payer dependence and disallowance risk [FY24-25 AR, MD&A "Risks and concerns", p.166 risk (c)]; receivables from "government payors, corporate bodies, insurers, and patients who pay directly to us" [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.171]; ECL allowance INR 807.88m [FY24-25 AR, Note 40, p.367] |
| Industrial cycle | n/a — dropped | Healthcare delivery is not tied to an industrial/capex cycle; no industrial-cycle exposure disclosed. | Business is 100% medical/healthcare services [FY24-25 AR, BRSR Section A, p.180]; no industrial-cycle dependency in risk section [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.166–167] |

#### 2. Sensitivity, If Disclosed

The company discloses quantitative market-risk sensitivities for interest rate and FX (Consolidated, Note 40) [FY24-25 AR, p.368–369]. No commodity-price sensitivity is published.

| Sensitivity (Consolidated) | Impact on PBT, FY25 | Impact on PBT, FY24 | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Interest rate +1% (on INR 6,791.84m variable-rate debt) | −INR 67.92m | −INR 53.20m | [FY24-25 AR, Note 40, p.369] |
| Interest rate −1% | +INR 67.92m | +INR 53.20m | [FY24-25 AR, Note 40, p.369] |
| Rs/USD +1% (net FX liability INR 456.15m) | −INR 4.55m | −INR 3.59m | [FY24-25 AR, Note 40, p.369] |
| Rs/Euro +1% | −INR 0.01m | −INR 1.34m | [FY24-25 AR, Note 40, p.369] |

Context for scale: FY25 consolidated PBT (before exceptional items / JV share) was INR 561.93m and total revenue INR 22,576.64m [FY24-25 AR, MD&A, p.168–169]. The interest-rate line is the only market-risk variable large enough to move PBT by a double-digit percentage on a modest rate shift; FX is immaterial. Note: the largest external pressures (DPCO price control, payer disallowances) are NOT captured by these market-risk sensitivities and are not quantified by the company.

#### 3. Classification

**Partly externally driven.**

HCG is a rupee-in, rupee-out domestic oncology hospital operator with structural (cancer-incidence-driven), largely non-discretionary demand. Financial market variables are minor-to-moderate: FX and commodity exposure are immaterial, and interest-rate exposure — while the largest disclosed sensitivity and rising with a leveraging, expansion-funding balance sheet — is partly within management's control (rights-issue-funded debt repayment, fixed/floating mix). The genuinely material and uncontrollable forces are government healthcare policy (DPCO drug price control, margin capping, the licensing/accreditation regime) and payer behaviour (insurance/TPA/government-scheme reimbursement and disallowances). These set a ceiling on pricing and a floor under bad-debt risk. But management retains real levers: it has shifted payer mix favourably (+100bps), is deliberately replacing low-margin business to lift average revenue per patient, and controls bed additions, clinical-talent mix and cost. So this is neither a pure pass-through nor company-controlled — it sits in the middle, with policy/regulation as the dominant exogenous overhang.

#### 4. External Dependency Risk Score

**45 / 100** (INVERTED scale — higher = worse).

Band: 41–60 — "Material external exposure, mixed mitigation."

Rationale: two High-dependency variables (government policy, regulation) that management cannot control and that directly cap pricing and licensing, pulling the score above the "partly externally driven / hedgeable" band (21–40). Offsetting this: immaterial FX and commodity exposure, non-cyclical demand, demonstrated pricing/mix and deleveraging levers, and India-domestic simplicity, which keep it well below the "mostly externally driven" band (61–80). The score is consistent with the "Partly externally driven" classification.

#### 5. The Single Biggest Lever

**Government healthcare pricing policy (DPCO drug price control and margin capping).** A 20%-adverse tightening — for example, deeper margin caps on medicines/consumables or expanded price ceilings on oncology drugs and devices — would compress the 25.72%-of-revenue cost-of-consumption spread and squeeze margins directly, with no hedge available; management itself flags that "price control or capping of margins on medicines has been stepped up" and is "impacting industry margins" [FY24-25 AR, MD&A "Risks and concerns", p.149, p.166].

---

## BM-11 · capital-allocation-governance — Capital Allocation & Governance — HCG

### Capital Allocation & Governance — HCG

HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Report date: 2026-06-01.

Scope note: this agent judges how cash is used and how the company is run. It does not score business quality, moat, or the overall verdict. The disqualifier-scan agent separately covers the hardest triggers (audit qualification, going concern, >50% promoter pledge, >25% related-party transactions); none of those is breached here. Figures are consolidated unless stated. Reporting currency INR million. The most recent *audited annual report* in the pool is FY24-25 (year ended 31 Mar 2025); FY26 (year ended 31 Mar 2026) is covered by the 20 May 2026 earnings transcript and 19 May 2026 deck only — the FY26 audited report is not yet in the pool. FCF is not separately scored here (valuation module); net debt = total borrowings + lease liabilities − cash, per the company's own leverage discussion.

Central fact bearing on almost every row: control of HCG changed hands twice in five years. Founder Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar sold control to CVC (via Aceso Company Pte Ltd) in 2020; CVC then sold ~51.59% to KKR-affiliated Hector Asia Holdings II Pte Ltd and KIA EBT II Scheme 1 under an SPA dated 23 Feb 2025, with the transfer completing on 30 May 2025 and Hector Asia (KKR) acquiring sole control [FY24-25 Annual Report, Directors' Report §15 (Change in control and Open Offer), p.76-77]. A mandatory open offer for up to 26% at INR 504.41/share (aggregate INR 1,870.87 crore) was launched by Kotak on behalf of the acquirer [same, p.77]. The year-end (31 Mar 2025) shareholding still shows CVC/Aceso as promoter; the KKR transfer is a subsequent event.

#### 1. Signal Table

Severity is INVERTED — **higher score = worse**.

| Signal | Observation | Evidence | Severity /100 *(higher = worse)* |
|---|---|---|---:|
| Acquisition pattern (frequency, size, integration outcomes) | Serial acquirer of hospitals/diagnostics; consolidated goodwill near-doubled to INR 4,299.50m (from 2,229.35m) on the FY25 Vizag deal, where INR 3,410.20m total consideration bought only INR 1,576.62m net identifiable assets (goodwill = 61% of price) plus a put option and a forward liability. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 7/7A Goodwill, p.333; Note 45.1 Business Combinations (VHCRPL), p.373-374] | 58 |
| Net share count trajectory (buybacks minus issuance, dilution) | Through FY25 dilution was minimal (shares 139.12m→139.42m, ESOP-only, <0.3% over two years) and there were no buybacks; in FY26 the company raised INR 425 crore via a rights issue, a real new dilution whose exact share-count impact is not yet in the pool. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 15.1 share reconciliation, p.258; Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks (rights issue INR 425 cr), 20 May 2026] | 40 |
| Dividend policy & coverage | No dividend has been paid; consolidated retained earnings are negative at INR (4,092.38)m at 31 Mar 2025, which legally precludes dividends; cash is fully retained for capex and M&A. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 16 Other equity (consolidated), p.340; Consolidated Cash Flow Statement — no dividend line, p.314] | 30 |
| Capex intensity vs depreciation (growth vs maintenance) | Capex is growth-led and exceeds depreciation: FY25 PP&E additions INR 2,088.67m vs depreciation/amortisation INR 2,113.44m, plus INR 1,971.95m of business-acquisition cash and rising gross PP&E (net block 12,689.18m vs 10,146.94m); FY26 growth capex guided at ~INR 90-100 cr for 200 brownfield beds. | [FY24-25 AR, Consolidated Cash Flow Statement, p.314; Note 5 PP&E, p.329; Q4/FY26 transcript, Q&A (capex per bed), 20 May 2026] | 45 |
| Debt level and trajectory (absolute + vs EBITDA) | Total debt incl. lease liabilities rose ~33% to INR 15,542.87m at 31 Mar 2025 (term/deferred 7,192.97m + leases 8,349.90m), D/E 1.09 (from 0.81), EBITDA/interest 2.74x (from 3.19x), net debt/EBITDA ~2.2-2.3x; in FY26 the rights issue and debt repayment cut net debt/EBITDA to 1.4x (internal ceiling 2.5x), ICRA rating A+ stable. | [FY24-25 AR, debt reconciliation, p.315; Liabilities ratios (D/E, EBITDA/interest, credit rating), p.173-174; Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks + Q&A (1.4x, 8% rate), 20 May 2026] | 48 |
| Related-party transactions | Operating RPTs are immaterial vs group scale (largest items single-digit to ~INR 19m: course-marketing to GHA Academy 19.17m, income from HCG Foundation 15.98m, capital-asset purchase from Sada Sarada 18.20m); the >25% disqualifier threshold is not breached. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 43 Related Party Disclosure, p.371-372; CARO Annexure A clause (xiii) RPT compliance, p.224] | 30 |
| Insider / promoter ownership and changes | Promoter is an institutional PE owner, not a founder block; at 31 Mar 2025 Aceso (CVC) held 60.40% and founder Dr. Ajaikumar 10.41% (both flat YoY), but control passed to KKR's Hector Asia (~51.59%) on 30 May 2025 with Aceso reclassified Promoter→Public — a full change of controlling shareholder. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 15.3/15.4 shareholding (consolidated), p.339; Directors' Report §15, p.76-77] | 55 |
| Promoter share pledging *(Indian listing)* | No promoter share pledge is disclosed; the promoter is an institutional PE holding company, and CARO reporting confirms no loans raised against pledge of shares in subsidiaries/JV. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 15.4 (no pledge noted), p.339; CARO Annexure A clause (ix)(f), p.224] | 8 |
| Auditor history (changes, qualifications, key audit matters) | Auditor is B S R & Co. LLP (KPMG India network), opinion UNMODIFIED on both standalone and consolidated statements, no auditor change and explicit "no resignation of statutory auditors during the year"; KAMs are impairment of goodwill and of investment in subsidiaries/related loans. | [FY24-25 AR, Independent Auditor's Report — Opinion + KAM, p.216-217 (standalone) and p.304-305 (consolidated); auditor signature B S R & Co. LLP, FRN 101248W/W-100022, 24 May 2025, p.225; CARO clause (xviii), p.225] | 22 |
| Restatements / accounting policy changes | FY24 comparatives were restated for the common-control acquisition of the oncology business from HCG NCHRI Oncology LLP (slump sale, accounted from 1 Apr 2023 under Ind AS 103 Appendix C) — a disclosed reorganization restatement, not an error correction; auditor opinion not modified on this matter. | [FY24-25 AR, Auditor's Report "Other Matter" (standalone), p.218; AOC-2 (NCHRI BTA), p.118] | 25 |
| Off-balance-sheet items | No hidden off-balance-sheet vehicles; the Vizag put option (INR 608m) and 34% forward-acquisition liability (INR 1,347m) are recognized on the balance sheet, and the largest contingent item is an EPCG export-obligation duty of INR 517.58m (total claims-not-acknowledged ~INR 689m vs total assets INR 35,432m). | [FY24-25 AR, Note 45.1 (put/forward liabilities), p.373-374; Note 33 Contingent liabilities, p.354-356; Note 34 Commitments (capital commitments INR 1,975.24m), p.356] | 35 |
| Working capital trend (receivable days, inventory days, cash conversion) | Receivables outpaced revenue — consolidated trade receivables rose 36% to INR 4,008.96m vs ~15% revenue growth, with ~INR 993m of billed receivables aged over one year and a loss allowance of INR 807.88m; FY26 operating-cash-flow conversion fell to ~75% of EBITDA, which management attributed to working capital. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 13 Trade receivables + ageing, p.336-337; Q4/FY26 transcript, Q&A (75% conversion), 20 May 2026] | 50 |
| Senior management turnover (CEO, CFO, board chair in last 3 years) | Near-complete C-suite/board churn around the control change: CEO Meghraj Gore exited (effective 30 Jun 2025) and was replaced by KKR nominee Dr. Manish Mattoo; CFO changed twice (Raghavan→Ritolia in FY24, then Saxena announced for FY26); founder moved from Executive Chairman to Non-Executive Chairman; both CVC nominee directors resigned and KKR nominees joined on 30 May 2025. | [FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §19.3-19.4 and §21 KMP changes, p.78-79; Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks (new CFO Sanjeev Kumar Saxena, new Head of IR), 20 May 2026] | 62 |

#### 2. Classification

**Governance red flags** — driven specifically by control and management instability, not by accounting integrity.

The reasoning: most *accounting-integrity* rows are clean and low-severity (unmodified audit opinion by a KPMG-network firm with no auditor change, immaterial operating related-party transactions well under the 25% disqualifier threshold, no promoter pledge, no hidden off-balance-sheet vehicles, disclosed and shareholder-approved processes throughout). On those alone HCG would read as standard professional management. But two rows sit in red-flag territory and they are structural, not cosmetic: the company has changed controlling shareholder twice in five years (Founder → CVC 2020 → KKR May 2025) and has turned over almost its entire senior leadership (CEO, CFO twice, board chair role, both sets of PE nominee directors) in the same window, with a 26% open offer still being completed. A new controlling owner installing a new CEO and CFO resets strategy, capital-allocation philosophy, incentive design and the meaning of any multi-year track record. That is the defining governance feature of this name today, so the classification is set by it rather than averaged away against the clean accounting rows.

Two qualifiers keep this from being a worse call. First, every step is disclosed, SEBI-LODR/Companies-Act compliant, and shareholder-approved — this is orderly PE ownership transition, not concealment or self-dealing. Second, the earliest FY26 signals under KKR point toward tighter discipline, not looser: a rights issue used to cut net debt/EBITDA from ~2.2-2.3x to 1.4x, a stated leverage ceiling of 2.5x, and a portfolio-sharpening divestiture (sale of the Milann fertility business) that partially reverses the prior diversification roll-up [Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks + Q&A, 20 May 2026]. The capital-allocation concerns (goodwill-heavy serial M&A, rising debt into FY25, a 36% receivable build) are real but secondary to the control/management-discontinuity flag.

#### 3. Most Material Signal

The single signal that, if it deteriorated further, would harden this classification is **senior management turnover layered on the change of controlling shareholder**. HCG's verdict rests on a leadership team and an owner that are both new: KKR took sole control on 30 May 2025, a KKR-nominee CEO (Dr. Manish Mattoo) took over from 30 Jun 2025, and a new CFO (Sanjeev Kumar Saxena) was announced for FY26 — the third CFO in roughly three years. The reason this matters more than the goodwill or leverage rows is that those are quantifiable and currently improving (debt is falling, M&A pace is being reset toward oncology focus), whereas leadership/owner instability is what determines whether the FY26 deleveraging and divestiture discipline persist or prove to be transition-window optics. A further deterioration — for example a fast follow-on exit by KKR, another CEO/CFO change, or a re-acceleration of debt-funded acquisitions under the new team — would confirm that the company is run for sponsor timelines rather than durable operating discipline, and would lock the governance read firmly in red-flag territory. Conversely, two to three years of stable leadership executing the stated 2.5x leverage ceiling and oncology-focus strategy would, on its own, justify re-rating this toward standard professional management.

#### 4. Capital Allocation Score /100

**52 / 100** (higher = better discipline and cleaner governance).

Derivation: the table averages to a mean severity of roughly 43 (higher = worse), which inverts to about 57 on a higher-is-better basis. That is then weighted down to 52 for what matters most in this specific case — the two highest-severity rows (control change at 55 and senior-management turnover at 62) are the load-bearing governance facts and are weighted above the clean accounting rows rather than averaged flat against them, consistent with not letting low-severity audit/RPT rows dilute a structural red flag. The score sits in the middle "mixed" band: accounting integrity, disclosure quality and the early FY26 deleveraging/focus moves pull it up, while two PE control changes in five years, full leadership turnover, goodwill-heavy serial M&A, and a 36% receivable build pull it down. It is not lower because nothing here impairs the integrity of the reported numbers (unmodified opinion, no auditor change, immaterial RPTs, no pledge, nothing hidden off balance sheet); it is not higher because the people and owner steering capital allocation are largely unproven in their current configuration.

---

## BM-12 · red-flags-sweep — Red Flags Sweep — HCG

### Red Flags Sweep — HCG

HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Sweep date: 2026-06-01. India-domiciled cancer-care hospital network, Ind AS reporter.

All six upstream specialist outputs are present (disqualifier-scan, segment-map, customer-geography, business-quality, external-dependency, capital-allocation-governance). No degraded-confidence flag needed. Authoritative audited base is the FY24-25 Integrated Annual Report (year ended 31 Mar 2025; auditor B S R & Co. LLP, signed 24 May 2025). FY26 (year ended 31 Mar 2026) is covered by the 19 May 2026 investor deck and 20 May 2026 earnings call only — the FY26 audited annual report is not in the pool.

Direction note: the Severity column in Section 2 is **INVERTED — higher = worse**.

---

#### 1. Already Covered Upstream

The items below are already prominent in upstream reports. This sweep does NOT re-flag them.

| Upstream Agent | Flag Already Surfaced |
|---|---|
| disqualifier-scan | No disqualifier triggered; FY24 pooling-of-interests restatement (Nagpur slump-sale) examined and cleared; CVC→KKR control change as a subsequent event; data gap on post-changeover Reg-31 pledge disclosure; CARO title-deed minor adverse remark. |
| segment-map | Single-segment concentration (one Ind AS 108 segment, ~97.6% oncology); economically decisive splits (centre-maturity ROCE, payor mix, cluster utilisation) live only in the deck, "excl. fertility" / "pre-Ind AS," not reconciled to audited statements; Milann fertility divestiture (EV ₹632m) + ₹319m goodwill impairment; 6 emerging centres at −22% ROCE. |
| customer-geography | Geographic concentration ~98% India (audited country split); ~33% of FY26 revenue is government-scheme payers on rate cards HCG does not set; no long-term volume contracts (per-episode, walk-in/referral); no single customer >10% (so customer concentration is NOT a flag). |
| business-quality | Heavy and rising capital intensity (capex/sales 11.5%, capex > D&A); group ROCE ~5% / FCF margin ~2%; pricing power capped near ~5% medical inflation (scored 48); thin and noisy PAT margin; disclosure-quality gap (deck-only cohort economics). |
| external-dependency | Government healthcare pricing/DPCO drug price control and margin capping (the single biggest lever); dense licensing/AERB radiation regime; interest-rate sensitivity (+1% = −₹67.92m PBT) on rising debt; FX and commodity exposure immaterial; payer-disallowance / ECL allowance ₹807.88m. |
| capital-allocation-governance | Two PE control changes in five years (Founder→CVC→KKR) + near-total C-suite/board churn (CEO change, third CFO in ~3 years) — the defining governance flag; goodwill-heavy serial M&A (Vizag: goodwill = 61% of price); debt rose ~33% into FY25, EBITDA/interest 2.74x, rights issue cut net-debt/EBITDA to 1.4x; 36% receivables build / ~75% CFO-to-EBITDA conversion; no dividend (negative retained earnings); unmodified audit opinion, no auditor change, immaterial RPTs, no promoter pledge; Vizag put/forward liabilities noted as "recognised on balance sheet, nothing hidden." |

---

#### 2. New Red Flags

Severity is INVERTED — **higher = worse**.

| Red Flag | Why It Matters | Evidence | Severity /100 *(higher = worse)* |
|---|---|---|---:|
| **Minority buy-out obligations (written puts + forward) are larger and harder-committed than the "nothing hidden" framing — and management says it depends on banks to fund them and "cannot assure" the funding** | Capital-allocation listed the Vizag put/forward as recognised on-balance-sheet and benign (severity 35). But the aggregate claim is larger than that row implied and is a near/medium-term call on cash: written put options to non-controlling interests **₹1,778.0m** (up from ₹931.58m a year earlier) **+** forward liability for purchase of investment **₹1,540.0m** (new) = **~₹3,318m** of committed equity buy-outs, of which **₹1,401.34m of put liability already sits in *current* financial liabilities**. Against ~₹3,171m annual operating cash flow and ~₹9.1bn gross debt, this is a real funding burden. Management's own risk factor states it relies on banks/FIs to "bridge the gap between cash flow from operating activities and investing activities (including put options of the partners)" and "cannot assure that the banks and financial institutions would fund us as per the planned timelines." That is a self-disclosed financing dependency the capital-allocation row did not surface. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 34 Commitments — written put options ₹1,778.00m, forward liability ₹1,540.00m, print p.356]; [FY24-25 AR, MD&A — "liability on put options amounting to INR 1,401.34 million" in current financial liabilities, print p.~169]; [FY24-25 AR, Risk Factors — bank funding "including put options of the partners… cannot assure… would fund us as per the planned timelines", print p.~149] | 55 |
| **Auditor flagged that the accounting-software audit-trail (edit log) was NOT enabled at the database level for revenue/consumption and for GL/payroll masters during FY25** | Capital-allocation called the audit "clean / unmodified, no auditor change" and scored auditor history low-severity (22). True for the opinion — but the auditor's Rule 11(f) reporting carries a specific control gap that no upstream agent surfaced: for the **revenue and consumption** books the edit-log feature was disabled at the database level from 1–5 Apr 2024; for another software (general ledger) it was disabled for direct/privileged database changes and at application level for certain tables from 1 Apr–15 May 2024, **and for payroll-master tables for the complete year.** Revenue and payroll are exactly the books where an unrecorded edit matters most. The auditor found no evidence of tampering where the trail was on, so this is a control-design weakness, not proof of manipulation — but it is a genuine internal-financial-controls finding that the "clean audit" framing omits. | [FY24-25 AR, Independent Auditor's Report (Standalone), Rule 11(f) audit-trail paragraphs, print p.219-220] | 38 |
| **Reported earnings are thin and heavily adjusted, and one of the FY26 "normalisations" (new Labour Codes) is a recurring forward cost, not a clean one-off** | Business-quality noted PAT is "thin and noisy" but did not quantify how reliant the headline is on add-backs, nor flag the labour-code cost. FY26 **reported** consolidated PAT is **₹137.6m (0.5% margin)** vs management's **adjusted PAT ₹556.8m (2.2%)** — a ~4x gap. Two of the three add-backs are one-time negatives (fertility goodwill impairment ₹319m; ESOP/one-time costs), but the third — new **Labour Code** impact of ₹127m (post-tax ~₹100m) — is a statutory wage/benefit cost that recurs once the codes are in force, so treating it as "normalised away" overstates clean run-rate earnings. On a standalone basis FY25 **basic EPS was ₹0.25** (PAT ₹35.27m), underlining how little true bottom-line cushion exists beneath the adjusted headline. Important: these adjustments are one-time *charges* — the read is that reported earnings are fragile, not that they are inflated. | [Q4/FY26 Investor Deck, 19 May 2026, "Q4 & FY26 Consolidated P&L" — reported PAT ₹137.6m, Adj. PAT ₹556.8m, normalisation note (1): Labour Code ₹127m / ~₹100m post-tax + fertility impairment ₹319m]; [FY24-25 AR, Note 35 EPS — standalone basic EPS ₹0.25, PAT ₹35.27m, print p.~264] | 40 |
| **EPCG export-obligation contingency: duty-free imported equipment carries an unfulfilled-export-obligation tail unusual for a domestic hospital** | Not owned by any upstream agent. HCG imports LINACs / PET-CT / TrueBeam radiation equipment customs-duty-free under the Export Promotion Capital Goods (EPCG) scheme, conditional on meeting export obligations within a fixed window; if missed, it must repay the duty benefit plus levies. The contingent "duties and taxes in respect of EPCG licenses" was **₹490.81m** (FY25, consolidated), up sharply from ₹345.45m. For a ~98%-domestic patient-care business, an export obligation is a structurally awkward condition — meeting it depends on medical-value-travel ("exports of services"), which the same filings flag as soft (Middle-East conflict hit MVT in Q4 FY26). Management "expects" to meet the obligations; that is an estimate, not a settled fact. | [FY24-25 AR, Note 33 Contingent liabilities — EPCG duties ₹490.81m (FY25) vs ₹345.45m (FY24), print p.355]; [FY24-25 AR, Note 24/EPCG note — "for certain licenses there is unfulfilled condition… expects that it will be able to meet its export obligations", print p.264] | 30 |
| **Profit leaks to minority partners while the company simultaneously commits to buy those same minorities out** | Secondary/structural. HCG runs many centres through partly-owned subsidiaries/LLPs: consolidated group PAT was ₹488.34m (FY25) but PAT **attributable to owners** was only ₹444.10m after ₹44.24m of non-controlling-interest profit share. The same minority stakes are the subject of the ₹3.3bn put/forward commitments above. So owners both share current profit with minorities AND owe future cash to buy them out — a double drag that the consolidated headline masks. Small in any single year, but it compounds the put-funding flag. | [FY24-25 AR, MD&A — group PAT ₹488.34m, NCI share ₹44.24m, PAT to owners ₹444.10m, print p.~169]; [FY24-25 AR, Note 34 / Note 45.1 (put & forward over subsidiary minorities)] | 28 |

---

#### 3. Most Severe New Flag

The single most material new flag is the **minority buy-out funding obligation**: ~₹3,318m of written put options (₹1,778.0m) plus forward-purchase liability (₹1,540.0m) committed to acquire non-controlling stakes, with ₹1,401.34m already classified current, against ~₹3.2bn of annual operating cash flow and ~₹9.1bn of gross debt [FY24-25 AR, Note 34, p.356; MD&A, p.~169]. The synthesizer should weight it because it is a contractual, near-dated claim on the very cash flow that business-quality showed is already almost fully consumed by expansion capex and below-EBITDA finance charges — and because management itself states, in its risk factors, that funding these partner puts depends on banks lending "per the planned timelines," which it "cannot assure" [FY24-25 AR, Risk Factors, p.~149]. This sharpens the existing leverage/capital-intensity picture rather than duplicating it: it is not "HCG has debt," it is "HCG has a self-disclosed, bank-dependent obligation to buy out minorities that competes with capex for the same constrained cash, and the company admits the funding is not assured." If MVT-driven export obligations or working-capital strain coincide with a tight credit window, the puts become the pressure point.

---

#### 4. Cross-Cutting Patterns

A consistent thread runs through three of the new flags: **HCG's reported headline is cleaner than its underlying cash and control reality.** The audit opinion is unmodified yet the auditor records a revenue/payroll edit-log control gap; reported PAT is positive yet ~4x below the adjusted figure and one "normalisation" is a recurring labour cost; the balance sheet shows "nothing hidden" yet carries ~₹3.3bn of bank-funding-dependent minority buy-out obligations plus an EPCG export tail. None of these is individually disqualifying and none contradicts the disqualifier-scan's clean read — but together they argue that the synthesizer should treat HCG's accounting *presentation* as flattering relative to its *cash-claim* profile, and discount management's adjusted metrics accordingly. This pattern reinforces, rather than offsets, the capital-allocation module's control-and-leadership-instability flag: an unproven new owner and CFO are inheriting a structure with embedded minority put liabilities and thin true earnings.

---

# PART III — Earnings · FULL MODULE (synthesis + 9 specialist reports)

*Folder: `analyses/HCG_2026-06-01/earnings/`. The module synthesis appears first (the module's own adjudicated verdict), followed by each specialist report in pipeline order, reproduced in full.*

## EARN-99 · Module Synthesis — Earnings Module — HCG (Synthesis)

### Earnings Module — HCG (Synthesis)

#### Abstract

HCG's earnings trend splits by line: revenue and EBITDA are accelerating (FY26 revenue +14.5%, reported EBITDA +20.3%, +88 bps margin), but reported PAT fell to ₹138 Mn from ₹444 Mn and intra-year revenue growth cooled from +16.6% (Q1) to +11.5% (Q4). The dominant driver is operating leverage as the ₹50–100 Mn ramping cohort (50% of revenue) matures on a near-flat 25-hospital, 58%-utilised base. The consensus bar is fair, not soft — FY27 revenue sits at the bottom of the guided 15–20% range and the EBITDA-margin bar (+141 bps) exceeds management's "~100 bps." The biggest risk is the structural below-EBITDA miss pattern: EPS missed consensus in three of the last four fiscal years and FY27 EPS-Normalized was cut 19% in six months. Verdict: mixed earnings setup.

#### 1. Earnings Verdict

- **Verdict:** Mixed earnings setup — accelerating at EBITDA, decelerating and distorted at PAT/EPS, with a fair (not soft) consensus bar and net-negative EPS revisions.
- Earnings quality /100: **66** *(from 06 — mostly clean, cash-backed at EBITDA, held in the 60s by eroding CFO/EBITDA, the FY25 receivable build, and a +305% adjusted-vs-reported PAT gap)*
- Consensus setup /100 *(higher = more beatable)*: **42** *(from 04 — bar assessed "Fair"; full CIQ consensus present so no 30-cap, but net-negative revision breadth and demanding revenue/EBITDA bars keep it in the mixed band, not beatable)*
- Earnings volatility /100 *(higher = WORSE — inverted)*: **64** *(from 07 — "High volatility"; confidence is order-of-magnitude only, see Section 4)*
- Next-quarter setup: **Balanced** *(from 05 — revenue/EBITDA modestly miss-tilted vs a demanding FQ1 bar, EPS miss-tilted, but the EPS bar is freshly reset lower)*
- Biggest earnings driver (one line): IP-patient volume produced through utilisation of the existing 25-hospital network (58% blended, West 45%-of-revenue at 50%) — currently improving, contributes ~12 of ~15 ex-fertility growth points.
- Biggest earnings risk (one line): The recurring below-EBITDA EPS miss — D&A, finance cost, tax, and exceptionals have driven a normalized-EPS miss in three of the last four fiscal years, and FY27 EPS-Normalized has been cut 19% in six months while reported PAT fell to ₹138 Mn.
- **Red-flag agent Severity Verdict (08, reported verbatim): "Material concerns."**

#### 2. Specialist Roll-Up

| Specialist | Verdict Line | Biggest Finding |
|---|---|---|
| earnings-data-triage | Sufficient — no active partial-data caps | Recent full-year audited results deck + transcript (May 2026), FY25 Annual Report, full CIQ consensus/revisions/surprise, segment detail all present; only FY26 statutory statements and discrete FY25 quarter packs absent (granularity only) |
| historical-financials | Revenue stable-to-decelerating; EBITDA accelerating; earnings-quality the real inflection | EBITDA up but reported PAT collapsed ₹482→₹444→₹138 Mn (FY24–FY26); FCF fell to ₹586 Mn as capex hit ₹2,885 Mn; net debt cut to 0.73x via a ₹4,250 Mn rights issue, not FCF |
| revenue-drivers | Growth organic and volume-led, not acquisition/FX | ~12 of ~15 ex-fertility growth points are volume; management says 75–80% of growth comes from existing-hospital utilisation; Milann (~2.4%) divests in Q1 FY27 (a forward subtraction) |
| margin-drivers | +88 bps EBITDA gain from operating leverage on a weakening gross base | COGS is the single biggest margin driver and a structural headwind (27.2% of revenue, +108 bps FY26, DPCO-capped, no contractual pass-through); the leverage tailwind is bounded by the mature-cohort ceiling |
| guidance-consensus | Bar is fair — with a downward EPS revision tilt | Revenue at bottom of guided 15–20%; EBITDA-margin bar (+141 bps) above the "~100 bps" guide; FY27 EPS-Normalized −19% over 6 months; EPS missed in 3 of last 4 FYs |
| beat-miss-setup | Balanced — revenue/EBITDA modestly miss-tilted, EPS miss-tilted, EPS bar freshly reset | FQ1 2027 revenue bar (₹7,340.9 Mn) implies ~+20% YoY vs a +11.5% Q4 exit — demanding; the base-rate prior is "operating line in-line, bottom line at risk" |
| earnings-quality | Score 66 — mostly clean, cash-backed, but eroding conversion and a distorted PAT line | CFO/EBITDA above 70% for four straight years (84%→74.5%, eroding); FY25 receivables +36% vs revenue +16%; reported FY26 PAT/EPS not a usable run-rate (adjusted PAT +305%) |
| earnings-sensitivity | Volatility 64 (inverted, higher = worse); confidence order-of-magnitude | Volume/utilisation and ARPP/case-mix each swing EBITDA ~₹600 Mn against a reported EPS base of ₹1.0; operating deleverage makes the downside non-linear; interest-rate sensitivity company-disclosed (+1% = −₹67.9 Mn PBT) |

*(08_earnings-red-flags is the dedicated red-flag specialist; its verdict — "Material concerns," 0 Critical / 10 High / 28 Medium / 9 Low — is carried in Section 1, Section 8, and throughout Sections 3 and 7 per the propagation rule.)*

#### 3. Reconciliation

1. **"Stable" (01) vs "decelerating" (02) revenue.** 01's FY22–FY26 summary tags revenue "Stable" (~16% compounding); 02 and 01's own §3/§6 body show intra-year YoY growth decelerating (+16.6% Q1 → +11.5% Q4 FY26). Reconciled view: the full-year *level* is stable but the within-year *run-rate is cooling* — I adopt the more conservative decelerating intra-year read, consistent with 08's contradiction table.

2. **TP raised (04) vs FY27 EPS cut −19% (04).** Brokers raised most target prices post-results even as FY27 EPS-Normalized was cut 19%. 04 reconciles this as a "base-year reset, not thesis deterioration." That reconciliation is plausible but unproven; 08 elevates it to a narrative High flag ("story outrunning the numbers"). Reconciled view: I treat the TP-up / EPS-down split as an unresolved narrative flag, not as a confirmed bull signal — the conservative reading.

3. **Two EBIT definitions (03 deck-basis +21 bps vs 01 pre-other-income +180 bps).** 03 explicitly reconciles: the gap is FY25 other-income treatment; the clean operating signal is Adjusted EBITDA +68 bps. Reconciled view: read margins at Adjusted EBITDA (+68 bps); the EBIT line is the least clean trend metric and is not read in isolation.

4. **Red-flag agent's "degraded-confidence" framing vs the now-present 05 and 07.** 08 was run before 05_beat-miss-setup and 07_earnings-sensitivity existed and flagged both as "missing," reconstructing categories 2.6 (beat/miss) and 2.8 (sensitivity) from raw data and applying a Low-confidence cap on earnings volatility. Both dedicated agents are now present: 05 returns a "Balanced" setup verdict and 07 returns a 64 volatility score. Reconciled view: the beat/miss read is the dedicated agent's "Balanced," not merely reconstructed; the volatility score (64) is real. However, 07 itself states its four highest-impact variables are not company-quantified and are "order-of-magnitude, not precise," so the substance of 08's caution survives — I carry earnings-volatility confidence as **Low** (Section 4), which both 07's own note and 08's cap support. This is not a verdict conflict.

5. **Red-flag verdict ("Material concerns") vs this synthesis verdict ("Mixed earnings setup").** No conflict. 08 itself states the evidence supports "Mixed earnings setup" and that the verdict must specify the metric (EBITDA up / PAT down). My verdict and 08's overlay are aligned; I report 08's verdict verbatim and do not soften it.

6. **Minority-put cross-module item (BM 12, ~₹3,318 Mn bank-funding-dependent).** The earnings module is otherwise silent on this; 08 surfaces it as the cross-module item that competes with FY27 expansion capex for the same constrained cash. It does not change the earnings *trajectory* verdict but is carried to the master synthesizer in Section 8. No within-module disagreement.

#### 4. Score Cap Application

| Cap Trigger | Applied? (Y/N) | Affected Score | Final Cap |
|---|---|---|---|
| No consensus / estimate data | **N** | Consensus setup | n/a — full CIQ consensus, revisions, trends, surprise present (00 §5; 04 §1); consensus setup is NOT capped at 30 |
| No cash flow statement | **N** | Earnings quality | n/a — audited consolidated cash flow FY23–FY25 + FY26 deck cash flow present (00 §3; 06 data status); earnings quality NOT capped at 45 |
| No revision history | **N** | Consensus setup | n/a — full CIQ revision/trend history present (04 §4–§5); consensus setup NOT capped at 60 |
| Only inferred sensitivities | **Y (partial)** | Earnings volatility confidence | **Confidence = Low.** Interest-rate and FX sensitivities are company-disclosed (Note 40), so this is not a fully-inferred case, but the four highest-impact variables (volume, ARPP, scheme tariff, COGS) are NOT company-quantified (07 §7 note; 08 §2.8). The volatility *score* (64) stands; its *confidence* is Low. |
| No quarterly data (earnings clarity max 60) | **N** | Earnings clarity | n/a — Q4/FY26, Q3/9M, Q2 decks + transcripts present (00 §5). |
| No earnings transcript (earnings clarity max 70) | **N** | Earnings clarity | n/a — Q4/FY26 (20 May 2026), Q3/FY26, Dec-2025 strategy transcripts present (00 §3). |
| No segment-level P&L (earnings clarity max 70) | **N (moot)** | Earnings clarity | Single-segment business (>97% oncology), so the no-segment-P&L cap is largely moot; centre-maturity cohort split (deck-tier) substitutes (02 §1; 03 §1; 08 §2.1). |
| Conflicting sources not reconcilable (overall usefulness max 65) | **N** | Overall usefulness | All material conflicts in Section 3 are reconcilable; no cap. |

Most restrictive binding cap: earnings-volatility **confidence Low** (the score itself is not numerically capped). No score is forced down by a partial-data cap; the consensus-setup score sits at 42 on the merits (fair bar + net-negative revisions), not because of a cap.

#### 5. Earnings Setup Summary

##### Revenue Setup
The current ~14.5% reported trajectory is sustainable in direction but not at the FY26 level without re-acceleration: the +11.5% Q4 exit rate, not the +14.5% full-year figure, is the run-rate going into FY27, and consensus already sits at the bottom of management's guided 15–20%. The single factor that would have to change to flip revenue direction is the West-cluster / ramping-cohort utilisation ramp (West is 45% of revenue at only 50% utilisation) — if that ramp stalls while a government-scheme disruption recurs (as Andhra Pradesh did in Q3 FY26), volume growth rolls over. Reported and underlying revenue diverge by roughly 2.4 percentage points in FY27: the Milann fertility divestiture (~₹605 Mn, close Q1 FY27) subtracts from reported revenue while organic oncology growth continues, so reported FY27 growth will understate the organic rate by about that gap.

##### Margin Setup
Current EBITDA margins are not at a cyclical peak in the usual sense — they are mid-ramp, with the +88 bps FY26 gain coming from spreading near-fixed costs over a ramping-cohort base, a tailwind that flattens (it does not reverse) once that cohort reaches the ~26% mature-cohort ceiling. The driver most able to take a large bite is COGS / drugs-and-consumables: a 10–20% adverse move in consumable inflation or a further shift toward drug-heavy medical oncology (38% of revenue) compresses gross margin faster than operating leverage can offset, because the regulated pharmacy slice is DPCO price-capped. HCG has no contractual margin-protection mechanism: pass-through is "slow and partial (annual mix/tariff cycle), not contractual," and on ~33% of revenue (government schemes) HCG is a pure price-taker — a structurally weaker margin-defence position than an operator with indexed escalators.

##### Quality Check
The single largest gap between reported and economic earnings is at the PAT line, not at EBITDA: reported FY26 PAT (₹138 Mn) understates economic earnings because two genuine, disclosed exceptionals (₹319 Mn goodwill impairment, ₹127 Mn labour-code) crushed it — the adjusted-vs-reported gap is +305%, and this is the largest gap in the file. The adjustments are mostly genuine one-offs, with one exception that recurs: the labour-code provision is a statutory forward cost dressed as a normalisation, so even adjusted PAT (₹557 Mn) is modestly flattered, and the ESOP add-back recurs every year (now tiny). To model normalized FY27 earnings I would start from adjusted PAT (₹557 Mn), not reported (₹138 Mn) — but haircut it for the recurring labour-code cost and watch DSO, because the FY25 receivable build (+36% vs +16% revenue) shows a chunk of revenue converts to cash slowly with real credit-loss leakage.

##### Consensus Bar
For HCG to beat the current bar by a material margin, Q4's two transient drags (deliberate low-margin paring + soft Middle East medical-value-travel) would have to normalise on management's "next couple of quarters" timetable AND the EBITDA-margin gain would have to run at or above the +141 bps the Street already prices — i.e. HCG must re-accelerate revenue and out-margin its own "~100 bps achievable" guide simultaneously. The bar is most likely set incorrectly at the FQ1 quarter level, where the ₹7,340.9 Mn revenue estimate implies ~+20% YoY against an +11.5% exit rate — that looks too high, and is the most probable miss-vs-bar even on healthy underlying growth. A meaningful slice of the current consensus is anchored to management's forward "normalisation" claim and to the rights-issue-driven interest-cost relief; the former is an unproven judgement, and a slower-than-promised normalisation would reset the forward bar down.

#### 5b. Leverage & Capital Structure

Leverage is within normal range and did not change materially in an adverse direction during the period — no dedicated treatment required. Net debt (company-defined, ex-lease) fell to ₹3,387 Mn = 0.73x reported EBITDA at 31 Mar 2026, from ₹6,317 Mn (1.63x) a year earlier (01 §1–2; FY26 deck p.15) — below the 3.0x absolute trigger and a *decrease*, not the >1.0x adverse increase that would fire Trigger B. The de-leveraging was funded by a ₹4,250 Mn rights issue completed in Q4 FY26, not by free cash flow (FCF fell to ₹586 Mn), and management cites an internal 2.5x ceiling with current leverage "1.4x" on its own broader basis (04 §2). One capital-structure item from the cross-module sweep is carried forward in Section 8 as a master-synthesizer note, not as a leverage trigger: ~₹3,318 Mn of bank-funding-dependent minority put/forward buy-out obligations (BM 12) that compete with FY27 expansion capex for the same cash — this is a fundability/contingency item, not current balance-sheet leverage, and HCG's reported net-debt/EBITDA does not capture it.

#### 6. Key Numbers

- Revenue growth rate: **+14.5% FY26** reported (₹22,228 → ₹25,454 Mn); intra-year decelerating +16.6% Q1 → +11.5% Q4 [01 §1, §3]
- EBITDA margin: **18.3% reported FY26** (+88 bps YoY); Adjusted 18.5% (+68 bps clean signal) [01 §1; 03 §3, §7]
- EPS: **reported ₹1.0 FY26** (not a usable run-rate); adjusted PAT ₹557 Mn implies ≈₹3.98; FY23–FY25 diluted ₹2.10 / ₹3.43 / ₹3.14 [01 §1, §4; 06 §7]
- CFO / EBITDA: **74.5% FY26** (above 70% for four straight years but eroding from 84%) [06 §1–2]
- Biggest driver current level: IP volume **294,912 patients FY26 (+12%)** on **58% blended utilisation** (West 50%) — the volume engine runs below capacity [02 §2, §7]
- Consensus gap (FY27): revenue **₹29,105.9 Mn (+14.3%)** at bottom of guided 15–20%; EBITDA-margin bar **+141 bps** vs "~100 bps" guide; EPS-Normalized **₹8.72** [04 §3]
- Estimate revision direction: **FY27 EPS-Normalized −19% over 6 months, −7.5% last month**; net breadth 2 up / 4 down (1-month) — net-negative [04 §4–5]
- Earnings volatility score: **64 / 100 (inverted, higher = worse)** — High volatility band; confidence Low [07 §7]

#### 7. What Would Change The Earnings Verdict?

| Current Verdict | What Would Upgrade It | What Would Downgrade It | Data Needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixed earnings setup | A clean, exceptional-free FQ1/FY27 print where revenue re-accelerates toward the ~15% guide AND D&A/finance/tax land where the cut estimates now sit — converting the EBITDA gain into bottom-line EPS and turning EPS revisions positive → toward "Earnings accelerating" | A government-scheme/DPCO tariff tightening on the ~33% policy-exposed revenue, a COGS/case-mix step-up the operating-leverage tailwind cannot offset, a re-build of the FY25-style receivable surge, a fresh exceptional, or a guide-down on the "normalisation" timetable → toward "Earnings decelerating" | FY26 audited statutory statements (current FY26 figures are deck-tier, not audited); FQ1 2027 print (release 30 Jul 2026) with D&A/finance/tax detail; per-modality/payor gross-margin split to size the COGS driver; quarterly consensus-surprise series; post-KKR-changeover Reg-31 shareholding / promoter-pledge line |

Constraint check: consensus setup is **not** "Unknown" (full CIQ data present), so the verdict is not forced to Mixed/Insufficient by that rule. The verdict is "Mixed earnings setup" on the merits — accelerating EBITDA but decelerating, distorted PAT/EPS, a fair-not-soft bar, and net-negative revisions. It is NOT "Earnings accelerating," consistent with the 08 red-flag agent's explicit guidance and the EBITDA-up / PAT-down divergence.

#### 8. Note To The Final Synthesizer

- **Red-flag Severity Verdict (08, verbatim): "Material concerns"** — 0 Critical, 10 High, 28 Medium, 9 Low, 2 Unclear. No Critical means no automatic verdict-lock, but the High cluster supports a "Material concerns" overlay on the earnings setup.
- The "earnings" story is two stories: EBITDA accelerates (+20%), the bottom line decelerates and is distorted (reported PAT −69% to ₹138 Mn). Any one-word earnings verdict that does not name the metric will mislead — this is the single most important takeaway.
- Earnings are cash-backed *at the operating line* (CFO/EBITDA >70% for four years) but conversion is eroding (84%→74.5%) and the FY25 receivable build shows scheme/insurance revenue converts to cash slowly — the quality score of 66 means "real but fragile, with a PAT line you cannot model off the reported figure."
- The consensus bar is fair, not beatable: revenue sits at the bottom of guidance, the EBITDA-margin bar exceeds management's own guide, and the Street is actively cutting EPS — so a "beat" requires re-accelerating *and* out-margining the guide at once.
- Next-quarter setup is Balanced but the FQ1 revenue bar (~+20% implied) looks demanding against a +11.5% exit; the second-quarter (FQ2) look-ahead is modestly better (Q4 drags lapped, FQ2 is the seasonal margin high, Kolkata clinicians and Milann close in the base) — but FQ2's consensus bar is not yet tabulated.
- Top sensitivity variable is IP volume/utilisation (improving now); the most dangerous *compounding* channel is an adverse government-scheme shift, which hits ARPP and the COGS spread together on a third of revenue — and the volatility read is **Low-confidence** (the biggest variables are not company-quantified).
- **High-severity red flags propagated** (all 10 surfaced here as biggest risks): (1) EBITDA-up / PAT-down divergence; (2) target prices raised while FY27 EPS cut 19% — narrative outrunning numbers; (3) "accelerating" true only at EBITDA; (4) EPS cut hard with net-negative breadth into the print; (5) Street over-models the bottom line — EPS missed 3 of last 4 FYs; (6) adjusted PAT +305% of reported — headline not a run-rate; (7) ~33% of revenue on uncontrollable government-scheme tariffs; (8) COGS headwind structural, least controllable, no pass-through; (9) earnings dominated by uncontrollable DPCO/scheme policy; (10) below-EBITDA earnings hyper-sensitive to rates on a thin PBT base.
- Cross-module item the earnings module is otherwise silent on: ~₹3,318 Mn bank-funding-dependent minority put/forward obligations (BM 12) compete with FY27 expansion capex for the same constrained cash — relevant to whether the volume-ramp growth thesis is self-fundable without new leverage.
- Partial-data note: earnings-volatility confidence is Low (highest-impact sensitivities are inferred, not company-quantified). No score is hard-capped by missing data — consensus, cash flow, and revision history are all present.
- Biggest missing data point: FY26 audited statutory statements (all current FY26 figures rest on the 19 May 2026 deck/transcript, Level-3, not a Level-5 audited statement) — an audit true-up is an unquantified risk.
- What would change the verdict: a clean, exceptional-free FQ1 2027 print (30 Jul 2026) that converts the EBITDA gain into bottom-line EPS and turns revisions positive would move it toward accelerating; a DPCO/scheme tightening, COGS step-up, receivable re-build, fresh exceptional, or a "normalisation" guide-down would move it toward decelerating.

#### 9. Simple Summary

- Revenue: growing ~14.5%, but cooling within the year (+16.6% → +11.5%); driver is patient volume from filling an under-utilised hospital network (58% utilised), not price or acquisitions.
- Margin: EBITDA margin up +88 bps from operating leverage as ramping centres mature — but the gross margin is shrinking (drugs/consumables cost rising, DPCO-capped, no pass-through), so the tailwind is bounded.
- Earnings clean? At EBITDA, yes and cash-backed; at the bottom line, no — reported PAT (₹138 Mn / EPS ₹1.0) is distorted by two exceptionals and is not a usable run-rate (use adjusted ₹557 Mn, then haircut for a recurring labour-code cost).
- Consensus beatable? No — the bar is fair, not soft: revenue at the bottom of guidance, the margin bar above guidance, and EPS being cut.
- Next quarter: Balanced overall, but the FQ1 revenue bar (~+20% implied) looks too high; EPS is where HCG structurally misses (3 of last 4 years).
- Biggest sensitivity: patient volume/utilisation — but a government-scheme tariff cut is the most dangerous swing because it hits a third of revenue with no hedge.
- Earnings volatility: High (64/100 inverted) — small input moves swing a thin earnings base a lot; confidence in the magnitudes is Low.
- Useful for the master synthesizer? Yes — it gives a clear "accelerating at EBITDA, decelerating and fragile at PAT, fair-not-soft bar, Material-concerns red-flag overlay" read, with the metric specified so it does not mislead.

Note: HCG's latest audited full-year (FY26, Mar-31-2026) results are present via the May 2026 investor deck and earnings transcript. The most recent full Integrated Annual Report is FY25; the FY26 Annual Report is not yet in the pool (FY26 results were only approved May 19, 2026). FY26 income/EBITDA/revenue, Q4 detail, net debt, and EBITDA-to-CFO (~75%) are disclosed in the FY26 deck and transcript.

- **Verdict:** Sufficient
- **Reason:** A recent full-year audited results set (FY26 deck + transcript, May 2026) plus the FY25 Integrated Annual Report give complete income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow coverage, alongside the latest quarter, full consensus/revision/surprise data, and segment detail.
- **Active partial-data caps:** None.
- **Critical missing items:** None for sufficiency. Minor gaps (FY26 Integrated Annual Report not yet published; discrete FY25 quarter packs absent) constrain granularity only and do not trigger any MODULE_RULES cap.

## EARN-01 · historical-financials — Historical Financials — HCG

### Historical Financials — HCG

**Company:** HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787) — pan-India oncology-focused hospital chain.
**Reporting currency:** INR million (₹ Mn), consolidated, unless stated. Fiscal year ends 31 March (FY25 = year ended 31 Mar 2025).
**Basis note:** All figures post-Ind AS 116 (leases capitalised). Two EBITDA constructs appear in HCG disclosures and are kept separate throughout: **Reported EBITDA** (audited statutory operating result, pre other-income) and **Adjusted EBITDA** (company-defined; Reported EBITDA + ESOP cost + one-time costs). See Section 4.

> **Data limitation:** The data pool contains audited statutory consolidated statements for FY23, FY24 and FY25 (FY25 and FY24 Annual Reports). FY26 figures are taken from the **board-approved audited results presentation dated 19 May 2026** (the standalone audited statutory results PDF is not in the pool). FY26 numbers are therefore cited to that filing, not to a full statutory statement. FY22 levels are available only from a company multi-year chart (deck), so FY22 detail below the revenue/EBITDA line is marked N/A.

---

QoQ/YoY read: Revenue and Adjusted EBITDA grow every quarter on a YoY basis, but YoY revenue growth steps **down** through FY26: Q1 +16.6%, Q2 +16.9%, Q3 +13.3%, Q4 +11.5%. Q3 of each year is the softest margin quarter (16.5% FY25, 17.5% FY26). Reported EPS is erratic: Q3FY26 was a loss (−₹0.7) on a ₹127 Mn labour-code exceptional charge, and Q4FY26 fell to ₹0.2 on a ₹319 Mn goodwill-impairment exceptional [5, p.14; 1, p.20].

#### 4. Reported vs Adjusted Metrics — ₹ Mn, consolidated (FY26)

HCG discloses adjusted metrics. Adjustments add back ESOP cost and one-time costs to EBITDA, and add back exceptional items (labour-code provision, goodwill impairment) to PAT.

| Metric | Reported Value (FY26) | Adjusted Value (FY26) | Adjustment Amount | Adjustment Reason | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| EBITDA | 4,658 | 4,711 | +53 | + One-time cost ₹39 Mn + ESOP ₹14 Mn | [1] p.20 |
| EBIT | 2,466 | n/d | — | Company does not publish an adjusted EBIT | [1] p.20 |
| PAT to owners | 138 | 557 | +419 | + Labour-code charge ₹127 Mn (post-tax ~₹100 Mn) + goodwill impairment ₹319 Mn | [1] p.8, p.20 |
| EPS | 1.0 | n/d | — | Company shows only reported EPS | [1] p.20 |

Prior-year (FY25) reconciliation for context: Reported EBITDA 3,873 vs Adjusted 3,963 (add-backs: one-time ₹25 Mn + ESOP ₹65 Mn = +90); reported PAT to owners 444 had no exceptional add-back disclosed [1, p.20].

---

#### 5. Quarterly Seasonality Table (FY25, FY26; FY24 incomplete) — revenue share of fiscal year

Three full fiscal years of quarterly *total revenue* are not available in the pool (FY24 quarterly totals are not disclosed at consolidated level — only HCG-centre revenue is shown in a Q2FY26 deck chart). Seasonality is therefore shown for the two years with complete quarterly data.

| Quarter | FY25 Rev Share | FY26 Rev Share | Avg Rev Share (FY25–FY26) | FY25 Adj. EBITDA Margin | FY26 Adj. EBITDA Margin |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Q1 | 23.6%ᵃ | 24.1%ᵃ | 23.9% | 17.7%ᵃ | 18.2%ᵃ |
| Q2 | 24.9% | 25.4% | 25.2% | 18.8% | 19.1% |
| Q3 | 25.1% | 24.9% | 25.0% | 16.5% | 17.5% |
| Q4 | 26.3% | 25.6% | 26.0% | 18.3% | 19.2% |

ᵃ Q1 derived as in Section 3.

**Seasonality flag:** No quarter exceeds 30% or falls below 20% of annual revenue. Revenue is close to evenly spread, with a mild back-half tilt (Q4 ~26%, Q1 ~24%) consistent with intra-year ramp of new beds/centres rather than a true seasonal pattern. Q3 is consistently the weakest margin quarter (state-scheme disruptions in Andhra Pradesh in Q3FY26; lower case-mix), but the swing is small (~150–230 bps vs Q2/Q4). Seasonality is therefore **not material** to the earnings setup.

---

#### 6. Key Trend Summary

- **Revenue: Stable-to-decelerating.** Top-line has compounded ~16% over FY23–FY26 (FY26 +14.5%), but the within-year run-rate is cooling — FY26 quarterly YoY growth stepped from +16.6% (Q1) to +11.5% (Q4) [1, p.6; 3; 5]. Growth is volume-led (~12% FY26 volume, ~3% ARPP), broad-based across South/West/East clusters, with the small International (Kenya) business growing fastest (+71% FY26) off a low base [1, p.6, p.9].
- **Margins: Expanding at the EBITDA line, compressing at gross.** Reported EBITDA margin rose to 18.3% in FY26 from 17.2% in FY24 (+88 bps YoY in FY26), driven by operating leverage on employee and other costs as centres mature [1, p.5, p.20]. Gross margin slipped from ~75% (FY23/FY24) to 72.8% (FY26) as consumables/COGS rose as a share of revenue — the EBITDA gain comes from below the gross line, not from pricing.
- **Seasonality: Immaterial.** Each quarter is ~24–26% of annual revenue; Q4 is the largest at ~26% and Q3 the softest on margin. No quarter breaches the 30%/20% thresholds.
- **Inflection point — earnings quality, not revenue.** The clearest break in the last 3–5 years is the divergence between operating performance and bottom line: revenue, EBITDA and CFO all rose steadily, yet PAT to owners fell from ₹482 Mn (FY24) to ₹444 Mn (FY25) to ₹138 Mn reported (FY26), and FCF dropped to ₹586 Mn as capex climbed to ₹2,885 Mn. The cause is the acquisition/expansion phase (Nagpur, Vizag, North Bangalore): depreciation rose to ₹2,442 Mn and finance costs to ₹1,766 Mn in FY26, and FY26 carried two exceptional charges (₹319 Mn goodwill impairment on the fertility business, ₹127 Mn labour-code provision) [1, p.20; 2, p.313-314]. A ₹4,250 Mn rights issue in Q4 FY26 cut company-defined net debt to ₹3,387 Mn (0.73x reported EBITDA) from ₹6,317 Mn, and the non-core Milann fertility business is being divested (close expected Q1 FY27) [1, p.7, p.15].

---

[2] HCG Integrated Annual Report FY2024-25 (audited) — file `52337fa7-...pdf`: Consolidated Balance Sheet p.312, Consolidated Statement of Profit & Loss p.313, Consolidated Cash Flow p.314-315.
