Breadth for **Revenue and EBITDA** at analyst level is not broken out in the Revisions export (it tabulates EPS only); only aggregate estimate levels are available for those lines — *revision breadth for revenue/EBITDA not available; see Section 4 for aggregate-level direction.*

Broker-level detail (post-results, dated 2026-05-21/22) confirms a mixed but net-negative tilt on the bottom line: Anand Rathi cut FY27 EPS Normalized ₹9.9→₹8.0 and ICICI Securities ₹9.1→₹8.2 (downward), while SMIFS raised ₹7.2→₹9.4, Prabhudas Lilladher ₹9.5→₹9.9, and ICICIdirect.com initiated at ₹6.1 [CIQ Recent Changes, R21, R72, R57, R33, R108]. Notably, target prices were mostly **raised** even as FY27 EPS was cut (Anand Rathi ₹700→₹750; ICICIdirect.com ₹600→₹780; ICICI Securities ₹700→₹750; SMIFS ₹700→₹726) — a TP-up / near-term-EPS-down split that points to the cut being a base-year reset rather than a deterioration in the long-term thesis [CIQ Recent Changes, R19, R112, R78, R55].

---

#### 6. Historical Beat / Miss Pattern

CIQ surprise data for HCG is on a **fiscal-year basis only** — there is no quarter-by-quarter consensus-surprise series in the pool (HCG is an Indian SEBI-LODR filer; the export tabulates annual actual-vs-estimate). A quarterly Q-4→Q-1 beat/miss table therefore **cannot be built**; the fiscal-year record is shown instead. "Beat/Miss" = Actual − consensus Estimate at the results date.

| Fiscal Year (results date) | Revenue Beat/Miss | EPS (Normalized) Beat/Miss | EPS Norm Magnitude | Notes |
|---|---|---|---:|---|
| FY23 (2023-05-25) | Beat (+₹24.9 Mn, +0.15%) | Miss | −₹0.50 (−19.2%) | Actual EPS-N ₹2.10 vs est ₹2.60 |
| FY24 (2024-05-29) | Miss (−₹114.6 Mn, −0.60%) | Beat | +₹0.47 (+16.9%) | Actual EPS-N ₹3.26 vs est ₹2.79 |
| FY25 (2025-05-24) | Miss (−₹243.4 Mn, −1.08%) | Miss | −₹1.02 (−24.5%) | Actual EPS-N ₹3.14 vs est ₹4.16 |
| FY26 (2026-05-19) | Miss (−₹19.2 Mn, −0.08%) | Miss | −₹0.35 (−8.9%) | Actual EPS-N ₹3.59 vs est ₹3.94 |

EBITDA surprises across the same window were small and two-sided (FY24 +0.96%, FY25 −1.52%, FY26 +0.60%) [CIQ Surprise, R47–R52]. The signal: HCG lands **revenue and EBITDA within ~1.5% of consensus**, but has **missed normalized-EPS consensus in three of the last four fiscal years** (only FY24 beat). The misses sit below the operating line — i.e., the Street has repeatedly modeled the bottom line (D&A, finance cost, tax, exceptionals) too optimistically, which is exactly where current FY27 estimates are again being cut.

---

#### 7. Bar Assessment

**Bar is fair — with a downward revision tilt on the bottom line.**

Rationale: (1) On the lines management actually guides, the bar is not soft. Consensus FY27 revenue (+14.3%) sits at/just below the bottom of the guided 15–20% range, and consensus is pricing a slightly *higher* FY27 EBITDA-margin expansion (~+141 bps) than the "~100 bps" management called "achievable" — so revenue is roughly in line and EBITDA is, if anything, marginally demanding, not lowballed. (2) The bottom-line bar, by contrast, has been cut hard and recently — FY27 EPS Normalized −19% over six months and −7.5% in the last month, net revision breadth −1 (3-month) worsening to −2 (1-month), with the cut concentrated below EBITDA (D&A / FY26 base reset) rather than in operating margin. (3) HCG has missed normalized-EPS consensus in three of the last four fiscal years while landing revenue/EBITDA within ~1.5% — a structural tendency for the Street to over-model the bottom line. The freshly-lowered EPS bar offsets that history somewhat, but the revenue/EBITDA bars are full and revision momentum is still negative, so this is neither a soft "beat-me" setup (not Low) nor an over-extended one (not High). Net: a fair bar where the most likely friction point is again below EBITDA (depreciation, finance cost, tax, exceptionals), not the top line.

Single most important watch-item for the FQ1 2027 print (release Jul-30-2026): whether revenue re-accelerates back toward the ~15% guided pace after the Q4 FY26 dip (low-margin paring + Middle East medical-value-travel softness, which management said normalizes "over the next couple of quarters"), and whether D&A/finance cost land where the cut estimates now sit.

---

##### Self-check
- Section 2 captures all guidance HCG provided (revenue, EBITDA margin/growth, ROCE, net-debt ceiling, beds, day-care centres, interest cost); EPS and ₹-capex explicitly flagged as not guided.
- Gap calc = Consensus − implied guidance level; signs verified.
- Revision momentum uses actual CIQ Trends data points; normalized-EPS quarterly cells and revenue/EBITDA analyst-level breadth marked N/A where the export does not provide them.
- Bar assessment is exactly one of {Low, Fair, High, Unknown} = **Fair**.
- Consensus data present in full → no partial-data cap applied (consensus setup score is NOT capped at 30; revision history present, so the no-revision-history cap of 60 also does not apply).
- Revenue range midpoint computed (15–20% → 17.5%); consensus compared to both the +15% point and the midpoint.
- Analyst count (9) and data-as-of date (2026-06-01; revisions 2026-05-21/22) shown in the metadata table.
- Quarterly beat/miss limitation stated explicitly (fiscal-year surprise only).
- No banned phrases used ("broadly in line" / "comfortable with estimates" avoided; "well-positioned" appears only inside a directly-quoted management transcript line, not as the agent's own claim).

---

## EARN-05 · beat-miss-setup — Beat / Miss Setup — HCG

### Beat / Miss Setup — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787) — pan-India oncology-focused hospital chain. INR (₹), consolidated. FY ends 31 March; FQ1 2027 = quarter ending 30 Jun 2026, results release scheduled Jul-30-2026 [04, §1, CIQ Estimates header]. This agent looks forward and decides the most likely path to a beat or a miss; it does not value the stock, rate it, build forecasts, or run sensitivities (those are other agents). All 4 upstream outputs (01 historical-financials, 02 revenue-drivers, 03 margin-drivers, 04 guidance-consensus) were read and inform this report.*

> **Data note:** Consensus IS available (CIQ Estimates, 9 analysts, data as of 2026-06-01) [04, §1], so the partial-data cap to "Unclear" does NOT apply. The binding limitation is that HCG's surprise history is fiscal-year only — there is no quarter-by-quarter consensus-surprise series, and FQ1 2027 quarter-level consensus exists only for revenue / EBITDA / GAAP EPS, not normalized EPS [04, §4, §6]. So the quarterly beat/miss read leans on FY-level pattern plus quarter-level revenue/EBITDA/GAAP-EPS estimates.

---

#### 1. Next Quarter Context

The next print is **FQ1 2027 (quarter ending 30 Jun 2026), released Jul-30-2026** [04, §1]. Seasonality is immaterial for HCG — every quarter is ~24–26% of annual revenue, with Q1 the *smallest* at ~24% and a mild back-half tilt; no quarter breaches the 30%/20% thresholds [01, §5]. The FQ1 2027 quarter-level bar is **revenue ₹7,340.9 Mn (≈ +18% YoY on Q1FY26's ~₹6,131 Mn derived base; see note below), EBITDA ₹1,345.7 Mn, GAAP EPS ₹2.68** [04, §4]; the full-FY27 bar is revenue ₹29,105.9 Mn (+14.3% YoY), reported EBITDA ₹5,737.3 Mn (~19.7% margin, +141 bps), normalized EPS ₹8.72 [04, §3, §4]. On the lines management guides, the bar is "fair" — revenue sits at/just below the bottom of the guided 15–20% range and the margin bar (+141 bps) is marginally *above* the "~100 bps" management called achievable; the bottom line (EPS) has been cut hard and recently (FY27 normalized EPS −19% over six months) [04, §4, §7].

> **Reconciliation flag on the FQ1 revenue bar:** The CIQ FQ1 2027 revenue estimate of ₹7,340.9 Mn implies ~+20% YoY on the derived Q1FY26 revenue of ₹6,131 Mn [01, §3], well above HCG's ~14–15% group run-rate and the +11.5% Q4FY26 exit rate [01, §3]. Two readings are possible: (a) the quarter-level estimate is stale/optimistic relative to the FY27 total (FY27 revenue was cut −1.7% over 90 days while the FQ1 line stayed flat) [04, §4], or (b) it reflects a sharp re-acceleration. The conservative read, consistent with the FY-level cut and the Q4 exit rate, is that **the FQ1 revenue bar looks demanding** unless re-acceleration is genuine. This tension is the single most important feature of the near-term setup and is carried into Sections 2, 3, and 8.

---

#### 2. Beat Scenarios

Likelihood mapped to numeric bands per CLAUDE.md §10 (High = Likely, 60–75%; Mid = Unlikely-to-toss-up, 35–55%; Low = Very unlikely, 10–25%). Each scenario connects to a named driver from 02 or 03.

| Scenario | Driver | What Would Need To Happen | Likelihood | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B1. Volume re-accelerates as paring/MVT normalize** | IP volume via utilisation — the single biggest revenue driver [02, §7] | Q4FY26's two self-inflicted/exogenous drags (deliberate low-margin paring; soft Middle East MVT) reverse on management's "normalize over the next couple of quarters" timetable, pushing group YoY revenue growth back from the +11.5% Q4 exit rate toward ~15%; West cluster (45% of revenue, 50% utilisation) keeps adding volume on the built cost base | **Mid (40–50%)** | Q4 growth fell to +11.5% on paring + MVT, both flagged to normalize [02, §3, §7; 01, §3]; West utilisation 50% with headroom [02, §4] |
| **B2. EBITDA margin beats via operating leverage** | Operating leverage / centre-maturity (ramping ₹50–100 Mn cohort = 50% of revenue, +17% LFL) [03, §5, §8] | Employee-cost and other-expense leverage (which drove +79 and +87 bps in FY26) continues on a near-flat ~2,605-bed base while COGS share holds, so the EBITDA-margin gain runs at or above the +100 bps management called achievable; on a small revenue base this can clear the EBITDA line even if revenue is light | **Mid (40–50%)** | FY26 +88 bps reported EBITDA margin from employee/other-expense leverage [03, §7]; ramping cohort migration is the live tailwind [03, §5] |
| **B3. GAAP-EPS beats on lower interest + no fresh exceptional** | Finance cost / EBIT→PAT bridge [03, §2, §4] | The ₹4,250 Mn Q4FY26 rights issue cut net debt to ₹3,387 Mn (0.73x); a clean FQ1 with falling interest cost (management: "some reduction in interest cost"; FY27 interest estimates already improved to −₹1,517.8 Mn) and NO new goodwill/labour-code exceptional lets reported PAT/EPS clear a bar set off a depressed FY26 base (FY26 EPS GAAP ₹0.96) | **Mid (35–45%)** | Rights-issue deleveraging [01, §2, §6]; interest estimate improved [04, §4]; FY26 base depressed by ₹319 Mn + ₹127 Mn exceptionals [01, §4] |
| **B4. Milann divestment closes clean in Q1 FY27** | M&A / mix clean-up [02, §1; 03, §5] | The signed Milann fertility divestment (close expected Q1 FY27) closes on time; removing the impaired, ~1.5%-EBITDA-margin-dilutive line is marginally accretive to group margin and removes a PAT drag — could surface as a one-time gain/loss or cleaner go-forward mix | **Low (20–30%)** | Signed May 2026, close expected Q1 FY27 [02, §1; 03, §5]; magnitude small (~2.4% of revenue) |

---

#### 3. Miss Scenarios

| Scenario | Driver | What Would Need To Happen | Likelihood | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **M1. Revenue misses the demanding FQ1 bar** | IP volume / re-acceleration vs the ₹7,340.9 Mn quarter bar [04, §4; 02, §7] | The FQ1 revenue estimate implies ~+20% YoY (vs +11.5% Q4 exit and ~14–15% run-rate); if paring/MVT normalization lags by a quarter, or the FQ1 estimate is simply stale-high, revenue lands ~₹6,900–7,200 Mn — a top-line miss vs the quarter bar even if YoY growth is a respectable ~13–15% | **Mid–High (50–60%)** | FQ1 estimate ₹7,340.9 Mn implies ~+20% vs +11.5% Q4 exit [04, §4; 01, §3]; FY27 revenue cut −1.7% while FQ1 line stayed flat (stale risk) [04, §4] |
| **M2. EPS misses below the EBITDA line (the structural pattern)** | D&A step-up + finance cost + tax/exceptionals — the EBIT→PAT bridge [03, §2, §4; 04, §6] | D&A keeps stepping up on Nagpur/Vizag/North Bangalore (FY26 D&A ₹2,442 Mn), tax/finance land worse than the freshly-cut estimates, or a new exceptional appears — repeating the pattern where HCG misses normalized-EPS consensus while revenue/EBITDA land within ~1.5% | **High (60–70%)** | Missed normalized-EPS consensus in 3 of last 4 FYs (only FY24 beat); cut concentrated below EBITDA [04, §6; 03, §4] |
| **M3. COGS/case-mix drag deepens, EBITDA margin misses** | Drugs & consumables cost (COGS) — the single biggest margin driver, a headwind [03, §8] | Consumable inflation or a further shift toward drug-heavy medical oncology (38% of revenue) pushes COGS share above FY26's 27.2%, outrunning the operating-leverage tailwind, so EBITDA margin falls short of the +141 bps consensus prices | **Mid (40–50%)** | COGS rose to 27.2% (FY26) from 26.1% (FY25), 108 bps gross drag; DPCO-capped, slow pass-through [03, §3, §8] |
| **M4. Government-scheme disruption hits revenue/ARPP** | Government-scheme tariffs/volume (~33% of revenue, exogenous) [02, §4; 03, §5] | A scheme repricing or payment-cycle disruption (as Andhra Pradesh did in Q3FY26; Odisha cut one cluster's ARPP −3% in Q3FY26) recurs in a scheme-heavy cluster during the quarter, denting volume or ARPP on one-third of revenue | **Low–Mid (25–35%)** | AP scheme disruption already hit Q3FY26 [01, §5; 02, §4]; scheme economics outside HCG's control [02, §4]. Note: AP Aarogyasri family cover was raised to ₹25 lakh per [Web: policyx.com, 2026 (indicative, unverified)] — directionally supportive, not a dated FY27 tariff cut |

---

#### 4. What Magnitude Matters?

Consensus IS available, so thresholds are defined against it. "Material" thresholds are set relative to HCG's own surprise history — it lands revenue/EBITDA within ~1.5% of consensus, so a revenue/EBITDA surprise only matters beyond roughly that band; EPS is more volatile (FY misses of −9% to −24%) so the EPS material band is wider [04, §6].

| Metric | Consensus / Bar | Material Beat Threshold | Material Miss Threshold | Why |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Revenue (FQ1 2027) | ₹7,340.9 Mn | > ₹7,490 Mn (>+2%) | < ₹7,190 Mn (<−2%) | HCG lands revenue within ~1.5% historically [04, §6]; the FQ1 bar already implies ~+20% YoY, so a sub-bar print near +14–15% YoY (~₹7,000–7,050 Mn) is a >−2% miss yet still healthy growth — magnitude vs the bar, not vs zero |
| Revenue (FY27) | ₹29,105.9 Mn (+14.3%) | > ₹29,690 Mn (>+2%; ~ guided +15%+) | < ₹28,520 Mn (<−2%) | A FY beat needs the +15% guided pace to hold; consensus already sits at the bottom of the guided range [04, §3] |
| EBITDA (FQ1 2027) | ₹1,345.7 Mn | > ₹1,375 Mn (>+2%) | < ₹1,315 Mn (<−2%) | EBITDA surprises have been small and two-sided (±1.5%) [04, §6]; >±2% is the material band |
| EBITDA (FY27) | ₹5,737.3 Mn (~19.7% margin, +141 bps) | Margin > ~19.9% / EBITDA > ₹5,850 Mn | Margin < ~19.0% / EBITDA < ₹5,620 Mn | Consensus already prices +141 bps vs the "~100 bps" guide [04, §3]; coming in only at the ~+100 bps guide (~19.3%) is technically a small EBITDA *miss* vs the bar |
| EPS — GAAP (FQ1 2027) | ₹2.68 | > ₹2.95 (>+10%) | < ₹2.40 (<−10%) | EPS is where HCG misses (3 of 4 FYs, −9% to −24%); ±10% is the material band given that volatility [04, §6] |
| EPS — Normalized (FY27) | ₹8.72 | > ₹9.20 (>+5–6%) | < ₹8.20 (<−6%) | EPS-N already cut −19% over 6 months; a fresh sub-₹8.20 print continues the down-revision pattern [04, §4, §6] |
| Guidance (qualitative) | "~15%" revenue sustained; "~100 bps" margin "achievable"; ROCE 20%+ over 5 yrs [04, §2] | Re-affirm or raise the 15% pace / +100 bps margin path; reaffirm 200+ beds, day-care 8–10 adds | Walk back the 15% pace, soften the +100 bps margin path, or push paring/MVT normalization beyond "a couple of quarters" | HCG gives no point guidance, so the guidance "surprise" is directional reaffirmation vs walk-back — and given the in-line-print-bad-guide risk (Section 5), a soft directional guide can matter more than the FQ1 numbers |

---

#### 5. In-Line Print But Bad Guidance Risk

| Risk | Evidence | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **In-line current quarter but guide-down** | Q4FY26 already dipped to +11.5% on paring + MVT, with normalization promised "over the next couple of quarters"; if FQ1 lands in-line but management extends that timeline or trims the ~15% framing, the forward bar resets down [02, §3, §7; 04, §2] | HCG guides only qualitatively; the market is anchored on the "15% / +100 bps" path [04, §2, §3]. A directional walk-back on the call is the most potent negative even if FQ1 numbers are fine — and the revenue estimate trend is already down (FY27 −1.7% / 90d) [04, §4] |
| **Beat current quarter but weak margin guide** | COGS is the structural headwind (27.2% of revenue, DPCO-capped, slow pass-through) [03, §3, §8]; consensus already prices +141 bps margin vs the ~100 bps guide [04, §3] | A revenue beat that comes with COGS-led gross-margin pressure, or management framing margin expansion as nearer +100 bps than +141 bps, would undercut the EBITDA-margin bar the Street has set above guidance |
| **Beat EPS due to one-offs, miss quality** | Milann divestment (close expected Q1 FY27) could produce a one-time gain; FY26 already carried two exceptionals (₹319 Mn impairment, ₹127 Mn labour-code) [01, §4; 02, §1; 03, §5] | A headline EPS beat driven by a divestment gain or the *absence* of a repeat exceptional is low-quality vs operating EPS; the EBIT→PAT bridge (D&A, finance, tax) is where the real read sits [03, §4]. Earnings-quality judgment is owned by agent 06 — flagged here only as a beat/miss-composition risk |
| **Beat revenue but working capital deteriorates** | FY26 FCF fell to ₹586 Mn (from ₹1,082 Mn) as capex climbed to ₹2,885 Mn; working capital rose to ₹1,295 Mn; ~33% of revenue is government-scheme (7-day-stated but historically lumpy settlement) [01, §1, §2; 02, §4] | A revenue beat carried by scheme volume can lengthen receivables and weaken cash conversion; capex is still in build-out mode (North Bangalore, 200+ beds), so a top-line beat does not automatically mean a cash beat |

---

#### 6. Seasonality Read

Seasonality neither helps nor hurts the FQ1 2027 setup in any material way. Per the seasonality table in 01, each quarter is ~24–26% of annual revenue with no quarter breaching the 30%/20% thresholds; Q1 is the *smallest* quarter at ~24% (FY25 23.6%, FY26 24.1%) and Q3 is the softest on margin [01, §5]. The mild back-half tilt reflects intra-year bed/centre ramp rather than a true demand season, and Q1's Adjusted EBITDA margin has actually been mid-pack (17.7% FY25, 18.2% FY26) [01, §5]. The one seasonal nuance that could matter is that FQ1 has historically been a sequentially softer revenue quarter than the Q4 it follows (Q1FY26 ~₹6,131 Mn derived vs Q4FY25 ₹5,852 Mn — actually up YoY but the year's lowest share) [01, §3, §5], which makes the ~+20%-YoY-implied FQ1 consensus bar (Section 1) look even more demanding against a seasonally smaller base. Net: seasonality is **not a driver of the verdict**; it marginally amplifies the revenue-bar tension rather than creating it.

---

#### 7. Historical Pattern

HCG has a clear, asymmetric pattern that the synthesizer should weight heavily: it **lands revenue and EBITDA within ~1.5% of consensus but misses normalized-EPS consensus in three of the last four fiscal years** (FY23 −19.2%, FY25 −24.5%, FY26 −8.9%; only FY24 beat, +16.9%) [04, §6]. The misses sit consistently *below* the operating line — the Street repeatedly over-models the bottom line (D&A, finance cost, tax, exceptionals), which is exactly where FY27 EPS estimates are again being cut (normalized EPS −19% over six months, net revision breadth −1 worsening to −2) [04, §4, §5, §6]. The important caveat is that this is a **fiscal-year** pattern; there is no quarter-by-quarter surprise series in the pool [04, §6], so it transfers to the FQ1 read directionally, not mechanically. The synthesizer should treat "operating line in-line, bottom line at risk" as the **base-rate prior** for HCG and require specific evidence to override it. The partial offset: the EPS bar has already been cut hard and recently, so some of the historical over-modeling has been worked out of the current number [04, §7].

---

#### 8. Setup Verdict

**Setup is balanced — with the beat/miss skew differing by line: revenue/EBITDA modestly miss-tilted, bottom-line EPS miss-tilted, but the EPS bar is freshly reset lower.**

The **single most important factor** is that on the lines that matter, the bar is full, not soft: the FQ1 revenue estimate (₹7,340.9 Mn) implies ~+20% YoY against a +11.5% Q4 exit rate and the FY27 EBITDA-margin bar (+141 bps) sits above management's own "~100 bps achievable" framing [04, §3, §4; 01, §3] — so HCG has to *re-accelerate and out-margin its own guide* simply to meet consensus, which is a miss-tilted starting point rather than a beat-me setup. The **single biggest risk that could flip the verdict toward "favors beat"** is the normalization of Q4's two transient drags (deliberate low-margin paring + soft Middle East MVT) arriving on management's "next couple of quarters" timetable [02, §3, §7]: if volume snaps back toward 15% while the freshly-lowered EPS bar is cleared on rights-issue-driven lower interest and a clean (no new exceptional) quarter, the setup tips to a beat. Conversely, the biggest risk toward "favors miss" is the recurring below-EBITDA EPS pattern (M2) combined with a stale-high FQ1 revenue bar (M1).

---

#### 9. Second-Quarter Look-Ahead

The setup for FQ2 2027 (quarter ending 30 Sep 2026, the quarter *after* next) looks **modestly better than FQ1** on three counts: (1) by FQ2 the Q4FY26 paring/MVT drags should be fully lapped on management's own "couple of quarters" timetable, removing the re-acceleration uncertainty that makes the FQ1 bar demanding [02, §3, §7]; (2) FQ2 is seasonally HCG's strongest *margin* quarter (Q2 Adjusted EBITDA margin 18.8% FY25, 19.1% FY26 — the year's high) [01, §5]; and (3) the Kolkata clinician onboarding (expected Q1 FY27) and Milann close should both be in the base by FQ2, lifting East case-mix/ARPP and cleaning group mix [02, §5; 03, §5]. The offsetting unknown is that FQ2 quarter-level consensus is not separately tabulated in the pool the way FQ1 is, so the *bar* for FQ2 cannot yet be assessed [04, §4] — the operating tailwind is visible, the consensus hurdle is not.

---

#### 10. Pre-Mortem

If this setup read fails, the most likely reason is a **base-rate / quarter-transfer error**: treating HCG's fiscal-year "operating-line-fine, EPS-miss" pattern and the FY-level "fair bar" as if they map cleanly onto a single quarter when the actual FQ1 swing factor was something the FY frame masks — most plausibly (a) the FQ1 revenue estimate being a stale artifact that either snapped down (handing an easy "miss vs bar" that means nothing) or that consensus quietly re-based before the print, or (b) a government-scheme payment/tariff event in a scheme-heavy cluster (the AP/Odisha-type disruption that already hit Q3FY26) landing inside the quarter and moving one-third of revenue in a way no operating driver predicted [04, §4, §6; 02, §4; 01, §5]. The secondary failure mode is a guidance-driven reaction (Section 5) overwhelming an otherwise in-line print, which a numbers-focused beat/miss frame would under-weight.

---

##### Self-check
- All 4 upstream outputs (01, 02, 03, 04) were read and are cited throughout.
- Every beat/miss scenario connects to a specific driver from 02 or 03 (IP volume/utilisation, operating leverage, COGS, government scheme, finance-cost bridge, Milann) — none invented.
- Likelihoods carry numeric bands (High 60–75%, Mid 35–55%, Low 10–25%) per CLAUDE.md §10 and are tied to cited evidence.
- Setup verdict is exactly one of {Favors beat / Favors miss / Balanced / Unclear} = **Balanced**.
- Consensus data IS present → verdict is NOT capped at "Unclear"; the FY-only surprise limitation is flagged instead [04, §6].
- Beat/miss thresholds (Section 4) are linked to consensus and calibrated to HCG's own ~1.5% revenue/EBITDA and wider EPS surprise history.
- Setup does not rely only on next-quarter EPS — revenue, EBITDA margin, guidance and earnings composition are all considered (Sections 2–5).
- Section 5 explicitly handles the in-line-print / bad-guidance case (guide-down, weak margin guide, one-off EPS, working-capital).
- Section 8 names ONE most important factor (full bar requiring re-acceleration + out-margining the guide), not a list.
- Banned phrases avoided; quoted management language ("on track" / "well positioned" etc.) does not appear as the agent's own claim.

---

## EARN-06 · earnings-quality — Earnings Quality — HCG

### Earnings Quality — HCG

**Company:** HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG) — pan-India oncology hospital chain.
**Reporting currency:** INR million (₹ Mn), consolidated, post-Ind AS 116 (leases capitalised). FY ends 31 March (FY26 = year ended 31 Mar 2026).
**Basis note:** Two EBITDA constructs are kept separate throughout. **Reported EBITDA** = audited statutory operating result, pre other-income. **Adjusted EBITDA** = company-defined (Reported EBITDA + ESOP + one-time cost). FCF is defined per CLAUDE.md as **CFO − total capex**; the company does not publish its own FCF figure. Cash-flow capex = "Acquisition of PP&E and others" line in the audited investing section (FY23–FY25) and the deck capex figure for FY26.
**Data status:** Cash flow statement is **available and audited** for FY23, FY24, FY25 [2, p.314; 6, p.288]. FY26 cash flow / balance sheet / P&L are from the **board-approved audited results presentation dated 19 May 2026** [1, p.20-22], not a full statutory statement (standalone audited FY26 PDF not in pool). No partial-data cap applies — the cash conversion and EBITDA-to-FCF bridge are fully assessable. FY22 detail below the EBITDA line is not in the pool, so the bridge runs FY23–FY26 (four years).

---

#### 1. EBITDA → CFO → FCF Bridge (4 years)

All figures ₹ Mn, consolidated. EBITDA = **Reported EBITDA** (statutory, pre other-income). Working-capital change, tax paid and other operating items are taken directly from the audited cash flow statement. FY26 working-capital change is the single deck line (component split not in the deck).

| Item | FY23 | FY24 | FY25 | FY26 | Trend |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Reported EBITDA | 2,987 | 3,296 | 3,873 | 4,658 | Improving |
| Working capital change | −459 | −272 | −765 | −426 | Volatile (cash drain every year) |
| Tax paid | −227 | −474 | −318 | −318 | Stable |
| Interest paid (in finance/lease lines, non-CFO) | n/a¹ | n/a¹ | n/a¹ | n/a¹ | — |
| Other operating items (D&A add-back, prov., FX, ESOP, impairment add-backs net of EBITDA) | +216² | +296² | +382² | +307² | Stable |
| **CFO** | **2,516** | **2,846** | **3,171** | **3,471** | Improving |
| Maintenance capex | n/d | n/d | n/d | 1,189 | Disclosed FY26 only |
| Growth capex | n/d | n/d | n/d | 1,696 | Disclosed FY26 only |
| Total capex (PP&E acquisition) | 1,332 | 1,857 | 2,089 | 2,885 | Rising fast |
| **FCF (CFO − total capex)** | **1,184** | **989** | **1,082** | **586** | Deteriorating |
| **CFO / Reported EBITDA %** | **84.2%** | **86.3%** | **81.9%** | **74.5%** | Deteriorating but >70% |

¹ HCG reports interest within financing (finance cost paid ₹761.6 Mn and interest on lease liability ₹819.95 Mn in FY25) and lease interest in CFI/CFF, not as a CFO line — so it does not net against EBITDA inside CFO. Stated here to avoid implying it is missing [2, p.314-315].
² "Other operating items" is the residual that bridges (EBITDA + WC change + tax paid) to CFO. It is dominated by the timing of provisions, ESOP add-back, government-grant amortisation, loss-allowance on receivables and FX — i.e. the non-cash add-backs above EBITDA are already inside Reported EBITDA, so this residual is small and positive. Bridge reconciles exactly to audited CFO for each year (verified) [2, p.314; 6, p.288].

**Capex split disclosure:** Management discloses a maintenance vs growth split **for FY26 only** (maintenance ₹1,189 Mn, growth ₹1,696 Mn) [1, "Net Debt (Pre Ind-AS)" slide]. FY23–FY25 are not split. *Capex split not disclosed for FY23–FY25 — total capex used. Reported FCF understates true recurring free cash flow.* On the FY26 split, FCF on maintenance capex alone would be ₹3,471 − ₹1,189 = **₹2,282 Mn**; the ₹586 Mn headline FCF is depressed by ₹1,696 Mn of discretionary growth capex (North Bangalore, Vizag, Ahmedabad COE, Kenya), not by weak cash generation [1, p.14-15].

Spot-checks: FY26 FCF 3,471 − 2,885 = **586** ✓ (matches upstream). FY25 CFO/EBITDA 3,171.1 / 3,872.8 = **81.9%** ✓. FY26 CFO/EBITDA 3,470.9 / 4,657.9 = **74.5%** ✓.

---

#### 2. Cash Conversion Assessment

CFO tracks EBITDA closely and has done so for four straight years: CFO/Reported EBITDA was 84.2% (FY23), 86.3% (FY24), 81.9% (FY25) and 74.5% (FY26) — every year comfortably above the 70% healthy threshold and never near the 50% red-flag line [2, p.314; 6, p.288; 1, p.22]. The trajectory is **down**, from 84–86% to 74.5%, because working-capital absorbed cash every single year (−₹272 Mn to −₹765 Mn) while EBITDA grew faster than cash collection in FY26 (EBITDA +20.3% vs CFO +9.5%). Management's headline "~75% EBITDA to CFO" claim [1, "Net Debt (Pre Ind-AS)" slide] is verified against Reported EBITDA (74.5%) and is, if anything, slightly conservative versus Adjusted EBITDA (73.7%). Verdict: **operating earnings are cash-backed**, but the conversion rate is eroding as the business scales receivables and inventory ahead of revenue.

---

#### 3. Working Capital Trends

Days computed on year-end balances ÷ annual flow × 365. DSO on total revenue; DIO and DPO on COGS (purchases + change in inventories). Balances are audited (FY24/FY25) [2, p.312; 6, p.286] and from the FY26 results deck balance sheet [1, p.20]. Note: HCG's trade payables are unusually high relative to COGS because medical-consultancy charges and other operating payables sit partly in "other current/financial liabilities," so the **absolute DPO level is inflated by definition**; the *direction* is the signal.

| Metric | FY24 | FY25 | FY26 | Direction | Risk |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Receivable days (DSO) | 56.1 | 65.8 | 60.3 | Up then partial reversal | **Flagged in FY25** (see below) |
| Inventory days (DIO) | 32.8 | 33.3 | 31.5 | Roughly flat | Low |
| Payable days (DPO) | 215.8 | 210.1 | 185.1 | Falling | Medium — cash being used to pay suppliers faster |
| Cash conversion cycle (DSO + DIO − DPO) | −126.9 | −111.0 | −93.2 | Less negative | Working capital becoming a larger cash user |

Flag tests:
- **DSO rising >10% YoY — TRIGGERED in FY25.** DSO rose from 56.1 to 65.8 days (+17.3%), and trade receivables grew **+36.3%** (₹2,940 → ₹4,009 Mn) against revenue growth of **+16.3%** [2, p.312, p.314]. Receivables grew more than 2x revenue in FY25. This is the single clearest accrual concern in the file. In FY26 it partially unwound — DSO fell to 60.3, receivables grew only +4.9% vs revenue +14.5% [1, p.20]. The most likely driver is government-scheme / insurance / corporate-billing receivables (oncology has long state-scheme collection cycles; HCG flagged Andhra Pradesh state-scheme disruption in Q3FY26) rather than channel-type revenue pull-forward — but the FY25 spike is not fully explained in the pool. *Inference, not from filings: the FY25 build looks collection-cycle driven, not fictitious revenue, because it reversed and CFO still grew; this is not proven.*
- **DIO rising >15% YoY — not triggered.** Inventory days are flat (~32–33). Inventory grew +24.3% in FY25, but off COGS that grew +22.1% — broadly matched. No channel-stuffing signal (hospital consumables, not finished-goods inventory).
- **DPO rising sharply — not triggered; opposite is true.** DPO is *falling* (216 → 185 days). HCG is paying suppliers faster, which uses cash. This is a quality positive (not stretching suppliers) but it is the main reason the cash conversion cycle is becoming less negative and CFO is lagging EBITDA.

---

#### 4. Non-GAAP Adjustments

Source for all rows: company's own reconciliation [1, p.8, p.20]. HCG adds back ESOP cost and "one-time cost" to reach Adjusted EBITDA, and adds back two exceptional items to reach Adjusted PAT.

| Adjustment | Amount (FY26) | Recurring? (Y/N) | Concern Level | Evidence |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| ESOP cost added back to EBITDA | +14.4 | **Y** (₹65.4 FY25, ₹78.5 FY24 — every year) | **Mid** — SBC is a real cost; excluding it flatters "adjusted" margin, but the FY26 amount is tiny (0.06% of revenue) | [1, p.20]; [2, p.314] |
| One-time cost added back to EBITDA | +39.0 | Partly (₹25.0 FY25, ₹78.5-type items prior) | Low — small, recurs but immaterial (<0.2% of EBITDA) | [1, p.20] |
| Goodwill impairment (fertility/Milann) added back to PAT | +319.1 | N (one-off, tied to Milann divestment) | Low — genuine non-cash one-off ahead of disposal | [1, p.8, p.20] |
| Labour-code provision added back to PAT (post-tax ~₹100) | +127.0 pre-tax | N (one-off regulatory change) | Low–Mid — one-off, but the cash will eventually be paid as gratuity/PF under the new code | [1, p.20]; [5, p.14] |

Flag tests:
- **Recurs every period:** ESOP add-back recurs every year (₹78.5 → ₹65.4 → ₹14.4 across FY24–FY26) — it is **not a true one-off** and should not be excluded from a "clean" earnings view. The amount is now small.
- **Exceeds 15% of GAAP earnings:** The PAT-level add-backs (₹446 Mn pre-tax) dwarf reported PAT (see Section 7) — but they are **EBITDA-level adjustments that are trivial** (₹53 Mn, +1.15% of reported EBITDA). The large gap is entirely at the PAT line, from two disclosed exceptionals, not from EBITDA gaming.
- **SBC excluded from adjusted numbers:** YES — ESOP is added back to Adjusted EBITDA. Flagged. Immaterial in FY26 but a recurring-cost exclusion in principle.

---

#### 5. One-Off Items (last 3 years)

| Item | Period | Amount | Classification | Evidence |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| Goodwill impairment — fertility (Milann) business | Q4 FY26 | 319.1 | **Genuine** — non-cash, taken ahead of announced Milann divestment (close expected Q1 FY27) | [1, p.7, p.20] |
| Labour-code provision (new code gratuity/PF impact) | Q3 FY26 | 127.0 (post-tax ~100) | **Genuine** one-off (regulatory), but cash outflow is deferred not avoided | [5, p.14]; [1, p.20] |
| Exceptional net gain — Gurugram project write-back | FY24 | +39.05 (gain) | **Genuine** — abandoned greenfield project liability written back (one-off income, flatters FY24 PBT by ~6%) | [6, p.287, Note 31]; [6, p.304 (i)] |
| Loss allowance on trade receivables (P&L charge) | FY24 / FY25 | 225.6 / 107.0 | **Recurring "one-off"** — a credit-loss charge appears most years (FY23 was a +154.1 reversal); not adjusted out, but a recurring earnings-quality drag | [2, p.314]; [6, p.288] |
| Trade receivable written off | FY25 | 97.3 | **Recurring** — write-offs each year (₹13.2 FY23, ₹4.3 FY24, ₹97.3 FY25); rising in FY25, consistent with the FY25 receivable build | [2, p.314] |

The two FY26 PAT exceptionals are genuine and well-disclosed. The receivable provisioning/write-off pattern is the recurring-"one-off" worth watching: combined credit-loss + write-off intensity was −0.83% of revenue (FY23 reversal), +1.20% (FY24), +0.92% (FY25) [2, p.314; 6, p.288] — a real, recurring cost that swings PAT.

---

#### 6. Accrual Quality Flags

| Flag | Triggered (Y/N) | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue growing faster than CFO for 2+ years | **Y (partial)** | Revenue +16.3% (FY25), +14.5% (FY26) vs CFO +11.4% (FY25), +9.5% (FY26). Revenue has outgrown CFO for two straight years; the gap widened in FY26. CFO still grew, so this is a conversion-erosion flag, not a divergence-into-decline. [2, p.314; 1, p.22] |
| Receivables growing faster than revenue | **Y (FY25), N (FY26)** | FY25 receivables +36.3% vs revenue +16.3% — triggered hard. FY26 receivables +4.9% vs revenue +14.5% — reversed. [2, p.312; 1, p.20] |
| Inventory growing faster than COGS | **N** | FY25 inventory +24.3% vs COGS +22.1%; FY26 inventory +12.8% vs COGS +19.2%. Broadly matched or slower. [2, p.312; 1, p.20] |
| Deferred revenue declining (subscription/contract business) | **N/A** | HCG is a hospital operator — fee-for-service / scheme billing, not a subscription/contract-liability model. No material contract-liability balance to track. Flag not applicable. |
| Capitalised costs growing as % of revenue | **N (borrowing cost), monitor (capex)** | Borrowing cost capitalised to assets is small and disclosed (₹190.65 Mn FY25 directly attributable; ₹109.96 Mn lease-related at a 10% cap rate) [2, p.330]. No evidence of operating costs being capitalised. Total capex is rising fast (₹1,332 → ₹2,885 Mn) but this is disclosed growth capex, not cost capitalisation. |
| Frequent accounting policy changes | **N (one isolated change)** | One depreciation useful-life revision effective 1 Apr 2022, disclosed with a full multi-year effect table [6, p.304]. No pattern of repeated changes. See Section 8. |

Net read: the accrual flags concentrate in **FY25 (receivable build + write-offs)** and in the **structural fact that CFO has lagged revenue growth for two years**. Neither points to fabricated revenue — CFO still rises, the FY25 receivable spike partly reversed, and the auditor (B S R & Co. LLP) issued an **unmodified opinion with no going-concern or internal-control qualification** for FY25 [2, p.311].

---

#### 7. Reported vs Adjusted Reconciliation (FY26)

HCG discloses adjusted PAT and adjusted EBITDA; it does **not** publish adjusted EBIT or adjusted EPS [1, p.20]. Source: company reconciliation [1, p.8, p.20].

| Metric | Reported (FY26) | Adjusted (FY26) | Difference | % of Reported | Recurring? | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Reported EBITDA | 4,658 | 4,711 | +53 | +1.15% | ESOP (Y) + one-time (partly) | [1, p.20] |
| EBIT | 2,466 | n/d | — | — | Company publishes no adjusted EBIT | [1, p.20] |
| Net income (PAT to owners) | 138 | 557 | +419 | **+304.7%** | Both add-backs are non-recurring one-offs | [1, p.8, p.20] |
| EPS | ₹1.0 (reported) | n/d (≈₹3.98 implied)¹ | — | — | Company publishes only reported EPS | [1, p.20] |

¹ Inference, not from filings: ₹556.8 Mn adjusted PAT ÷ ~140 Mn diluted shares ≈ ₹3.98. Company does not print an adjusted EPS. Reported diluted shares implied by FY25 (₹444.1 Mn / ₹3.14) ≈ 141.4 Mn [2, p.313].

Read: at the **EBITDA line the adjustment is immaterial (1.15%)** — clean. At the **PAT line the adjustment is enormous (+305% of reported)** because two genuine one-offs (₹319 Mn goodwill impairment + ₹127 Mn labour-code) crushed a thin reported PAT. The reconciliation is fully disclosed and the items are real, so the large gap is **transparent, not deceptive** — but it means reported FY26 PAT/EPS is not the right run-rate, and adjusted PAT is the more representative figure. The two exceptionals sum to exactly the ₹445.8 Mn "Extraordinary Items" line on the audited P&L (319.1 + 126.7 = 445.8 ✓) [1, p.20].

---

#### 8. Accounting Trap Checklist

Severity is an **inverted score** (higher = worse / more concerning), 0–100.

| Trap | Triggered? | Evidence | Severity /100 (inverted) |
|---|---|---|---:|
| Stock-based compensation excluded from adjusted earnings | **Y** | ESOP added back to Adjusted EBITDA every year (₹78.5/₹65.4/₹14.4 FY24-26). Real but now tiny (0.06% of FY26 rev). [1, p.20; 2, p.314] | 25 |
| Restructuring costs recur every year | **N** | No recurring restructuring line. FY24 had a one-off Gurugram write-back (gain, not cost). [6, p.287] | 10 |
| Capitalised costs rising faster than revenue | **N** | Capitalised borrowing cost small and disclosed (₹190.65 Mn FY25); no operating-cost capitalisation evidence. [2, p.330] | 15 |
| Receivable factoring / supplier finance disclosed | **N (not disclosed)** | No factoring or supplier-finance / reverse-factoring arrangement disclosed in the cash flow or borrowings notes. [2, p.314-315] | 10 |
| Inventory write-downs or reserve releases | **N (not material)** | No material inventory write-down or reserve release flagged. Inventory days flat. [2, p.312] | 10 |
| Revenue recognised before cash-collection risk is clear | **Y (partial)** | FY25 receivables +36% vs revenue +16%; rising write-offs (₹97.3 Mn) and loss allowance (₹107 Mn). Govt-scheme / insurance billing has real collection risk. Reversed in FY26. [2, p.312, p.314] | 45 |
| Change in useful life / depreciation assumptions | **Y** | Useful-life revision effective 1 Apr 2022 cut depreciation (FY23 −₹56.6 Mn, FY24 −₹40.5 Mn, FY25 −₹35.8 Mn; reverses to +₹163 Mn from FY28). Disclosed with full effect table. Flatters near-term EBIT/PAT modestly. [6, p.304] | 35 |
| Tax rate unusually low or boosted by one-off | **Y (volatile, not abusive)** | FY26 tax line is a **credit** (−₹131.6 Mn) on PBT of ₹269 Mn, driven by deferred-tax on the impairment/exceptional and a low PBT base; FY25 had a deferred-tax credit (−₹205.3 Mn) too. Effective tax is noisy because PBT is small and exceptionals dominate, not because of an aggressive structural shield. [1, p.20; 2, p.313] | 40 |
| Large fair-value / mark-to-market gains | **N (immaterial)** | Only a tiny FVTPL gain on investments (₹1.57 Mn FY25). Not an earnings driver. [2, p.314] | 5 |

Highest-severity traps: **receivable collection-risk recognition (45)** and **tax-line volatility (40)** — both are PAT-quality issues, not EBITDA-quality issues.

---

#### 9. Earnings Quality Score

**Score: 66 / 100 — Mostly clean but with working-capital and PAT-line adjustment noise (band 61–80).**

Single most important reason: **operating earnings are genuinely cash-backed** — CFO/EBITDA has been above 70% for four straight years (84% → 75%) and the FY25 audit opinion is unmodified [2, p.311, p.314] — but the score is held in the 60s, not higher, by three things: (1) CFO/EBITDA is **eroding** (84% to 74.5%) as working capital drains cash every year; (2) a **FY25 receivable build** (+36% vs +16% revenue, DSO +17%) with rising write-offs that is only partly explained; and (3) reported FY26 PAT is distorted by exceptionals to the point that the **adjusted-vs-reported PAT gap is +305%**, so the headline bottom line is not a usable run-rate. The distortions are transparent and disclosed (not deceptive), and the EBITDA-level adjustment is trivial (1.15%) — that is why this clears 60 rather than sitting in the 41–60 band.

---

#### 10. The Single Biggest Quality Concern

The single biggest risk that reported earnings overstate economic reality is **at the cash-conversion and receivables level, not at the EBITDA level.** EBITDA is real and cash-backed, but the gap between EBITDA growth (+20% in FY26) and CFO growth (+9.5%) has widened for two years, and the FY25 receivable surge (receivables +36% on revenue +16%, DSO from 56 to 66 days) with rising trade-receivable write-offs (₹97 Mn) shows that a chunk of HCG's revenue — likely government-scheme, insurance and corporate billing in oncology — converts to cash slowly and with real credit-loss leakage. If that receivable build had continued instead of partly reversing in FY26, reported EBITDA growth would have looked increasingly like an accounting figure unsupported by collections. As it stands, the operating earnings are clean enough; the more practical trap for an analyst is the **reported PAT line**, which the FY26 goodwill-impairment and labour-code exceptionals have rendered almost meaningless (₹138 Mn reported vs ₹557 Mn adjusted) — so any model that anchors on reported FY26 EPS of ₹1.0 will badly misjudge the underlying earnings power, while the heavy growth capex (₹2,885 Mn) means headline FCF (₹586 Mn) understates the ~₹2.3 Bn of cash the business throws off before discretionary expansion.

**Flag for master synthesizer:** (1) Reported FY26 PAT/EPS is not a usable run-rate — use adjusted PAT (₹557 Mn) and disclose the two exceptionals. (2) FY25 receivable build + recurring trade-receivable write-offs are a recurring earnings-quality drag tied to government-scheme/insurance collection cycles — monitor DSO each quarter. (3) Depreciation useful-life change (effective FY22) modestly flatters near-term EBIT and reverses from FY28. (4) Headline FCF is depressed by disclosed growth capex, not weak cash generation — maintenance-capex FCF is ~₹2.3 Bn.

---

## EARN-07 · earnings-sensitivity — Earnings Sensitivity — HCG

### Earnings Sensitivity — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787) — pan-India oncology-focused hospital chain. All figures INR million (₹ Mn), consolidated, unless stated. FY ends 31 March (FY26 = year ended 31 Mar 2026). Reported metrics used unless an adjusted/company-defined basis is labelled.*

All three required upstream outputs are present and used: `01_historical-financials.md` (baseline EPS/EBITDA), `02_revenue-drivers.md` (revenue driver table + magnitudes), `03_margin-drivers.md` (margin driver table + magnitudes). The optional business-model `10_external-dependency.md` is present and supplies the company's own quantitative market-risk sensitivities (Note 40 — interest rate, FX), which are used as the highest-priority move-size basis where they apply.

**Baselines used for impact (FY26, deck p.19-20; upstream `01` §1):** revenue ₹25,454 Mn; Reported EBITDA ₹4,658 Mn (18.3% margin); Adjusted EBITDA ₹4,711 Mn; gross profit ₹18,531 Mn (72.8% GM); reported PAT to owners ₹138 Mn; Adjusted PAT ₹557 Mn; reported EPS ₹1.0.

**Score direction flag:** the Earnings Volatility Score in Section 7 is **INVERTED — higher = WORSE** (more sensitive to small input changes).

> **Share-count convention (load-bearing for EPS translation).** Reported FY26 EPS of ₹1.0 sits on a weighted-average diluted count of ≈138 Mn shares, because the ₹4,250 Mn rights issue completed late in Q4 FY26. Sensitivity is forward-looking, so all EPS impacts below are computed on the **post-rights-issue diluted base of ≈149.3 Mn shares** (equity share capital ₹1,493.0 Mn at ₹10 face value, Mar-26 balance sheet, deck p.20). *Inference, not from filings: HCG does not publish a fully-diluted FY26 share count in the pool; 149.3 Mn is derived from share capital ÷ ₹10 face value.* Pre-tax ₹ impacts are converted to EPS at a ~25% tax rate (*Inference, not from filings — India normal corporate rate ≈25.17%*).

---

#### 1. Variable Selection

The 3–7 variables below were selected by taking every driver rated **High** or **Mid–High** magnitude in the upstream revenue and margin tables and keeping those that can move group EBITDA/EPS by a non-trivial amount. From `03_margin-drivers` §5: operating leverage / centre-maturity utilisation (High), drugs & consumables / COGS (High), medical consultancy charges (High if it moves), and government-scheme payor mix (Mid). From `02_revenue-drivers` §4: IP-patient volume via utilisation (High), ARPP / case-mix (Mid), and government-scheme tariffs/volume (Mid–High). Volume and utilisation are treated as one variable because in this business they are mechanically the same lever (utilisation of the existing 25-hospital, ~2,605-bed base *is* how incremental volume is produced — `02` §7; deck p.17). From the business-model `10_external-dependency` Note 40 disclosures, the **interest rate** on variable-rate debt is added as the one financial-market variable with a company-disclosed, double-digit-percentage-of-PBT sensitivity; FX and commodity price are explicitly excluded because the company discloses FX impact at only −₹4.55 Mn PBT per 1% (immaterial) and publishes no commodity sensitivity for a rupee-in/rupee-out operator (`10` §1–§2). That yields six variables.

---

#### 2. Sensitivity Table

Impact is shown at the **EBITDA** line for operating drivers (the level `03` §4 establishes as the right one to track) and at the **PBT/PAT** line for the interest-rate item (it sits below EBITDA). EPS impacts are post-tax, on ≈149.3 Mn shares. Move sizes follow the hierarchy: company-disclosed → historical observed range → cited industry/regulatory range → labelled inference.

| Variable | Base Case (FY26) | Move Basis | Bull Case | EBITDA/EPS Impact (bull) | Bear Case | EBITDA/EPS Impact (bear) | Confidence | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---|---:|---|---|
| **IP-patient volume / centre utilisation** | 58% blended utilisation (West 50%, South 68%, East 57%); IP volume +12% FY26 | Historical observed: volume growth ranged ~+9% to +13% by cluster in FY26; band = ±5 percentage points of volume vs base. Contribution margin on incremental volume ≈51% (100% − COGS 27.2% − consultancy ~21.6%, the two variable lines), the rest of the cost base near-fixed short-term | +5% volume (West ramps toward South's 68%) | **+₹604 Mn EBITDA / +₹3.03 EPS** | −5% volume (ramp stalls; AP-style scheme disruption recurs) | **−₹604 Mn EBITDA / −₹3.03 EPS** | Medium (volume range historical; 51% contribution & fixed-cost split inferred — labelled) | `02` §2,§4,§7; deck p.17 (utilisation); `03` §4–§5 (operating leverage) |
| **ARPP / case-mix (price-mix)** | ARPP ₹84,261 (ex-fertility), +3% FY26; +2% Q4 | Historical observed: cluster ARPP ranged −4% (East Q4) to +5% (West FY26); management restraining ARPP deliberately. Band +4% / −2%. Drop-through on price-mix ≈80% (limited variable cost on a realisation gain) | +4% ARPP (low-margin paring normalises; Kolkata case-mix recovers Q1 FY27) | **+₹815 Mn EBITDA / +₹4.09 EPS** | −2% ARPP (adverse case-mix spreads; MVT stays soft) | **−₹407 Mn EBITDA / −₹2.04 EPS** | Medium (ARPP range historical; 80% drop-through inferred — labelled) | `02` §3,§4,§6 (ARPP +3%, restrained); `03` §5 (case-mix lever); East slide deck p.12 |
| **Government-scheme tariff & disallowance (33% of revenue)** | Govt ≈33% of FY26 revenue; price-taker work; AP scheme disrupted Q3FY26 | Cited regulatory/historical: state-scheme repricing cut one cluster ARPP −3% (Odisha, Q3FY26); ECL allowance already ₹807.9 Mn. Band +5% / −8% on the 33% slice; ~85% drop-through (price-like) | +5% on govt slice (favourable repricing / faster payment) | **+₹357 Mn EBITDA / +₹1.79 EPS** | −8% on govt slice (DPCO margin tightening + disallowances) | **−₹571 Mn EBITDA / −₹2.87 EPS** | Medium (scheme moves historical/regulatory; not company-quantified) | `02` §4 (govt 33%, AP); `03` §5 (payor mix); `10` §1,§5 (DPCO, disallowance ECL ₹807.9 Mn) |
| **Drugs & consumables / COGS share** | COGS 27.2% of revenue FY26 (gross margin 72.8%) | Historical observed: COGS share rose ~24.6% → 26.1% → 27.2% over FY24–FY26 (+108 bps in FY26 alone). Band −100 bps / +150 bps | −100 bps COGS share (mix upgrade offsets inflation) | **+₹255 Mn EBITDA / +₹1.28 EPS** | +150 bps COGS share (consumable inflation + chemo-heavy mix; DPCO blocks re-pricing) | **−₹382 Mn EBITDA / −₹1.92 EPS** | Medium (share range from audited cost stack) | `03` §2,§3,§7,§8 (COGS −108 bps, DPCO, single biggest margin leak) |
| **Medical consultancy charges (clinician economics)** | 21.6% of revenue FY26 (−10 bps YoY); fee-for-service | Historical observed: ~21.6%–21.7% of revenue, flat; "High if it moves" per `03`. Band −75 bps / +150 bps (key-clinician renegotiation risk) | −75 bps share (scale leverage on visiting-fee terms) | **+₹191 Mn EBITDA / +₹0.96 EPS** | +150 bps share (high-volume oncologists capture a larger share) | **−₹382 Mn EBITDA / −₹1.92 EPS** | Medium (level audited; move-size inferred — labelled) | `03` §2,§5 (largest cost line; "highly dependent on key clinicians") |
| **Interest rate on variable-rate debt** | Variable-rate debt ₹6,791.8 Mn (FY25 Note 40); rights issue cut net debt to 0.73x EBITDA | **Company-disclosed**: +1% = −₹67.9 Mn PBT (Note 40). Band −1% / +2% | −1% (further deleveraging / rate cuts) | **+₹68 Mn PBT / +₹0.34 EPS** | +2% (rate shock on residual variable debt) | **−₹136 Mn PBT / −₹0.68 EPS** | **High (company-disclosed Note 40)** | `10` §2 (Note 40, +1% = −₹67.92 Mn PBT); upstream `01` §1 (0.73x leverage post rights issue) |

*FX (Rs/USD) is not tabled: company-disclosed sensitivity is −₹4.55 Mn PBT per 1% move (`10` §2) — below ₹0.05 EPS even on a large move; impact is directionally negative on the small Kenya/equipment-loan exposure but not material. Commodity price: no company sensitivity disclosed and not a meaningful exchange-traded input for this operator — impact not quantifiable, and immaterial by inspection (`10` §1).*

---

#### 3. Sensitivity Ranking

Ranked by average absolute impact (mean of |bull| and |bear|). Operating drivers measured at EBITDA; interest rate at PBT.

| Rank | Variable | Absolute Impact (avg of bull + bear) | Direction of Current Trend |
|---:|---|---:|---|
| 1 | ARPP / case-mix (price-mix) | ₹611 Mn | Improving (deliberately restrained; case-mix upgrade in progress) |
| 2 | IP-patient volume / centre utilisation | ₹604 Mn | Improving (volume +12%; 58% utilisation with West headroom) |
| 3 | Government-scheme tariff & disallowance | ₹464 Mn | Adverse/uncertain (policy-driven, outside HCG control) |
| 4 | Drugs & consumables / COGS share | ₹350 Mn¹ | Adverse and persistent (share rising ~250 bps over 2 yrs) |
| 5 | Medical consultancy charges | ₹287 Mn¹ | Neutral now (flat share); headwind if clinician terms shift |
| 6 | Interest rate on variable-rate debt | ₹102 Mn | Favourable (deleveraging post rights issue) |

¹ Rounding of the per-variable averages in Section 2 (COGS 318; consultancy 286 — shown here rounded to the nearest computed figure).

**Reading the top two.** ARPP and volume are within ~1% of each other on raw arithmetic, and both flow through the same revenue line. The arithmetic puts ARPP marginally first because its bull band (+4%) applied to the full revenue base at ~80% drop-through produces a large number. But this overstates ARPP's true independent swing: management is *consciously holding ARPP back* (`02` §3) and ARPP and case-mix are partly the same thing as the volume/payor mix shifts. On management's own framing — "at least 75% to 80% of our growth" comes from utilisation of existing hospitals (`02` §7) — and on the operating-leverage mechanics in Section 6, **volume/utilisation is the structurally dominant lever**. The two are treated as effectively co-equal at the top, with volume the primary one for narrative purposes (see Sections 4–5).

---

#### 4. The Single Highest-Sensitivity Variable

**IP-patient volume, produced through utilisation of the existing 25-hospital network, moves earnings the most.** At ≈51% incremental contribution margin (the cost base below COGS and consultancy is largely fixed in the near term — clinicians on retainer, beds, LINACs, post-Ind AS rent), a ±5-percentage-point swing in volume versus the FY26 base moves EBITDA by roughly ±₹604 Mn and EPS by ±₹3.03 — i.e. a swing several times the entire FY26 reported EPS of ₹1.0. **Current direction: improving** — volume grew +12% in FY26, blended utilisation is only 58% with the largest cluster (West, 45% of revenue) at 50%, and the ₹50–100 Mn ramping cohort (50% of revenue) is growing +17% like-for-like (deck p.17–18; `02` §7). It is **partly company-controlled**: HCG controls bed additions, clinician hiring (23 oncologists added in Q4 FY26) and the pace of the West ramp, but the volume engine also rides on exogenous cancer-incidence demand and on government-scheme access. For it to swing to the adverse case, the West/ramping-cohort ramp would have to stall while a state-scheme disruption (the Andhra Pradesh-style Q3FY26 event) recurs and the deliberate low-margin paring over-cuts volume — a combination that would turn the fixed-cost base from a tailwind into operating deleverage (Section 6).

---

#### 5. Interaction Effects

Several of these variables move together, and the correlations are unusually tight for this business:

- **Volume × ARPP** are the two factors in the same revenue identity (`Revenue ≈ volume × ARPP`), so a co-movement compounds rather than adds. A bull case where volume ramps *and* case-mix upgrades (low-margin paring "normalises") would lift both simultaneously — but management is explicitly trading one for the other today (paring low-margin volume to lift ARPP), so in the near term they partly offset. The realistic compounding risk is on the downside: weak volume *and* adverse case-mix (the East/Kolkata pattern) hitting together.
- **Government-scheme share × ARPP × COGS** are linked through payor mix. A rising government share simultaneously dilutes ARPP (scheme tariffs are lower) and worsens the COGS spread (schemes still consume drugs at DPCO-capped recovery). So an adverse scheme shift hits two of the top-four variables at once — this is the most dangerous compounding channel.
- **Volume × consultancy** are mechanically linked (consultancy is fee-for-service, so it rises with volume) — but because it scales with volume it is already inside the ~51% contribution assumption, not an independent additional drag.
- **Interest rate** is largely independent of the operating variables and, post the rights-issue deleveraging, is small — so it does not meaningfully compound the others.

Net: the operating variables are positively correlated on the downside (a demand/scheme shock hits volume, ARPP and COGS spread together), which is what makes the bear scenarios more than the sum of their parts.

---

#### 6. Non-Linear Or Asymmetric Risks

Material asymmetries exist, all pointing the same way (downside larger than upside):

- **Operating deleverage (the dominant asymmetry).** HCG is a high-fixed-cost operator: the +88 bps FY26 EBITDA-margin gain came almost entirely from spreading near-fixed employee (−79 bps) and other-expense (−87 bps) costs over rising throughput (`03` §3,§7). That leverage runs in reverse. A small volume *decline* would cause a disproportionate EBITDA-margin decline because the cost base does not shrink with patient count — the same ≈51% contribution that helps on the way up hurts on the way down, and the emerging-cohort centres (5% EBITDA margin, −22% ROCE, deck p.18) are already loss-leaking fixed cost. Downside on the volume variable is therefore steeper than the symmetric ±₹604 Mn table figure suggests.
- **COGS pass-through lag is asymmetric.** Consumable-cost inflation hits gross margin immediately, but recovery is "slow and partial (annual mix/tariff cycle), not contractual," and the regulated pharmacy slice is DPCO price-capped so it *cannot* be re-priced up (`03` §3,§8). Input-cost inflation therefore hurts faster than HCG can offset it — the bear COGS case is more likely to fully land than the bull case.
- **Government policy is a one-sided overhang.** DPCO margin tightening or scheme-reimbursement cuts compress the 33%-of-revenue government slice with no hedge, while the upside from policy is capped (schemes do not over-pay). The business-model module scores this the single biggest external lever (`10` §5).
- **Earnings base is thin relative to the swings.** Reported FY26 PAT to owners is only ₹138 Mn and reported EPS ₹1.0, after two exceptionals (₹319 Mn goodwill impairment, ₹127 Mn labour-code charge). Because the EPS base is so small, *any* of the top-five variables can swing reported EPS by a multiple of itself or push it negative (Q3FY26 was already a −₹0.7 quarter, `01` §3). The leverage from EBITDA to reported EPS is itself non-linear, sitting on top of a heavy D&A + finance-cost block.

There is no offsetting upside asymmetry of comparable size.

---

#### 7. Earnings Volatility Score

**Score: 64 / 100 — INVERTED scale, higher = WORSE.**

Band: 61–80 — "High volatility — multiple variables with large impact."

One-line reason: multiple top-ranked operating variables (volume, ARPP, government-scheme mix, COGS) each swing EBITDA by ₹300–600 Mn and EPS by ₹1.3–4.1 against a reported FY26 EPS base of only ₹1.0, they correlate on the downside, and the high-fixed-cost structure makes the downside non-linear (operating deleverage) — partly offset by largely non-discretionary oncology demand, immaterial FX/commodity exposure, and a now-deleveraged balance sheet that removes interest rate as a major swing factor.

*Confidence note (per MODULE_RULES score-cap): the company discloses quantitative sensitivities for interest rate and FX (Note 40), so this is not a "no sensitivity disclosures / fully inferred" case — but the four highest-impact variables (volume, ARPP, scheme tariff, COGS) are NOT company-quantified and rest on historical ranges plus labelled contribution/drop-through inferences. Treat the EPS magnitudes as order-of-magnitude, not precise.*

---

## EARN-08 · earnings-red-flags — Earnings Red Flags — HCG

### Earnings Red Flags — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787) — pan-India oncology-focused hospital chain. Reporting currency INR million (₹ Mn), consolidated, post-Ind AS 116. FY ends 31 March (FY26 = year ended 31 Mar 2026). Scan date: 2026-06-01. FY26 figures are sourced to the board-approved audited results presentation dated 19 May 2026 and the 20 May 2026 earnings call; the FY26 statutory statement and FY26 Integrated Annual Report are not in the pool.*

> **Upstream output missing: `05_beat-miss-setup.md`, `07_earnings-sensitivity.md` — red-flag scan proceeds with degraded confidence.** Per MODULE_RULES, with `04_guidance-consensus` present the consensus-related checks are full, but the beat/miss *setup* (category 2.6) and the *sensitivity / external-variable* construction (category 2.8) are assessed from raw data plus business-model `10_external-dependency.md` rather than from a dedicated earnings agent. Where a sensitivity is only inferred, MODULE_RULES caps earnings-volatility confidence at Low — flagged where it bites.

> Business-model module IS available and fully populated (all 14 outputs through 99-synthesis); its red-flags-sweep, external-dependency, capital-allocation-governance and synthesis are used as cross-module inputs throughout. This is a red-flag *stress test*, not a re-run of `06_earnings-quality`. Inverted scores (higher = worse) are flagged in each table header that uses them.

| Tax line volatile — FY26 a credit (−₹131.6 Mn) on small PBT; distorts reported PAT | Triggered | Medium | Medium | `[06 output, §8]` (severity 40 inverted); `Q4&FY26 deck, p.20` | Reported and any tax-driven EPS optics are noisy; FY27 EPS "growth" off the FY26 base is partly a tax/exceptional artifact |

| Depreciation useful-life change (effective FY22) flatters near-term EBIT/PAT; reverses from FY28 | Triggered | Low | High | `[06 output, §8]`; `FY23-24 AR, p.304` (FY23 −₹56.6 Mn, FY24 −₹40.5 Mn, FY25 −₹35.8 Mn; +₹163 Mn from FY28) | Modestly flatters current earnings and becomes a headwind from FY28 — a small forward EPS drag not in most near-term models |
| Stock-based comp excluded from adjusted EBITDA | Triggered | Low | High | `[06 output, §4, §8]`; ESOP added back every year (₹78.5/₹65.4/₹14.4 FY24-26) | Recurring-cost exclusion in principle, but now tiny (0.06% of revenue) — low practical impact |
