#### 3. Litigation & Tax Contingencies

Consolidated "claims against the Group not acknowledged as debt" [FY24-25 AR, consolidated Note 33]. No provision is recorded against these (the Group states resolution timing is not estimable); the recorded provision column is therefore nil except where a balance-sheet provision exists (EPCG).

| Matter | Recorded Provision (₹ Mn) | Maximum / Claimed (₹ Mn) | Status (active / remote) — company language | Source |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| **EPCG export-obligation duty** (customs duty benefit repayable if export obligations not met) | 3.19 (balance-sheet contingency provision for cases where shortfall *expected*) | 517.58 (full EPCG duty contingent if obligations unmet) | **Active / largest item.** Group "expects that it will be able to meet its export obligations"; provision booked only for "certain cases" with expected shortfall (provision fell 155.84 → 3.19) | FY24-25 AR, consolidated Note 33(vii); Note 20(i)-(ii) (provision movement) |
| Income tax (capital-project disallowances + other) | nil | 33.37 | Active appeals; management "expects its position will likely be upheld" | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(vi) |
| Excise & service tax (FDG excisability; service-tax audits) | nil | 31.72 | Active (CESTAT appeals); company "positive of winning" | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(ii) |
| Sales tax (CST — non-submission of 'F' forms) | nil | 26.00 | Active appeals; "procedural challenge", no adverse impact expected | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(iv) |
| Value added tax (multiple states — canteen/food, ITC) | nil | 49.03 | Active appeals (some demands already recovered/under appeal) | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(iii) |
| Former employees (overseas subsidiaries — termination claims) | nil | 17.15 | Active; "will not have any material adverse effect" | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(ix) |
| Bonus (retrospective Payment of Bonus Amendment 2015) | nil | 9.98 | Dormant/stayed (two High Court stays; no provision required) | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(viii) |
| Customs duty (HCG Medisurge LINAC second consignment CVD) | nil | 4.48 | Active (CESTAT appeal) | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(i) |
| Provident Fund (Supreme Court special-allowance ruling, Feb-2019) | nil | **Unquantified** | Reasonably possible / monitoring — "keeps a close watch", no amount estimated | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(x) |
| Other disputes / lawsuits / commercial matters (ordinary course) | nil | **Unquantified** | Active but "no such pending matters expected to have material adverse effect" | FY24-25 AR, Note 33(xi) |
| **Total quantified claims not acknowledged as debt** | **3.19** | **689.31** | — | FY24-25 AR, consolidated Note 33 (sum) |

**Notes on this section.**
1. **EPCG is the single largest litigation/tax contingency (₹517.58 Mn = 75% of the quantified claims).** It is a recurring, structural exposure for a hospital that imports LINACs and PET-CT under duty exemption: if export obligations (typically earning foreign exchange via medical tourism) are not met, the avoided customs duty becomes payable. The Group recognises a provision only where it *expects* a shortfall (₹3.19 Mn at FY25, down from ₹155.84 Mn after settling ₹135 Mn) and treats the remaining ₹517.58 Mn as contingent on the view that obligations will be met. Probability language is favourable but this is an **active** exposure, not remote.
2. **The remaining tax/litigation tail is small and mostly procedural.** Excise (FDG), VAT, CST and customs items are individually ≤₹49 Mn, mostly old (FY11-FY17 periods), under appeal, with management "positive of winning." Aggregate ex-EPCG quantified = ₹171.73 Mn (≈0.04x FY26 EBITDA).
3. **Two unquantified live items:** the PF special-allowance ruling and "other disputes in the ordinary course." Neither is sized; both are described as not materially adverse. For a multi-entity serial acquirer with overseas subsidiaries, these are the cracks where undisclosed exposure can hide — flagged.
4. **No going-concern, fraud, or regulator-enforcement litigation** is disclosed. Auditor opinion was unmodified on both standalone and consolidated statements [business-model/11; FY24-25 AR, Independent Auditor's Report].

---

#### 4. Contingent Exposure Summary

Equity base for the ratio: **total equity ₹14,102.1 Mn (FY26)** per `01` §5. EBITDA reference: FY26 reported EBITDA ₹4,657.9 Mn per `01`. All figures ₹ Mn, consolidated. To avoid double-counting, **capitalised leases (₹8,249.5 Mn) are EXCLUDED** here — they sit in `01`'s debt stack, not in off-balance-sheet/contingent exposure.

| Metric | Value |
|---|---:|
| Total **recognised** contingent/debt-like liabilities (outside 01's borrowings): put 634.00 + forward 1,401.34 + gratuity net 257.71 + EPCG provision 3.19 | **₹2,296.24 Mn** |
| Total **maximum / gross** exposure: gross put 1,778.00 + forward commitment 1,540.00 + gratuity DBO 273.81 + claims-not-acknowledged 689.31 + corporate guarantee (parent) 631.57 + capital commitments 1,975.24 + lease commitments 239.85 | **₹7,127.78 Mn** |
| — of which already crystallised (Vizag 2nd tranche paid Apr-2026) | 1,543 |
| — of which unquantified (BG/CC-facility guarantee, letter of support to 13 subs, PF ruling, residual 15% Vizag, ordinary-course suits) | Not estimable — **upside to the ₹7,127.78 Mn** |
| **Max exposure ÷ recognised** | **3.10x** (7,127.78 ÷ 2,296.24) |
| **Max exposure ÷ total equity** | **50.5%** (7,127.78 ÷ 14,102.1) |
| Memo: recognised ÷ total equity | 16.3% |
| Memo: max exposure ÷ FY26 reported EBITDA | 1.53x |
| Memo: claims-not-acknowledged (litigation/tax only) ÷ equity | 4.9% |

**Notes on the math.**
1. The ₹7,127.78 Mn max excludes capitalised leases (already in `01`) and **understates** the true maximum because three items are unquantified (the bank-guarantee/cash-credit corporate guarantee, the open-ended letter of support to 13 subsidiaries, the residual 15% Vizag tranche) plus the unsized PF and ordinary-course litigation. The 50.5%-of-equity figure is therefore a **floor**, not a ceiling.
2. The 3.10x max-to-recognised ratio is driven mostly by items the company has *committed* to but not yet recognised as liabilities (capital commitments ₹1,975.24 Mn, gross put ₹1,778.00 Mn, parent guarantee ₹631.57 Mn) — i.e. spend it has signed up for, not surprise claims. The pure-surprise litigation/tax tail (₹689.31 Mn) is only ~4.9% of equity.
3. **Capital commitments (₹1,975.24 Mn) are the second-largest single line** — these are signed capex contracts for the brownfield bed expansion, a discretionary growth commitment rather than a distress claim, but they are a real near-term call on cash that the leverage ratios in `01` do not show.

---

#### 5. Contingency Read

The largest off-balance-sheet exposures are the **Vizag (VHCRPL) put + forward acquisition obligations** — ₹2,035 Mn recognised in other financial liabilities and up to ~₹3,318 Mn at the commitment-note gross figures — and they sit entirely outside `01`'s borrowings line, so HCG's "1.4x" headline net leverage understates the cash claims on the group; ₹1,543 Mn of this has already crystallised (paid Apr-2026) and is live, not contingent. Outside the Vizag M&A obligations, the only material *litigation* contingency is the **₹517.58 Mn EPCG export-obligation duty** (75% of the ₹689 Mn claims-not-acknowledged), which is active but for which the Group expects to meet its obligations and has provided only ₹3.19 Mn — a low-probability-of-loss but unhedged tail tied to its imported oncology equipment. If the full maximum exposure (₹7,128 Mn, ≈50% of equity, before three unquantified items) crystallised simultaneously it would be a material but not solvency-ending event — roughly 1.5x FY26 EBITDA — and the realistic near-term cash drain (the ₹1,543 Mn Vizag tranche plus ₹1,975 Mn of committed capex) is the part that actually bites liquidity rather than the dormant tax tail.

**Partial data:** the FY26 statutory commitments-and-contingencies note is not in the pool — the quantified contingencies (₹689.31 Mn) and the put/forward/guarantee figures are at 31 Mar 2025 values, one year stale. Two parent obligations (the corporate guarantee for subsidiary bank-guarantee/cash-credit facilities, and an open-ended letter of support to 13 subsidiaries) and the residual 15% Vizag acquisition price are **disclosed but unquantified**. For a serial hospital acquirer with overseas subsidiaries and an EPCG/import-duty tail, undisclosed FY26 contingent movements cannot be ruled out. Per `MODULE_RULES.md` (off-balance-sheet exposures partly undisclosed for a levered, acquisitive name), solvency strength should be capped at 75 at the synthesis layer on this account.

---

## BSS-06 · downside-stress-test — Downside Stress Test — HCG

### Downside Stress Test — HCG

HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Report date: 2026-06-01. India-focused cancer-care / multispecialty hospital operator (≈25 hospitals; one centre in Kenya). **Reporting currency: INR million (₹ Mn), consolidated, Ind AS, as at 31 March 2026 (FY26)** unless a date is stated. Fiscal year ends 31 March. EBITDA throughout is **Reported EBITDA (audited statutory operating result, pre other-income, post-Ind AS 116)**, which is the cash-backed base used here (see note below); Adjusted EBITDA differs by only +₹53 Mn (+1.15%), so Reported is the conservative anchor.

**Inputs consumed:** `01_capital-structure-and-leverage.md` (net debt, EBITDA base, rate mix), `02_maturity-wall-and-refinancing.md` (12-month obligations, floating exposure, rate shock), `03_liquidity-runway.md` (committed liquidity, near-term uses, runway), `04_coverage-and-covenants.md` (coverage, the tightest covenant — labeled assumption — and breach proximities), `05_off-balance-sheet-and-contingencies.md` (Vizag cash calls, EPCG, committed capex). Cross-module: `business-model/10_external-dependency.md` (cycle depth), `earnings/03_margin-drivers.md` (downside-margin levers), `earnings/06_earnings-quality.md` (cash-backed EBITDA). This agent does **not** assign probabilities, value the company, or rate the stock — that is the master synthesizer's job.

**EBITDA basis — cash-backed cross-check (per earnings/06).** Reported EBITDA ₹4,657.9 Mn is **cash-backed but the conversion rate is eroding**: CFO/Reported EBITDA was 74.5% in FY26 (down from 84–86% in FY23–FY24), above the 70% healthy line for four straight years; the adjusted-vs-reported gap is only +1.15% [earnings/06 §1–2, §9]. So Reported EBITDA is **not** materially above cash-backed EBITDA and is the correct stress base. The practical caveat the stress must carry: the CFO that actually services debt runs ~25% below EBITDA, so coverage is modeled on cash uses, not on headline EBITDA, where it matters.

**Two-covenant caveat (carried from 04).** **No lender financial maintenance covenant is disclosed in the data pool** [04 §2; FY24-25 AR, Note 18, searched in full]. The breach checks below therefore run against **labeled-assumption** market covenants for an A+ (ICRA)-rated Indian operating company — **assumed min interest coverage 2.0x (lease-inclusive)** and **assumed max net leverage 3.5x (incl. lease)** — plus management's quantified **internal 2.5x ex-lease net-leverage ceiling** (a board discipline, not a lender test). **True covenant headroom is "Not assessable"; all covenant breach points below are indicative**, per the partial-data rule [MODULE_RULES partial-data + score-cap tables].

---

#### 1. Base Case (today)

| Input | Value | Source |
|---|---:|---|
| Base EBITDA (cash-backed, Reported, FY26) | ₹4,657.9 Mn | 01 §7; earnings/06 §1 (CFO/EBITDA 74.5%, cash-backed) |
| Net debt — incl. leases (cash only) | ₹11,993.1 Mn | from 01 §4, §7 |
| Net debt — ex-lease (cash only) | ₹3,743.6 Mn | from 01 §4, §7 |
| Net debt / EBITDA — incl. leases | **2.57x** | 11,993.1 ÷ 4,657.9 [01 §5] |
| Net debt / EBITDA — ex-lease | 0.80x | 3,743.6 ÷ 4,657.9 [01 §5] |
| EBITDA / interest — total finance cost (incl. lease interest) | **2.64x** | 4,657.9 ÷ 1,765.7 [04 §1] |
| EBITDA / interest — cash bank interest only (ex-lease, ~₹970 Mn proxy) | ~4.8x | 4,657.9 ÷ ~970 [04 §1, flagged proxy] |
| EBIT / interest (incl. lease interest) | 1.40x | 2,466.1 ÷ 1,765.7 [04 §1] |
| Tightest *disclosed/quantified* constraint | Internal 2.5x ex-lease net-leverage ceiling (not a lender covenant) | 04 §3; Q4/FY26 transcript |
| Tightest *assumed* lender covenant (labeled assumption) | Min interest coverage 2.0x (lease-inclusive) | 04 §3 (labeled assumption — not assessable for scoring) |
| Next-12m obligations — hard (overdraft rolled), ex-Vizag, ex-growth capex | ~₹4,338 Mn | 03 §3 (term principal ~917 + lease principal 649.7 + cash interest ~1,582 + maint capex 1,189) |
| Next-12m obligations — conservative (full overdraft repaid), ex-Vizag, ex-growth capex | ~₹6,533 Mn | 03 §2–3 |
| Committed liquidity (cash only) | ₹5,360.5 Mn | 03 §1 (revolver/cash-credit availability undisclosed → excluded; ~₹287 Mn margin money restricted → excluded) |
| Floating-rate debt (gross) | ~₹8,740 Mn (~96% of ₹9,104.1 Mn borrowings) | 01 §1, §7; 02 §3 |
| Hedge coverage (if any) | None located in the pool — floating exposure appears largely unhedged | 02 §3; 00_triage §3 |
| Working-capital seasonality / peak build | Not materially seasonal (each quarter ~24–26% of revenue); structural WC drain already inside CFO; no separately disclosed peak-quarter build | 03 §3 (Seasonality Hard Check); earnings/06 §3 |

**Currency / EBITDA basis:** INR million, consolidated, Ind AS, post-Ind AS 116. Reported EBITDA is the default base; the cash-backed cross-check (CFO/EBITDA 74.5%) holds [earnings/06 §1].

**Cycle-depth calibration (per 10_external-dependency).** HCG is **"partly externally driven"** (external-dependency score 45/100, inverted) — oncology demand is largely **non-discretionary and not cyclical-consumer**, so a deep volume-led EBITDA collapse is less likely than for a discretionary or commodity name [10 §3–4]. There is **no commodity cyclicality** (drugs/consumables are regulated-price sourced goods, not exchange-traded) and **no industrial/weather cycle** [10 §1]. The genuine downside drivers are **margin, not volume**: (i) DPCO drug-price control / margin caps compressing the 27%-of-revenue consumables spread (the single biggest margin driver, a structural Headwind [earnings/03 §8]); (ii) a rising government-scheme payer share (≈33% of revenue, price-taker work) diluting realisation [earnings/03 §5]; (iii) state-scheme collection disruption (e.g. the Q3FY26 Andhra Pradesh event) draining cash via receivables [earnings/06 §3, §10]. Because HCG is **not a deep cyclical**, the −30/−40/−60% haircuts below already span more than its own demand history would suggest; the −40%/−60% cases are therefore best read as a **margin-and-collections shock** (DPCO + payer-mix + bad-debt), not a volume cliff. A separate history-calibrated trough scenario is not warranted (and a clean EBITDA trough-to-peak history is not in the pool — FY23–FY26 EBITDA rose every year, ₹2,987→₹4,658 Mn [earnings/06 §1]); the standard haircuts bound the realistic downside.

---

#### 2. Stress Scenarios

EBITDA haircuts applied to the ₹4,657.9 Mn base. **Leverage and coverage** lines hold net debt and total finance cost constant at the haircut snapshot (the standard point-in-time covenant test). The **12-month liquidity gap** is modeled dynamically: internal cash generation falls with EBITDA (CFO scaled at the 74.5% conversion ratio [earnings/06 §1]); maintenance capex is held flat (hospitals must keep maintaining); the on-demand working-capital overdraft (~₹2.8 Bn) is **assumed rolled** in the EBITDA-haircut columns (its normal behaviour, but availability is undisclosed [03 §1]); the ₹1,543 Mn Vizag deferred consideration (paid Apr-2026, inside the FY27 window [05 §1]) is included as a real first-half cash call. Liquidity gap = 12-month uses − (opening cash ₹5,360.5 Mn + scaled CFO); positive = shortfall. Coverage uses the **lease-inclusive** total finance cost as the conservative anchor.

| Metric | Base | −30% EBITDA | −40% EBITDA | −60% EBITDA | −40% + WC shock | −40% + rates +200bp |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| EBITDA (₹ Mn) | 4,658 | 3,261 | 2,795 | 1,863 | 2,795 | 2,795 |
| Net debt / EBITDA — incl. leases | 2.57x | 3.68x | 4.29x | 6.44x | 4.29x | 4.29x |
| Net debt / EBITDA — ex-lease | 0.80x | 1.15x | 1.34x | 2.01x | 1.34x | 1.34x |
| EBITDA / interest (lease-incl total) | 2.64x | 1.85x | 1.58x | 1.06x | 1.58x | 1.44x |
| EBITDA / interest (cash bank interest, ~₹970 Mn proxy) | 4.80x | 3.36x | 2.88x | 1.92x | 2.88x | 2.44x |
| Tightest covenant headroom — assumed 2.0x coverage (lease-incl)¹ | +32% | −8% | −21% | −47% | −21% | −28% |
| Covenant breach? — assumed 2.0x coverage (lease-incl)¹ | No | **YES** | **YES** | **YES** | **YES** | **YES** |
| Covenant breach? — assumed 3.5x leverage (incl. lease)¹ | No | **YES** | **YES** | **YES** | **YES** | **YES** |
| Covenant breach? — internal 2.5x ex-lease ceiling | No | No | No | No | No | No |
| 12-month liquidity gap (overdraft rolled, incl Vizag) | −2,951 (surplus) | −1,910 (surplus) | −1,562 (surplus) | −868 (surplus) | −290 (surplus) | −1,387 (surplus) |
| Cash runway vs net burn (months) | ~27 | ~19 | ~17 | ~14 | ~13 | ~16 |
| Survives without external action? | Yes | **Liquidity yes; assumed covenant no** | **Liquidity yes; assumed covenant no** | **Liquidity yes; assumed covenant no** | **Liquidity yes (thin); assumed covenant no** | **Liquidity yes; assumed covenant no** |

¹ **Indicative only — no lender covenant is disclosed [04 §2].** Headroom signed so positive = headroom remaining; computed as (threshold − actual) ÷ threshold on the coverage line. The "internal 2.5x ex-lease" line is a management discipline, not a lender test. The assumed-covenant breaches are the floor a real bilateral-loan covenant *could* sit at; they are not a confirmed contractual trip.

**Working-capital shock — basis (labeled assumption).** No seasonal peak build is disclosed [03 §3]. The WC-shock column adds an **incremental cash outflow of ₹1,272 Mn = 5% of ~₹25,450 Mn FY26 revenue** (a labeled assumption sized to a plausible state-scheme collection-cycle stall on top of the structural drain already inside CFO; e.g. an Andhra-Pradesh-style disruption scaled up [earnings/06 §3]). Even with this, the rolled-overdraft 12-month position stays in a thin surplus (gap −₹290 Mn).

**Rate shock — basis.** ~96% of borrowings float and are largely unhedged [01 §7; 02 §3]. A **+200 bps shock on the floating book ≈ −₹175 Mn pre-tax** (midpoint of 02's −₹170 to −₹180 Mn full-book estimate), ~3.8% of base EBITDA [02 §3]. It cuts lease-inclusive coverage from 1.58x to 1.44x at −40% but does **not** by itself open a liquidity gap (rolled-overdraft gap −₹1,387 Mn surplus). Direction note: 02 finds the near-term *repricing* risk is actually downward (RBI repo at 5.25% vs ~6.5% when most loans were struck), so +200 bps is a genuine adverse shock, not the base path.

---

#### 3. Break Points

Net debt and finance cost held constant; the EBITDA decline that first triggers each event. Coverage break points are **indicative** (no lender covenant disclosed). Liquidity break points are solved dynamically (CFO scaled at 74.5% conversion; maintenance capex flat; Vizag ₹1,543 Mn included).

| Break Point | EBITDA Decline That Triggers It |
|---|---:|
| **Assumed 2.0x interest coverage breaches** (lease-incl total finance cost) — *the first thing to break* | **≈ −24%** (EBITDA to ~₹3,531 Mn) |
| Assumed 3.5x net leverage breaches (incl. lease) | ≈ −26% (EBITDA to ~₹3,427 Mn) |
| Assumed 2.0x interest coverage breaches (if covenant tested on cash bank interest ~₹970 Mn, not lease-incl) | ≈ −58% (EBITDA to ~₹1,940 Mn) |
| Committed liquidity exhausted within 12 months — **overdraft rolled** (incl Vizag) | ≈ −85% (EBITDA to ~₹698 Mn) — i.e. effectively does not break on EBITDA alone |
| Committed liquidity exhausted within 12 months — **market closure / overdraft repaid** (incl Vizag) | **≈ −22%** (EBITDA to ~₹3,644 Mn) — driven by the ₹2.8 Bn overdraft repayment + ₹1,543 Mn Vizag, **not** by the EBITDA decline |
| Committed liquidity exhausted within 12 months — market closure / overdraft repaid, **ex-Vizag** | ≈ −66% (EBITDA to ~₹1,573 Mn) |
| Internal 2.5x ex-lease net-leverage ceiling breaches (management discipline, not a lender test) | ≈ −68% (EBITDA to ~₹1,497 Mn) |
| Net leverage exceeds 6x — **incl. leases** (typical stressed-refi threshold) | ≈ −57% (EBITDA to ~₹1,999 Mn) |
| Net leverage exceeds 6x — **ex-lease** | ≈ −87% (EBITDA to ~₹624 Mn) |

**Reading the break points.** Two different things break at two very different depths, and *which covenant exists* decides the answer:

1. **If the bilateral bank loans carry a typical ~2.0x lease-inclusive interest-coverage covenant** (assumed — not disclosed), it trips at only **~−24% EBITDA**, shallower than a −30% recession. That is the binding constraint and it sits *inside* the standard stress range. But it is a labeled assumption; the pool shows no such covenant [04 §2].
2. **Liquidity, by contrast, is hard to break on earnings alone.** As long as the ~₹2.8 Bn on-demand overdraft rolls (its normal behaviour), committed cash + scaled CFO covers 12-month uses down to a **−85% EBITDA decline** — i.e. liquidity is not the constraint in any realistic earnings downside. Liquidity only breaks at **~−22%** in the **market-closure case where the overdraft is *also* pulled** and the ₹1,543 Mn Vizag payment lands in the same window — and even then the trigger is the **₹2.8 Bn overdraft repayment, not the EBITDA fall** (ex-Vizag the break moves out to ~−66%).

So the **first thing to break is an (assumed) coverage covenant at ~−24%**; the next real cliff is **the on-demand overdraft being called** (a counterparty action, not an earnings event), which would open a ~₹0.6–1.3 Bn 12-month gap in a −40% to −60% downside.

---

#### 4. Survival Read

**Market-closure test (no new unsecured issuance for 12 months).** HCG carries **₹5,360.5 Mn cash** (raised in the Q4 FY26 ₹4,250 Mn rights issue) against hard 12-month uses, has **no bonds, no ECB, and no near-term unsecured bullet** to refinance, and generates ~₹2.3 Bn/yr of maintenance-capex-adjusted operating cash [01 §7; 03 §3–4]. **Liquidity holds through a −40% EBITDA decline with the markets shut, provided the ~₹2.8 Bn on-demand secured working-capital overdraft is not called.** If that overdraft *is* pulled at the same time (its availability is undisclosed [03 §1]), a 12-month gap opens — ~₹0.6 Bn at −40%, ~₹1.3 Bn at −60% (incl. the ₹1,543 Mn Vizag payment) — and the overdraft repayment, not the EBITDA fall, is what breaks the position. **The first thing to break is therefore the working-capital line (a counterparty/refinancing action); after that, an assumed coverage covenant.**

**Where the structure first breaks, and what it would need.** On the conservative lease-inclusive basis, the structure first breaks at **~−24% EBITDA** *if* a typical bilateral-loan interest-coverage covenant (assumed 2.0x) exists — shallower than a normal recession — and the company would need a **covenant waiver or amendment** (no equity or asset sale required to *survive* at that depth, since liquidity is intact). Because no lender covenant is actually disclosed, this is the single biggest unknown: there is no visible early-warning trigger, but the bilateral loans almost certainly carry covenants this analysis cannot see [04 §2, §4]. If the constraint is instead measured on cash bank interest (~4.8x today), the coverage break moves out to **~−58%**.

**Is a 30–40% decline survivable on its own?** **On liquidity, yes** — even a −60% EBITDA year leaves ~14 months of cash runway with the overdraft rolled, and the −30%/−40% cases sit in a ₹1.5–1.9 Bn 12-month surplus [§2–3]. The optional levers (none required for survival, but available) are real: a **₹4,250 Mn rights issue was just executed**, KKR is in sole control with an internal 2.5x ex-lease leverage ceiling and ~68% headroom to it [01 §6], discretionary growth capex (~₹1.7 Bn/yr) can be paused, and a signed Milann divestment brings ~₹282 Mn upfront [02 §4]. **On a covenant basis, a −30%/−40% decline is *not* clean** against the assumed 2.0x coverage test (breaches at ~−24%) and would require a waiver — but that depends on a covenant the pool cannot confirm exists. The honest verdict: **liquidity survives a 30–60% EBITDA decline so long as the secured overdraft keeps rolling; the genuine break risks are (1) that overdraft being pulled and (2) an undisclosed lender coverage covenant tripping near −24%** — both of which sit outside what the EBITDA haircut alone controls.

---

##### Self-Check
- Three EBITDA haircuts run (−30/−40/−60%); cyclical-history scenario assessed and **not warranted** — HCG is "partly externally driven," non-cyclical demand, no commodity cycle, and FY23–FY26 EBITDA rose every year (no trough in the pool); the standard haircuts already over-span the demand downside [10 §3–4; earnings/06 §1]. WC-shock and rate-shock columns added. ✓
- Each scenario recomputes net leverage (incl. + ex-lease), coverage (lease-incl + cash-interest), covenant headroom (assumed + internal, breach Y/N), and the 12-month liquidity gap. ✓
- Base EBITDA is cash-backed (CFO/EBITDA 74.5%, cross-checked vs earnings/06; adjusted-vs-reported gap only +1.15%) — Reported EBITDA used, not headline adjusted. ✓
- Break points solved explicitly: assumed coverage breach ~−24%; assumed 3.5x leverage ~−26%; liquidity exhaustion ~−85% (overdraft rolled) / ~−22% (market closure, overdraft repaid, incl Vizag); internal 2.5x ceiling ~−68%; 6x incl-lease at ~−57%. ✓
- Each scenario states whether survival needs external action (waiver for the assumed covenant; no equity/asset sale needed to survive on liquidity unless the overdraft is pulled). ✓
- No probability assigned to the downside — left to the master synthesizer. ✓
- EBITDA base exists → stress run (downside resilience assessable). Partial-data cap propagated: **no lender covenant disclosed → covenant headroom "Not assessable" for scoring; all covenant breach points indicative** [04 §2; MODULE_RULES]. Revolver availability undisclosed → overdraft excluded from committed liquidity and the market-closure case treats it as repayable [03 §1]. ✓
- No banned phrases (every coverage, headroom, runway and leverage figure is stated as a number). ✓

---

# PART V — Management-Governance · FULL MODULE (synthesis + 7 specialist reports)

*Folder: `analyses/HCG_2026-06-01/management-governance/`. The module synthesis appears first (the module's own adjudicated verdict), followed by each specialist report in pipeline order, reproduced in full.*

## MG-99 · Module Synthesis — Management-Governance Module — HCG (Synthesis)

### Management-Governance Module — HCG (Synthesis)

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Indian SEBI-LODR issuer, Ind AS, FY ends 31 March. Reporting currency INR (₹). Synthesis date: 2026-06-01. This module is the governance deep-dive and SUPERSEDES the single `business-model/11_capital-allocation-governance` quick-read; the master synthesizer should treat THIS module's governance verdict and scores as the primary governance read. The most recent audited governance disclosure is the FY24-25 Integrated Annual Report (year ended 31-Mar-2025); FY26 is covered by the 20-May-2026 transcript and 19-May-2026 deck only. This report does not value HCG, assign probabilities, compute risk/reward, rate the stock, or size a position — those belong to the valuation module and the master synthesizer.*

#### Abstract

HCG is a competent oncology operator under new private-equity control, but it is not yet a demonstrated steward of per-share value, so the stewardship call is standard-to-mixed with weak per-share results. Over FY23–FY26 capital went almost entirely into growth capex (~68% of CFO) and goodwill-heavy M&A (Vizag was 60.7% goodwill), nothing was returned, and diluted EPS fell from ₹3.43 to ~₹1.0 even as EBITDA rose — value creation below EBITDA has been weak. Disclosed pay rewards revenue, absolute profit and margin (no ROCE/EPS/TSR metric), the current CEO's and CFO's pay is undisclosed, and skin in the game is a sponsor's cash-bought 50.84% block, not management conviction buying. The biggest governance signal is the omission of FY26 reported PAT (−69%, ₹138 Mn) from how management framed its worst year, alongside ~26% minority votes-against a promoter-funded bonus. No hard disqualifier; verdict: standard/mixed.

#### 1. Stewardship Verdict

- **Verdict:** Standard / mixed
- **Hard disqualifier flagged (business-model/01)?** N — `business-model/01_disqualifier-scan` triggered NONE of the eight hard disqualifiers (auditor qualification N, going concern N, promoter pledge >50% N, related-party >25% N at ~1%, repeated auditor changes N, material error-restatement N, regulatory enforcement N, negative-CFO N). Verdict is therefore NOT capped at "Serious governance concerns," and Governance risk is NOT floored at 80.
- Management quality /100: **46**
- Capital allocation /100: **45**
- Incentive alignment /100: **29** *(partial-data capped at max 50 — current-team pay/metrics undisclosed)*
- Shareholder friendliness /100: **73**
- Disclosure candor /100: **55**
- Governance risk /100 *(higher = WORSE — inverted)*: **52**
- Data quality /100: *(from 00)* **78** — triage verdict "Sufficient," all six specialists ran, no category gap; held below 90 by the FY26-audited-AR freshness gap, undisclosed current-team comp, and vendor-tier ownership data.
- Overall usefulness /100: **70** *(MODULE_RULES "no current-team comp disclosure" usefulness cap = max 70; binding)*
- Insider ownership (one line): KKR/Hector Asia holds **50.84%** (cash-bought via SPA + 26% open offer at ₹504.41/sh); founder Ajaikumar **~9.22%** (legacy pre-IPO, not added to nor sold); current CEO/CFO personal stake **not disclosed**; **no promoter pledge** anywhere [04: 04-001/002/003/010].
- Biggest governance signal (one line): Management omitted FY26 reported PAT (−69% YoY to ₹138 Mn) from its worst-year narrative and led only on adjusted EBITDA/PAT, while per-share value fell (EPS ₹3.43→~₹1.0) — the bottom-line miss that matters is obscured by emphasis [06: 06-001/015; 02: 02-017].
- **Governance Score /100:** **49** — `0.20×CapAlloc(45) + 0.18×Incentive(29) + 0.18×ShFriendliness(73) + 0.16×Candor(55) + 0.16×MgmtQuality(46) + 0.12×(100−GovRisk(52))` = `9.00 + 5.22 + 13.14 + 8.80 + 7.36 + 5.76 = 49.28 → 49`.
- **Confidence-Adjusted Governance Score /100:** **30** (= 49 × 62 / 100 = 30.55).
- **Governance Rating:** **Weak** (Governance Score 49 falls in the 40–54 "Weak" band).
- **Confidence Score /100:** **62** — audited FY24-25 AR evidence is confidence-5 across board/RPT/comp/ownership, but FY26 rests on transcript/deck (confidence 3), current-team comp and personal stakes are undisclosed, ownership uses a vendor export (confidence 2), and incentive alignment is partial-data capped.
- **Red-Flag Count / Critical Red-Flag Count:** **7 / 0** (seven High-severity flags; no Critical flag fired — see §5A).

#### 3. Reconciliation

The specialists do not contradict each other on facts, but they pull in different directions on the verdict, and the conservative reconciliation matters. The clearest tension is **ownership/board (70 / 73, "aligned in quantum / adequate protection") vs capital allocation, incentives and candor (45 / 29 / 55, weak-to-mixed)**. Ownership (04) rests on a 50.84% controlling block genuinely bought for cash, no pledging, a clean single-share-class structure and a priced minority exit; board (05) rests on independent-chaired audit and NRC committees, RPT <1% of revenue, and no royalty/brand/management-fee leakage. Capital allocation (02), incentives (03) and candor (06) rest on the opposite evidence: per-share value fell (EPS ₹3.43→~₹1.0), pay names no per-share metric, and the worst result was omitted from the narrative. **Reconciled view (the more conservative reading wins where they diverge):** HCG has clean governance *machinery* but a weak governance *outcome* — the structure protects minorities from theft (no leakage, no pledge, no dual-class), yet the people running it have not been shown to convert that clean structure into per-share value, and they manage the narrative around the one number (reported PAT) that exposes the gap. A second, smaller tension: agent 01 calls controls-function continuity Green (no auditor/CompSec change) while flagging CFO turnover Red — these reconcile cleanly (the *finance-leadership* seat churned three times; the *audit/compliance* seat did not), and the synthesis keeps both, treating the clean controls function as the reason this is instability rather than an integrity flag. A third item to reconcile across modules: the same Milann sale is logged by 02 as RF-RPT-002 (promoter-linked, High) and by 04 as a "monitor" item — the conservative treatment carries it as a triggered High RPT flag (promoter is the counterparty of a just-impaired asset), softened only by Board/Audit-Committee approval, not dismissed.

#### 4. Score Cap Application

| Cap Trigger | Applies? | Affected Score | Cap / Floor | Pre-Cap Score | Applied Result | Reason / Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---:|---|
| No proxy / compensation disclosure | **Partial-Y** | Incentive alignment; usefulness | Incentive max 50; usefulness max 70 | 29 / 70 | **29 / 70** | Proxy-equivalent (FY24-25 CG Report) IS present, but the *current* CEO/CFO pay and metric weights are undisclosed → 03 applied the "no current-team comp" cap. 29 sits below the 50 cap; usefulness held at the 70 cap (binding) [03 cap note; 00 §5] |
| No ownership / insider data | **N** | Shareholder friendliness | max 60 | 73 | **73 (uncapped)** | Ownership data IS present (AR shareholding pattern 71.22% + CIQ export + control-transfer terms) per triage §5 — cap does NOT bind; 73 stands [00 §5; 04] |
| No board disclosure | **N** | Board / shareholder-rights read | Not assessable / cap | 73 | **73 (assessable)** | Full board composition, committee independence, tenure, RPT and voting disclosure present in FY24-25 CG Report — board IS assessable [05; 00 §5B] |
| No multi-year capital-allocation history | **N** | Capital allocation | max 65 | 45 | **45 (uncapped)** | FY23-24 + FY24-25 audited ARs + CIQ multi-year financials + FY26 deck = a true 3-5yr scorecard; triage confirms "No multi-year history: N" — cap does NOT bind [00 §5; 02 cap note] |
| No prior promises / transcripts / letters | **N** | Management quality / disclosure candor | candor max 65 | 55 | **55 (uncapped)** | Four transcripts + CEO message + prior-year AR present; candor read off two full bad-news calls — cap does NOT bind (55 is below 65 on its own merits) [00 §5; 06 cap note] |
| Hard disqualifier flagged (business-model/01) | **N** | Governance risk / verdict | risk floor 80; verdict ≤ "Serious governance concerns" | 52 / Standard-mixed | **52 / Standard-mixed** | `business-model/01` triggered NONE of the eight disqualifiers — no floor, verdict not capped at "Serious governance concerns" [BM/01 §3 verdict-lock: "Any disqualifier triggered: N"] |
| Critical red flag triggered in this module | **N** | Governance rating / verdict | rating ≤ "Weak" until disproven | 49 / Weak | **49 / Weak** | Seven High-severity flags but ZERO Critical flags fired (§5A); the "Weak" rating arises from the composite score (49 in the 40-54 band), not from a Critical-flag cap |

Net effect of caps: only the **usefulness cap (max 70)** and the **incentive-alignment cap (max 50, where 29 < 50)** bind. No disqualifier floor, no Critical-flag rating lock. The "Weak" Governance Rating and "Standard / mixed" stewardship verdict are produced by the score formula and the verdict categories, not forced by a cap.

#### 5. Stewardship Summary

(a) **Delivery on promises:** the team reliably hits its operating promises — FY26 revenue ~15%, EBITDA +20.3%/+88 bps, leverage cut to 1.4x — but reliably misses below EBITDA: normalized EPS missed consensus in three of the last four fiscal years and reported PAT collapsed to ₹138 Mn in FY26 as EBITDA grew, so capital has not been converted to per-share value (diluted EPS ₹3.43→~₹1.0 over FY24–FY26), and the FY26 deleveraging that looks disciplined was funded by a ₹425 cr rights issue, not free cash flow [01-010/011/013/014; 02-017]. (b) **Where incentives and ownership point:** at size, not per-share value — the disclosed variable-pay metrics are revenue, absolute profit and operating margin with no ROCE/EPS/TSR trigger despite a public 20% ROCE target, and "skin in the game" is a private-equity sponsor's cash-bought 50.84% block on an exit clock rather than operators buying personally, with the current CEO/CFO holding no disclosed stake [03-005/007; 04-001/005]. (c) **Minority protection and candor:** minorities are protected from *theft* — RPTs are <1% of revenue, there is no royalty/brand/management fee to the promoter or KKR, no pledge, one share-class, and a priced open-offer exit — but they are not protected from *narrative management*, since the worst figure (reported PAT) was omitted from how the year was sold, an operating KPI (occupancy) was removed under the new team, and ~26% of minorities voted against a promoter-funded bonus [05-010/011/017; 06-001/011]. (d) **The single most important reason to be cautious with this team's stewardship:** clean governance machinery has not yet produced per-share value, and management manages the disclosure around exactly the line where that gap shows — so the trust question is competence-and-candor under a brand-new PE-installed team, not integrity (the audit is unmodified and controls are clean).

#### 5B. Peer Governance Benchmark

`business-model/08_competitive-map` provides a peer set (Apollo Hospitals, Max Healthcare, Narayana Hrudayalaya, Fortis, plus the closest like-for-like AOI which is private), but it explicitly flags that **no governance-metric dataset for these peers is in the pool** (no peer board-independence %, no peer pledge %, no peer non-audit-fee ratio, no peer RPT intensity, no peer AGM votes-against), and agent 03 could not retrieve a usable peer compensation figure. Relative governance is therefore **largely not assessable** from the data; the cells below are populated only where HCG's own figure exists, with peer columns marked "Not in pool."

| Metric | Company (HCG) | Peer Median | Peer Verdict |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Board independence % | 50% (4 of 8; at LODR floor for a non-independent chair) | Not in pool | Not assessed |
| Insider / promoter holding % | 71.22% audited (FY25); KKR 50.84% + founder ~9.22% post-changeover | Not in pool | Not assessed |
| Pledge % | 0% (none disclosed) | Not in pool | Not assessed |
| Non-audit / audit fee ratio | Not separately extracted by specialists (no flag raised) | Not in pool | Not assessed |
| RPT intensity (% of revenue) | ~1% consolidated (BRSR 0.0012% of sales) | Not in pool | Not assessed |
| AGM votes-against (key resolutions) | 25.99% against the KMP-bonus resolution; <4% against all routine items | Not in pool | Not assessed |

Net: **No peer governance benchmark available — relative governance not assessed.** The single highest-value comparator to acquire is MD/CEO remuneration and board-independence data for Apollo, Max and Narayana, to test whether HCG's size-based pay design and floor-level independence are sector norms or company-specific.

#### 5C. Governance Change Since Last Run

No prior dated run exists for this ticker (`analyses/HCG_{prior-date}/management-governance/` not found) — **this is the first governance snapshot.** The deltas below are therefore not run-over-run; they record the within-pool change across the FY25→FY26 / control-transition window, for the next run to baseline against.

| Item | Prior (FY25 / pre-changeover) | Current (FY26 / post-changeover) | Change | Good / Bad | Material? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Board / KMP changes | CVC-nominee board; founder Exec Chairman; CEO Gore; CFO Ritolia | KKR-nominee board (10 dirs); founder Non-Exec Chairman; CEO Mattoo; CFO Saxena incoming | Near-total C-suite/board turnover; 3rd CFO in 33mo | Mixed (orderly/disclosed but discontinuous) | Yes |
| Promoter holding / pledge | CVC/Aceso 60.40% + founder 10.41%; no pledge | KKR/Hector 50.84% + founder 9.22%; no pledge disclosed (FY26 Reg-31 not in pool) | Controller changed CVC→KKR; both diluted by rights issue | Neutral; pledge still 0% | Yes (control change) |
| RPT intensity | ~1% of revenue; no leakage | ~1%; + new promoter-linked Milann sale (RF-RPT-002) | Stable intensity; one new conflicted exit | Mixed | Medium |
| New legal / regulatory items | None (BRSR penalties N/A) | None (no enforcement; routine LODR cover headings only) | No change | Good | No |
| New AGM opposition | n/a in pool | 25.99% against promoter-funded KMP bonus (Apr-2025) | New minority dissent signal | Bad | Medium |
| CFO / PAT (from earnings) | FY25 PAT ₹444m; CFO/EBITDA 81.9% | FY26 PAT ₹138m; CFO/EBITDA ~74.5% | PAT −69%; conversion eroding | Bad | High |

Movement on the governance score: the FY26 PAT collapse + the conversion erosion + the new promoter-linked RPT + the 26% votes-against all push the score *down* vs a clean prior baseline; the control transition itself is neutral-to-slightly-negative (orderly and disclosed, but it resets the track record and installs an exit-clocked owner). **What to investigate next:** the FY26 audited Annual Report — specifically current-team pay/metrics, whether occupancy/ARPOB returns, the Vizag CGU ROCE, the Milann fairness opinion, and the post-changeover Reg-31 encumbrance line.

#### 5D. Analyst Follow-Up Questions

- **RF-MGT-001 (CFO turnover, Red):** Was each of the three CFO changes orderly (retirement / term-completion / owner-change) rather than concern-driven, and what are incoming CFO Saxena's specific prior employers (currently Insufficient Data)? Is the finance function company-specific unstable or a normal PE-transition artefact?
- **RF-SHR-001 (25.99% votes-against, Red):** What was the quantum, recipient list and metric of the promoter-funded KMP bonus, did a proxy advisor (IiAS/InGovern) recommend against, and did the sponsor-funded plan create a two-tier incentive that disadvantages minority holders? Recurring or one-off?
- **RF-CAP-001 (Vizag M&A + minority puts, Red):** Is there any disclosed IRR/synergy target for Vizag to test the ~2.9% pre-integration return against, and when do the ~₹3,318m bank-dependent put/forward obligations crystallize relative to FY27 capex (management itself "cannot assure" the funding)? Material to cash and valuation.
- **RF-RPT-002 (Milann to founder's fund, Red):** Was there an independent fairness opinion and competitive-process evidence beyond management's assertion, and were terms arm's-length? Affects minority holders directly (Chairman is the counterparty of a just-impaired asset).
- **RF-FIN-004 (goodwill impairment, Red):** Is further goodwill at risk (Indore ₹416.89m, CCC ₹484.52m CGUs), or was the ₹319m Milann write-down a contained one-off? Material to reported earnings.
- **RF-DISC-001 (PAT omission / narrative, Red):** Does management state reported PAT and acknowledge the EPS-miss pattern once the FY26 exceptionals lapse — i.e., is the omission a one-off framing artefact of a noisy year or a durable communication style? Re-test on the FY27 calls.
- **RF-DISC-002 (recurring "one-off" add-backs, Red):** Will the labour-code provision and the goodwill impairment both genuinely not recur, and does the FY26 audited AR reconcile to the ₹557m adjusted PAT? Strip the labour-code add-back from any clean run-rate.
- **Amber items to monitor:** occupancy/ARPOB KPI restoration (06-011), the +22.5% Chairman pay raise recurrence now that he is Non-Executive (03-003), overboarded independents Mathur/Kanakia attendance (05-007), CFO/EBITDA conversion recovery vs working-capital drain (06-005), and the KKR exit-timeline / Promoter-Agreement inter-se terms (04-014/015).

#### 6. What Would Change The Stewardship Verdict?

| Current Verdict | What Would Strengthen It | What Would Weaken It | Data Needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard / mixed** | (1) FY26 AR discloses current-CEO/CFO pay tied to a ROCE/EPS/TSR metric; (2) FY27 shows EPS/per-share value rising toward the 20% ROCE path with leverage held <2.5x on FCF (not equity); (3) Vizag CGU earns above a stated hurdle and no further goodwill impairs; (4) management states reported PAT and restores the occupancy KPI; (5) the new team stabilises (no further CFO change) — would support an upgrade toward **Aligned & competent** | (1) A further CFO/CompSec/auditor exit or an independent-director resignation citing concerns; (2) the FY26 Reg-31 reveals the KKR block is encumbered (pledge); (3) the Milann sale closes without an independent fairness opinion; (4) RPTs/royalty/management fees to the promoter or KKR appear; (5) another goodwill impairment or a delayed/restated result — would push toward **Misaligned or weak stewardship**, and any enforcement/restatement toward **Serious governance concerns** | The FY2025-26 audited Annual Report + post-KKR Reg-31(1) shareholding-and-encumbrance pattern + FY26 Corporate Governance Report (current-team remuneration/metrics, board %, occupancy KPI, Vizag CGU ROCE, Milann fairness opinion, pledge line) — the single highest-value document |

#### 7. Note To The Final Synthesizer

- **Stewardship verdict + strongest evidence:** Standard / mixed — competent oncology operators with clean governance machinery who have not been shown to create per-share value; the strongest single piece of evidence is diluted EPS falling ₹3.43→~₹1.0 (FY24→FY26) while EBITDA grew, the spending landing below EBITDA as D&A, finance cost and a goodwill impairment [02-017].
- **Capital-allocation record (per-share):** value-destructive-to-flat on a per-share basis over FY23–FY26 — ~68% of CFO into capex, ~33% into goodwill-heavy M&A (Vizag 60.7% goodwill), zero returned, and the FY26 deleveraging was equity-funded (₹425 cr rights issue), not FCF; mature centres earn ~25% ROCE but the blend is 14% vs a 20% target [02-001/004/012/015/017].
- **Incentives/ownership alignment with minorities:** weak-to-mixed — disclosed pay names NO per-share/returns metric (only revenue/profit/margin), current-team pay is undisclosed, and skin in the game is a sponsor's cash-bought 50.84% block on an exit clock, not management conviction; minorities are protected from leakage but pay is pointed at size [03-005/007; 04-001/005].
- **Biggest governance risk / red flag + severity:** the candor cluster — RF-DISC-001 (High): the FY26 reported PAT collapse (−69%) and the 4-year EPS-miss pattern omitted from the worst-year narrative while management leads on adjusted figures; paired with RF-SHR-001 (High, 25.99% minority votes-against a promoter-funded bonus). No Critical flag.
- **Hard disqualifier (business-model/01), verbatim:** "Any disqualifier triggered: N … None … No disqualifier triggers. The synthesizer is NOT required to lock the verdict." No audit qualification, going concern, >50% pledge, >25% RPT, repeated auditor change, error-restatement, or enforcement.
- **Partial-data caps applied:** the "no current-team comp" cap binds — Incentive alignment held within max 50 (actual 29) and Overall usefulness held at max 70; it limits the alignment read for the people actually running HCG today (Mattoo/Saxena pay and metrics are undisclosed). No disqualifier floor, no multi-year-history cap, no candor cap.
- **Biggest missing data point (single highest-value next request):** the FY2025-26 audited Annual Report + post-KKR Reg-31(1) shareholding-and-encumbrance pattern + FY26 Corporate Governance Report — it would fill current-team pay/metrics, board %, the occupancy KPI, the Vizag CGU ROCE, the Milann fairness opinion, and the KKR-block pledge line in one document.
- **Explicit handoff:** this module is the governance deep-dive and **SUPERSEDES** the `business-model/11_capital-allocation-governance` quick-read; the master synthesizer should treat THIS module's governance verdict ("Standard / mixed"), Governance Score (49 / Weak), Confidence-Adjusted Score (30), and the seven High red flags as the **primary governance read**, not the business-model quick-read.

#### 8. Simple Summary

- Management hits its revenue and EBITDA promises but misses the bottom line — EPS missed Street in 3 of the last 4 years and FY26 reported profit fell to ₹138m even as EBITDA grew.
- Capital has destroyed-to-flat per-share value: EPS fell from ₹3.43 to ~₹1.0; cash went into capex and goodwill-heavy acquisitions, nothing was returned, and the FY26 deleveraging was paid for with a rights issue, not cash flow.
- Pay rewards size, not per-share value — the disclosed bonus metrics are revenue, profit and margin, with no ROCE/EPS/TSR trigger; the current CEO's and CFO's pay is not disclosed.
- Skin in the game is a KKR private-equity block (50.84%, bought for cash) plus the founder's legacy ~9.22%; there is no insider open-market buying (no conviction signal) and no selling (no red flag), and no shares are pledged.
- Minority shareholders are protected from theft — RPTs are ~1% of revenue, no royalty/management fees to the owner, one share-class, a priced exit — but cannot outvote a ~51–71% control bloc, and 26% of them voted against a promoter-funded bonus.
- Management is candid about operating bad news (the AP strike, the Q4 revenue dip) but NOT about the bottom line — it omitted reported PAT from its worst-year pitch and dropped the occupancy KPI when an analyst asked for it.
- No hard disqualifier: clean unmodified audit, no auditor/compliance-officer resignation, no pledge, no enforcement, no error-restatement.
- This module is useful for the master synthesizer (usefulness 70, the partial-data ceiling), and it supersedes the business-model governance quick-read — but the verdict on the team actually in charge is provisional because they have 0–11 months in seat and their pay is undisclosed.

## MG-00 · governance-data-triage — Governance Data Triage — HCG

### Governance Data Triage — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG, BSE: 539787). Indian SEBI-LODR issuer, Ind AS, fiscal year ends 31 March. Healthcare (oncology hospital network) sector. Reporting currency INR. Date of triage: 2026-06-01. The proxy-equivalent under SEBI-LODR is the AGM Notice + Corporate Governance Report; ownership is the shareholding-pattern filing; compensation is the Board's Report / CG Report. US form names (DEF 14A, 10-K, Form 4) are NOT used as the missing-data test — local equivalents govern.*

This agent runs first and decides one thing: is there enough data to assess management quality, capital allocation, incentives, ownership, board, and candor? It does not score management or judge whether the team is good or bad — later agents do that.

#### 1. File Inventory

PDF filenames in the pool are opaque UUIDs; "Type" is established by reading the cover letter / first pages of each.

#### 6. Sufficiency Verdict

- **Verdict:** Sufficient
- **Reason:** A proxy-equivalent (FY24-25 AGM Notice + Corporate Governance Report), ownership data (AR shareholding pattern + CIQ ownership exports), board/related-party disclosure, and a multi-year capital-allocation history (FY23-24 + FY24-25 ARs, CIQ financials, FY26 deck/transcript) are all present, so all six specialists can run.
- **Specialists that can run:** management track record (01), capital allocation (02), incentives (03), ownership (04), board (05), candor (06) — all six.
- **Hard disqualifier already flagged by business-model/01_disqualifier-scan?** N — no disqualifier triggered (audit qualification N, going concern N, promoter pledge >50% N/none disclosed, related-party >25% N at ~1%, repeated auditor changes N, material error-restatement N, regulatory enforcement N). Note for synthesis: a full change of controlling shareholder (CVC/Aceso → Hector Asia / KKR ~51.59%, 30-May-2025), founder reclassification, near-total C-suite/board turnover, and a 26% open offer are the defining governance facts — all disclosed and SEBI-LODR-compliant, flagged by business-model/11 and 12 as governance red flags (control/management discontinuity), not as a hard disqualifier.
- **Active partial-data caps:** None (verdict is Sufficient, not Partial).
- **Critical missing items:** None at the category level. Two sub-item gaps to carry forward, neither cap-triggering: (1) AGM voting *outcomes* / votes-against (resolutions are present, results are not); (2) the post-KKR-changeover FY26 audited annual report / Reg-31 shareholding pattern / FY26 CG Report (FY26 governed by transcript + deck only).
- **Single highest-value missing document:** the FY2025-26 Corporate Governance Report + post-changeover Reg-31(1) detailed shareholding/encumbrance disclosure (the first audited governance, ownership, and remuneration picture under KKR control).

## MG-01 · management-and-track-record — Management & Track Record — HCG

### Management & Track Record — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Indian SEBI-LODR issuer, Ind AS, FY ends 31 March. Reporting currency INR (₹). Report date: 2026-06-01. Proxy-equivalent = AGM Notice + Corporate Governance Report; KMP/management changes are disclosed in the Board's Report (§19/§21) and 8-K-equivalent exchange announcements/transcripts. Most recent audited governance disclosure is the FY2024-25 Integrated Annual Report (year ended 31-Mar-2025, filed Sep-2025); FY26 (year ended 31-Mar-2026) is covered by the 20-May-2026 earnings transcript and 19-May-2026 deck only — the FY26 audited Annual Report / CG Report is not in the pool.*

Scope: this agent answers who runs HCG, whether they are stable, and whether they delivered on what they promised. It does not score capital allocation (02), incentives (03), ownership (04), the board (05), or candor (06) in detail, and it does not value the company or rate the stock. Cross-module inputs used: `business-model/02_business-identity.md` (control context), `business-model/11_capital-allocation-governance.md` (turnover/control facts), and `earnings/04_guidance-consensus.md` + `earnings/99_earnings-synthesis.md` (guidance and beat/miss record). All three were available.

**One fact governs this entire read:** the people running HCG today are almost all new, installed by a new controlling owner. KKR (via Hector Asia Holdings II Pte Ltd) acquired sole control (~51.59%) on 30-May-2025; a KKR-nominee CEO took over on 30-Jun-2025; a third CFO in ~33 months joins at end-May 2026; and the founder moved to a Non-Executive role. The current configuration has roughly 0–11 months of tenure together, so its own promise-vs-delivery record is mostly not yet observable. The checkable record below belongs largely to the *prior* (CVC-era) management and to the company across owners.

#### 1. Key Executives

| Role | Name | Appointed | Tenure (as of 2026-06-01) | Relevant Background | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Executive Director & CEO | Dr. Manish Mattoo | 30-Jun-2025 (KKR nominee) | ~11 months | ~20+ yrs healthcare; ex-Regional CEO Apollo Hospitals (Karnataka & Central, 2,000+ beds), prior VP Fortis Healthcare, leadership roles at Medtronic and Monitor Group; ISB MBA (Finance + Strategy), MBBS + MD (Anaesthesiology) | FY24-25 AR, Board profiles p.38-39; Directors' Report §19.4(d)/§21(c), p.78-79; Q4/FY26 transcript, intro (20-May-2026) |
| CFO (incoming) | Sanjeev Kumar Saxena | Announced 20-May-2026; joining end-May 2026 | <1 month (not yet in seat at the 20-May-2026 call) | Stated to bring "relevant experience in healthcare services"; specific prior employers not disclosed in the pool | Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks lines 118-121 (20-May-2026) — *background detail Insufficient Data* |
| CFO (FY25 incumbent / outgoing) | Ms. Ruby Ritolia | 21-Aug-2023 | ~2.8 yrs (being succeeded) | Chartered Accountant; previously at Marico | FY24-25 AR, Note 50 p.6382 + remuneration p.6369; FY23-24 AR p.5655, p.5753 |
| CFO (prior) | Mr. Srinivasa V. Raghavan | Ceased 20-Aug-2023 (retirement) | — | Long-serving CFO; departed on retirement | FY23-24 AR, Board's Report p.5646; FY24-25 AR Note 50, p.19392 |
| Founder / Non-Executive Chairman | Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar | Founder; CEO Jul-2006→Jan-2021; Exec Chairman → Non-Exec Chairman w.e.f. 30-May-2025 (term to 30-Jun-2030) | Founder since inception; ceased KMP 30-May-2025 | Founder of HCG; practicing radiation oncologist (US + India, 30+ yrs); MD Anderson + Univ. of Virginia residency; MBBS St. John's, Bengaluru; E&Y Entrepreneur of the Year | FY24-25 AR, Board profiles p.38; Directors' Report §19.4(c)/§21(a), p.78-79; CEO-tenure dates p.2516 |
| Company Secretary & Compliance Officer | Ms. Sunu Manuel | In seat through FY25 (no change disclosed) | Stable across the changeover | KMP per Companies Act §203 | FY24-25 AR, §21 KMP list, p.79 |

**Founder / owner-operator status:** HCG is **no longer founder-controlled or owner-operator-run.** It is a private-equity-controlled company. Founder Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar built and ran HCG (CEO 2006-2021) but sold control to CVC (via Aceso) in 2020 and was reclassified from Executive to Non-Executive Chairman on 30-May-2025, ceasing to be a KMP [FY24-25 AR, §21(a), p.79]. Control then passed from CVC to KKR's Hector Asia (~51.59%) on 30-May-2025 [FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §15, p.76-77]. The CEO (Mattoo), incoming CFO (Saxena), and key directors (Mehta, Tanna) are KKR appointees. This is a sponsor-run operating company, not a manager-owner.

#### 2. Management Stability

| Signal | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Senior departures (last 3 years) | High churn around two control changes: CFO Raghavan ceased 20-Aug-2023 (retirement); IndependentDirector Jeyandran Venugopal resigned 09-Nov-2024 ("professional and personal commitments"); CEO Meghraj Gore's WTD term ended 09-Feb-2025 and he exited as CEO 30-Jun-2025; both CVC-nominee directors (Siddharth Patel, Amit Soni) resigned 30-May-2025 on the KKR SPA; CFO Ruby Ritolia being succeeded (FY26). Founder reclassified Executive→Non-Executive 30-May-2025. | FY24-25 AR, Board's Report §19.3-19.4, §21, p.78-79; Q4/FY26 transcript (20-May-2026) |
| CFO turnover | **Three CFOs in ~33 months.** Raghavan (until 20-Aug-2023, retirement) → Ritolia (from 21-Aug-2023) → Saxena (announced 20-May-2026, joining end-May-2026). The first change was a retirement; the third coincides with the KKR ownership change. This is the single clearest stability warning. | FY23-24 AR p.5646/5655; FY24-25 AR Note 50 p.19392; Q4/FY26 transcript lines 118-121 |
| CEO succession / continuity | Discontinuous, not orderly internal succession. Founder-CEO (to Jan-2021) → Meghraj Gore (WTD 10-Feb-2022; CEO to 30-Jun-2025) → Dr. Manish Mattoo (KKR nominee, Exec Director & CEO from 30-Jun-2025). Two CEO handovers in ~4.5 years, the latest owner-driven. Gore's exit is disclosed as completion of a 3-year WTD term, not a stated dispute. | FY24-25 AR, §19.4(b)/(d), §21(b)/(c), p.78-79; founder CEO dates p.2516 |
| Other C-suite / board build-out (FY26) | New layer being installed under KKR: Ravi Gothwal (Head of IR), Vinayak Nayak (Head of International Sales), plus "regional and other corporate leadership additions… throughout the year"; two KKR nominee directors (Simrun Mehta, Akshay Tanna) and a new Independent Director (Bijou Kurien, 30-Jun-2025) added. | Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks lines 118-122, 255; FY24-25 AR §19.4(b)/(e), p.78 |

**Warning flagged:** CFO turnover (three CFOs in ~33 months) and near-complete C-suite/board turnover concentrated around the May-2025 control change are a clear instability signal. Mitigants visible in the data: (1) every change is disclosed and SEBI-LODR/Companies-Act compliant — orderly PE transition, not concealment; (2) the first CFO change was a retirement and the CEO change is framed as a completed fixed term; (3) the statutory auditor (B S R & Co. LLP) and Company Secretary (Sunu Manuel) did **not** turn over — there is no auditor or compliance-officer resignation, which are the more severe red-flag triggers. The instability is concentrated in owner-driven executive and PE-nominee-director seats, not in the controls function.

#### 3. Stated Strategy & Targets

Management's stated plan under the new (KKR-installed) CEO is **profitable, return-focused growth on the existing oncology network rather than top-line chasing**: deepen utilisation of the 25-hospital / ~58%-occupancy base, shift case- and payor-mix toward higher-realization work, add brownfield capacity, and exit the non-core Milann fertility business (sale agreed May-2026) [Q4/FY26 transcript, prepared remarks + Q&A; business-model/02]. The explicit, checkable targets it has set: (a) sustained revenue growth of "~15%," with a "15-20%" range cited; (b) EBITDA margin expansion of "~100 bps" in FY27 (off FY26's 18.5% adjusted), and EBITDA growing faster than its historical ~18% CAGR; (c) a 5-year **pre-tax ROCE target of "at least 20%+"** (FY26 actual 14%); (d) a **net-debt/EBITDA internal ceiling of 2.5x** (currently 1.4x); and (e) "200+ beds" of brownfield capacity over the next 24 months plus 8-10 day-care centres [earnings/04_guidance-consensus §2; Dec-04-2025 Investor & Analyst Meet transcript; Q4/FY26 transcript]. HCG does **not** give formal point/range revenue, EBITDA, or EPS guidance — targets are qualitative/directional.

#### 4. Promise vs Delivery

The checkable record below is mostly the **prior (CVC-era) team's** and the company's across owners; the current CEO/CFO configuration has too little tenure to have a self-contained delivery record (its first measurable test windows — FY26 margin and FY27 growth — are noted but largely forward).

| Prior Promise / Target | Period Set | Actual Outcome | Kept / Missed | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sustain "~15%" revenue growth | Dec-2025 strategy meet / reiterated FY26 | FY26 revenue ₹25,454 Mn, **+~15% YoY** (+14.5% per earnings synthesis) | **Kept** (top line) | Dec-04-2025 transcript Q&A; Q4/FY26 transcript Q&A (line 245); earnings/99 §1 |
| EBITDA to grow faster than ~18% historical CAGR; margin to expand | Dec-2025 / FY26 calls | FY26 reported EBITDA **+20.3% YoY**, +88 bps reported margin (Adjusted EBITDA 18.5%, +68 bps) | **Kept** (EBITDA growth/margin direction) | earnings/99 §1; business-model/02; Q4/FY26 deck |
| Cut leverage toward the 2.5x net-debt/EBITDA ceiling | FY26 (post rights issue) | Net debt/EBITDA cut from ~2.2-2.3x to **1.4x** after the ₹425 cr rights issue + debt repayment | **Kept** (delevered below ceiling — but via equity raise, not FCF) | Q4/FY26 transcript Q&A (lines 290-293); business-model/11 |
| Complete the rights issue / Milann divestiture (portfolio focus) | FY26 | ₹425 cr rights issue completed; Milann sale agreed May-2026 (close expected Q1 FY27) | **Kept** (executed as stated) | Q4/FY26 transcript prepared remarks; business-model/02 |
| Street's normalized-EPS bar (proxy for bottom-line delivery vs expectations) | FY23-FY26 (annual) | **Missed normalized-EPS consensus in 3 of the last 4 fiscal years** (FY23 −19.2%, FY25 −24.5%, FY26 −8.9%; only FY24 beat). Revenue/EBITDA landed within ~1.5% each year. | **Mostly Missed** (bottom line); kept on revenue/EBITDA | earnings/04 §6 (CIQ Surprise); earnings/99 §1 |
| FY26 reported PAT trajectory | Implied by growth narrative | Reported PAT **fell to ₹138 Mn** (from ₹444 Mn FY25, ₹482 Mn FY24); FY26 adjusted PAT differs from reported by +305% | **Missed** (reported earnings deteriorated despite EBITDA growth) | earnings/99 §1, Specialist Roll-Up; earnings/06 |
| 5-year pre-tax ROCE "20%+" (FY26 14%) | Dec-2025 (5-yr horizon) | Not yet testable (long-dated) | **Pending — not assessable** | Dec-04-2025 transcript; FY26 ROCE Q4/FY26 transcript |

**Read of the record:** the company reliably hits its *revenue and EBITDA* lines (FY26 ~15% / +88 bps; revenue and EBITDA within ~1.5% of consensus every year for four years) but reliably **misses the bottom line** — normalized EPS missed consensus in three of the last four years, and reported FY26 PAT collapsed to ₹138 Mn even as EBITDA grew 20%. The misses sit *below* EBITDA (D&A, finance cost, goodwill-heavy acquisitions, exceptionals), which is exactly where serial M&A and rising debt land. The FY26 deleveraging "promise" was kept, but through a ₹425 cr equity raise, not free cash flow. So: directional operating promises kept; per-share/earnings promises mostly missed; the highest-value target (20%+ ROCE) is unproven and 6 percentage points away.

#### 5. Management Read

HCG is a private-equity-controlled (KKR) oncology operator whose entire top team is new: a CEO with ~11 months in seat (ex-Apollo regional CEO), the **third CFO in ~33 months** joining end-May-2026, and the founder moved to a non-executive role — so the *current* team's promise-vs-delivery record barely exists yet and the prior multi-year record belongs to a CVC-era management that has been replaced. On the record that is checkable, the company keeps its revenue and EBITDA promises (FY26 ~15% growth, +88 bps margin, delevered to 1.4x) but has **missed the normalized-EPS bar in three of the last four fiscal years** and saw FY26 reported PAT fall to ₹138 Mn, so promises are best described as **kept at the operating line, missed below it.** The single biggest management signal is the combination of two control changes in five years and near-total C-suite turnover (CFO three times) — a clean controls function (no auditor/CompSec change, unmodified audit) keeps this from being a fraud/integrity flag, but it resets the meaning of any track record and makes execution durability under KKR the open question; the read is **partially capped because there is no checkable delivery record for the team actually in charge today.**

#### Management Quality Score

*Higher = better. Partial-data cap applied (see note below the table): the team actually in charge today has 0-11 months of tenure and no self-contained delivery record, so promise-vs-delivery and execution are scored on the company's/prior-team's checkable record and the early FY26 evidence, with the unobservable portion marked Insufficient Data and the components capped accordingly.*

| Component | Score | Max Score | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Stability and tenure | 7 | 20 | Three CFOs in ~33 months [01-003]; near-total C-suite/board churn across two control changes in 5 yrs [01-006]; CEO ~11 months [01-001]; founder out of executive role [01-005]. Partly offset by a stable controls function (no auditor/CompSec resignation) [01-007] and changes being orderly/disclosed. Low score reflects discontinuity, not misconduct. |
| Promise-vs-delivery | 14 | 30 | Operating promises kept: FY26 revenue ~15% [01-010], EBITDA +20.3%/+88 bps [01-011], delevered to 1.4x [01-012]. Bottom-line promises missed: normalized-EPS missed consensus 3 of 4 FYs [01-013], reported PAT fell to ₹138 Mn [01-014]. The kept items belong partly to the prior team and the deleveraging used equity, not FCF. Net mixed-to-negative. |
| Execution record | 13 | 25 | Executes operationally (revenue/EBITDA within ~1.5% of consensus each year; rights issue + Milann divestiture done as stated [01-010/012]). But execution converts poorly below EBITDA (recurring EPS miss, ₹138 Mn PAT) and serial goodwill-heavy M&A is the proximate cause (cross-ref BM 11). Current team's execution is largely unproven [01-016]. |
| Succession / depth | 6 | 15 | Succession has been owner-driven and discontinuous, not internal-bench [01-008]; finance leadership especially unstable [01-003]. New depth is being built (IR head, International Sales head, regional leaders) but is brand-new and unproven; bench quality not demonstrated. |
| Transparency around misses | 6 | 10 | Changes, departures, and the control transition are fully disclosed and SEBI-LODR-compliant [01-006/007]; CEO answers tenure/strategy questions directly. But the bottom-line misses run through adjusted-vs-reported gaps (+305%) [01-014] and the candor read is owned by agent 06 — scored conservatively pending that. |
| Total | 46 | 100 | Mixed band. Operating delivery and clean disclosure pull up; CFO/leadership instability, the recurring below-EBITDA miss, and the absence of a checkable record for the current team pull down. |

**Partial-data cap applied:** Per MODULE_RULES Partial-Data Rule and the triage's freshness flag, the team in charge today has no multi-year delivery record under its current configuration, and the only audited governance disclosure is FY24-25 (pre-changeover). Promise-vs-delivery and execution are therefore scored on the company's checkable cross-owner record plus early FY26 results; the portion attributable specifically to the current CEO/CFO is marked **Insufficient Data** [01-016, 01-004, 01-015]. The Management Quality total (46) should be read as **provisional**, to be re-tested after the FY26 audited Annual Report and 2-4 quarters under Mattoo/Saxena.
