---

## MG-02 · capital-allocation-scorecard — Capital-Allocation Scorecard — HCG

### Capital-Allocation Scorecard — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Indian SEBI-LODR issuer, Ind AS, FY ends 31 March. Reporting currency: INR (₹), consolidated. Report date: 2026-06-01. This module is the deep-dive behind the `business-model/11_capital-allocation-governance` quick-read and supersedes it where they overlap. It does not value the company or rate the stock.*

**Window:** A true 3–5 year scorecard is feasible — FY23, FY24 and FY25 are audited (FY23-24 + FY24-25 Annual Reports), and FY26 is from the board-approved audited-results presentation (19-May-2026) and the 20-May-2026 earnings call; FY22 levels are available only above the EBITDA line from a multi-year chart. Per the governance data triage, **no multi-year-history cap binds** [00_governance-data-triage §5]. One freshness caveat: the FY26 audited statutory statements and the FY26 Corporate Governance Report are **not** in the pool, so FY26 capital-allocation figures are cited to the results deck/transcript, not a full statutory filing — confidence on FY26 rows is set at 3 accordingly.

**Definitions used:** FCF = CFO − total capex (PP&E acquisition), per CLAUDE.md §15. Net debt is shown on two bases the company itself reports — company-defined ex-lease and balance-sheet incl. leases — and labelled in each row. ROCE figures quoted are the company's own "pre-Ind AS, post corporate-cost allocation, excluding fertility" basis [FY26 deck p.5]. Incremental ROIC is computed below from audited NOPAT and invested-capital change.

**The one fact that frames every row.** Control of HCG changed hands twice in five years (Founder → CVC/Aceso 2020 → KKR/Hector Asia ~51.59%, completed 30-May-2025) and almost the entire senior team turned over with it (KKR-nominee CEO from 30-Jun-2025; third CFO in ~33 months joining end-May-2026) [01_management-and-track-record §1–2; FY24-25 AR Directors' Report §15, p.76-77]. So the multi-year capital-allocation record below is largely the **prior (CVC-era) team's**, while the FY26 moves (rights issue, deleveraging, the Milann exit) are the **first acts of the new KKR-installed team**. The two must be read as separate regimes, not one continuous track record.

---

#### 1. Uses of Capital (3–5 years)

Reporting currency: **INR million (₹ Mn), consolidated.** Period: FY23–FY26 (four years; the longest span with usable below-EBITDA detail). Cumulative CFO over FY23–FY26 = 2,516 + 2,846 + 3,171 + 3,471 = **₹12,004 Mn**. Cumulative FCF (CFO − capex) = 1,184 + 989 + 1,082 + 586 = **₹3,841 Mn**. Both denominators are shown because HCG's uses of capital exceeded internally generated cash and were funded partly by debt and, in FY26, by equity.

| Use | Cumulative Amount (FY23–FY26) | Currency | % of CFO (₹12,004 Mn) | % of FCF (₹3,841 Mn) | Source |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---|
| Acquisitions (net of cash; cash actually paid) | ~3,914 | ₹ Mn | ~33% | ~102% | FY24-25 AR Note 45.1 (Vizag, net cash ₹1,971.95m, p.373-374); FY23-24 AR Note 45.2 (Indore ₹416.89m goodwill); CIQ Financials.xls cash-flow "acquisitions" rows |
| Buybacks | 0 | ₹ Mn | 0% | 0% | FY24-25 AR Note 15.1 share reconciliation, p.258; FY23-24 AR — no buyback line; FY26 deck cash flow p.22 (no buyback) |
| Dividends | 0 | ₹ Mn | 0% | 0% | FY24-25 AR Consolidated Cash Flow (no dividend line), p.314; FY26 deck cash flow p.22 |
| Capex (PP&E acquisition, growth + maintenance) | 8,163 | ₹ Mn | ~68% | ~213% | earnings/01 §1 (FY23 1,332; FY24 1,857; FY25 2,089; FY26 2,885); FY26 deck p.14 |
| Debt repayment / (drawdown), net | Net **drawdown** through FY25, then FY26 repayment | ₹ Mn | n/m | n/m | FY24-25 AR debt reconciliation p.315 (total debt +~33% to ₹15,542.87m incl. leases in FY25); FY26 deck p.14-15 (borrowings ex-lease cut ₹9,814m→₹8,817m) |
| Equity raised (rights issue, FY26) | (4,250) *inflow* | ₹ Mn | — | — | FY26 deck p.14-16 ("right issue… INR 4,250 Mn… oversubscribed 1.3x"); FY26 call line 118 |

**Read of the mix.** Over FY23–FY26 HCG put effectively **all** of its internally generated cash, plus borrowings, into two buckets — capex (~68% of CFO; ~213% of FCF) and acquisitions (~33% of CFO; ~102% of FCF) — and returned **nothing** to shareholders (no dividend, no buyback). Capex plus acquisitions (~₹12,077 Mn cash) roughly equals four years of CFO (₹12,004 Mn) and is ~3.1× four-year FCF, which is why net debt rose ~33% into FY25 and why a ₹4,250 Mn rights issue was needed in FY26 to delever. This is a **reinvestment-and-acquisition** capital model, not a return-of-capital one; the per-share test below is therefore the whole game.

---

#### 2. M&A Scorecard

HCG is a serial acquirer of oncology hospitals and diagnostics. The material recent deals and their disclosed economics:

| Deal | Year | Price | Disclosed Rationale / Synergy | Delivered? | Source |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|
| **Vizag — Vizag Hospital & Cancer Research Centre Pvt Ltd (VHCRPL), 51% acquired; committed to +34% forward, +15% put** | FY25 (control from 02-Oct-2024) | Total consideration **₹3,410.20m** (cash ₹2,063.20m for 51% + forward liability ₹1,347m for 34%); net cash paid ₹1,971.95m; + ₹608m written put on the residual 15% | "Comprehensive provider of comprehensive cancer care in the Vizag region… strong primary catchment" [FY24-25 AR Directors' Report; MD&A]. No quantified synergy or return target disclosed. | **Goodwill = ₹2,070.14m = 60.7% of the ₹3,410.20m price**; net identifiable assets only ₹1,576.62m. Contributed ₹501.74m revenue and ₹48.84m PAT in ~6 months (~₹98m PAT annualized → ~2.9% on total consideration before integration). No disclosed post-deal return vs a hurdle. | FY24-25 AR Note 45.1, p.373-374; goodwill movement Note 7/7A, p.333; MD&A p.167 |
| **Indore — Cancer Center, Indore** | FY24 | Goodwill **₹416.89m** recorded (total price not separately broken out in pool) | Network expansion (no quantified synergy disclosed) | Goodwill carried at ₹416.89m at 31-Mar-2025 (no impairment) [Note 7A]. Operating contribution not separately disclosed. | FY24-25 AR Note 7A (CGU table), p.333; Note 45.2 |
| **Nagpur — HCG NCHRI Oncology LLP (oncology business)** | FY24 (from 01-Apr-2023) | Common-control slump sale; accounted under Ind AS 103 Appendix C (pooling); FY24 comparatives restated | Consolidation of an oncology business already under common control | Mandatory pooling restatement, **not** an error correction; auditor opinion "not modified" [01_disqualifier-scan #5]. A reorganization, not third-party value creation. | FY24-25 AR Auditor "Other Matter" p.218; AOC-2 p.118; 01_disqualifier-scan |
| **Milann / BACC Healthcare (fertility) — DIVESTITURE** | FY26 (agreed May-2026; close Q1 FY27) | Sold at **EV ₹632m / equity ₹376m** (₹282m upfront + ₹94m within 18 months) to **Inviga Healthcare Fund I — "an entity controlled by Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar, Promoter and Non-Executive Chairman"** | "Sharpen focus on core oncology… unlock value from a non-core investment" [FY26 deck p.6]. Stated to follow a "competitive sale process" with Board + Audit-Committee approval [FY26 call line 619-624]. | **Round-trip with a write-down:** held 13 years (majority 2013, fully owned 2020), carried ₹424.30m goodwill, **impaired ₹319m in FY26**, then exited to the promoter. A genuine portfolio-sharpening exit, but a **promoter-linked related-party sale** of an asset HCG had just written down. | FY26 deck p.6; FY26 call lines 605-624; FY24-25 AR Note 7A (BACC goodwill ₹424.30m), p.333; earnings/01 §6 (₹319m impairment) |

**M&A read.** The defining M&A fact is **goodwill intensity**: the Vizag deal paid ₹3,410m for ₹1,577m of net identifiable assets, so 60.7% of the price was goodwill, and consolidated goodwill near-doubled to ₹4,299.50m (from ₹2,229.35m) in FY25 [Note 7A, p.333]. No deal in the pool carries a **disclosed return-on-investment or synergy target measured against a hurdle**, so management's M&A discipline cannot be verified from outcomes — the only quantified outcome (Vizag's ~₹98m annualized PAT on ₹3,410m, ~2.9%) is thin and pre-integration. The Milann exit cuts the other way on focus (clearly non-core, declining 9→6 centres, near break-even) but is a **promoter-linked related-party divestiture of a just-impaired asset**, which is a candor/conflict flag the synthesizer should weigh even though it was Board- and Audit-Committee-approved. This is the goodwill-heavy serial-M&A pattern that `business-model/11` flagged at severity 58, and it is the proximate cause of the below-EBITDA earnings drag (D&A + finance cost) documented in `earnings/01`.

---

#### 3. Buyback Scorecard

HCG has executed **no buybacks** in any year in the pool. The relevant capital-allocation share event is the **opposite** — a FY26 rights issue (net dilution), preceded by minimal ESOP-only dilution. Columns are kept per the module template; "Implied Avg Price" is the issue/strike price, not a repurchase price.

| Year | Amount Spent (buyback) | Currency | Shares Bought | Implied Avg Price | Share-Count Change | Offset SBC Dilution? | Price-Discipline Evidence | Judgment | Source |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|---|---|
| FY23 | 0 | ₹ Mn | 0 | n/a | flat (ESOP-only) | n/a | No buyback | No buyback | FY23-24 AR share note |
| FY24 | 0 | ₹ Mn | 0 | n/a | +minimal (ESOP) | n/a | No buyback | No buyback | FY24-25 AR Note 15.1, p.258 |
| FY25 | 0 | ₹ Mn | 0 | n/a | **+125,683 sh (+0.09%)**, 139.29m → 139.42m, ESOP-only | n/a | No buyback | No buyback | FY24-25 AR §16.2 (13,92,89,687 → 13,94,15,370), p.171; Note 15.1 |
| FY26 | 0 (rights issue **+₹4,250m raised**) | ₹ Mn | 0 (≈ **+9.88m new shares issued**) | ≈ **₹430/sh** (₹4,250m ÷ ~9.88m; *implied — see note*) | **Up:** equity share capital ₹1,394.2m → ₹1,493.0m (+₹98.8m face @ ₹10) → ~139.4m → ~149.3m | n/a (raise, not offset) | Rights issue to fund growth + delever; oversubscribed 1.3× | **Net dilution, not return of capital** | FY26 deck p.16, p.20 (equity share capital); FY26 call line 118 |

**Average-price note.** No buyback average price exists. The FY26 **rights-issue** price is **not separately disclosed** in the pool; it is **estimated at ~₹430/share** from gross proceeds ₹4,250m ÷ the ~9.88m new shares implied by the ₹98.8m rise in ₹10-face equity share capital (₹1,394.2m → ₹1,493.0m). This is an **inference from two disclosed figures**, labelled as such, not a stated price — *Inference, not from filings.*

**Buyback read.** This module runs before valuation, so price discipline is judged only on observable evidence — and the observable evidence is unambiguous: **no shares were ever repurchased, and the only material equity event increased the share count.** Through FY25, dilution was trivial and ESOP-only (+0.09% in FY25), so per-share value was not eroded by stock-based comp. In FY26 the company **issued** ~9.88m new shares (~7% of the pre-issue count) at an estimated ~₹430 to fund capex and cut leverage. That is a defensible use of a rights issue for a capital-hungry hospital roll-up, but it is the opposite of returning capital and it dilutes existing holders. **Intrinsic-value buyback discipline is not assessable from this module** (no buyback occurred and the valuation module has not run). Net judgment: not a buyback story — a net-issuance story, with no per-share accretion from capital-structure actions.

---

#### 4. Dividends & Organic Reinvestment

| Metric | Value | Source |
|---|---:|---|
| Dividend paid (any year FY23–FY26) | **₹0** (never paid) | FY24-25 AR Consolidated Cash Flow (no dividend line), p.314; FY26 deck cash flow p.22 |
| Payout ratio | **0%** | derived (no dividend) |
| Why no dividend | Consolidated retained earnings **negative at ₹(4,092.38)m** (31-Mar-2025) — legally constrains distribution; cash fully retained for capex/M&A | FY24-25 AR Note 16 Other equity (consolidated), p.340 |
| Dividend coverage (FCF) | n/a (no dividend); FY26 FCF ₹586m, FY23–FY26 cumulative FCF ₹3,841m | earnings/01 §1-2 |
| Reported ROCE (company basis, ex-fertility, pre-Ind AS) | **14.0% (FY26)** vs **20%+ five-year target** (6 ppt gap) | FY26 deck p.5; FY26 call line 170; 01_management §3 |
| Incremental ROIC (Δ NOPAT / Δ invested capital), FY24→FY26 | **≈ 13% (gross) ; ≈ 7% post-write-down** — see computation | computed below from earnings/01 + FY26 deck |

**Incremental-ROIC computation (Δ NOPAT / Δ invested capital).** NOPAT ≈ EBIT × (1 − tax). FY24 EBIT ₹1,552m; FY26 EBIT ₹2,466m [earnings/01 §1]. Using an illustrative ~25% effective tax: NOPAT FY24 ≈ ₹1,164m; FY26 ≈ ₹1,850m; ΔNOPAT ≈ **₹686m** over two years. Invested capital ≈ net debt (balance-sheet, incl. leases) + total equity: FY24 ≈ 10,018 + ~8,500 ≈ ₹18,518m; FY26 ≈ 11,993 + 14,102 ≈ ₹26,095m; Δ invested capital ≈ **₹7,577m** [earnings/01 §1; FY26 deck p.20-21]. Incremental ROIC ≈ 686 / 7,577 ≈ **9.1% gross on a two-year stack**; on the FY25→FY26 step alone (ΔNOPAT ≈ ₹530m on Δ invested capital ≈ ₹2,400m after the rights issue/Vizag) the figure is higher (~13–22%) but is distorted by the equity raise and the held-for-sale reclassification. *This is an estimate (tax rate and invested-capital base are approximations from the deck, not a full statutory cash-tax reconciliation) — Inference, not from filings; treat as directional.* The honest read: **incremental returns on the FY23–FY26 reinvestment have run roughly in the low-teens at best and below the 20%+ ROCE target**, and a portion of the deployed capital (the ₹319m Milann impairment) earned a negative return outright.

**Reinvestment read.** Capex is **growth-led and runs at/above depreciation** — FY26 capex ₹2,885m (growth ₹1,696m + maintenance ₹1,189m) vs D&A ~₹2,442m, and FY25 PP&E additions ₹2,088.67m vs depreciation ₹2,113.44m [FY26 deck p.14; FY24-25 AR p.314, p.329]. The company's own ROCE disclosure shows the **payoff exists but is below target**: mature centres (>₹100m/month, 44% of revenue) earn ~25% ROCE while the newest cohort (<₹50m/month) is still **negative (−22% in FY25, recovering to 6% in FY26)** [FY26 deck p.~37]. Blended FY26 ROCE is 14.0% against a 20%+ five-year target — a 6 ppt gap. So organic reinvestment is **value-creating at the mature-centre level but value-dilutive while new capacity ramps**, and the consolidated incremental return has not yet cleared a 20% hurdle.

**Forward capital claim the synthesizer must carry.** Beyond capex and M&A, HCG has **~₹3,318m of committed minority buy-out obligations** — written put options to NCI ₹1,778.0m (up from ₹931.58m a year earlier) + a forward-purchase liability ₹1,540.0m — of which **₹1,401.34m already sits in current liabilities**, and management's own risk factor states it relies on banks to fund "put options of the partners" and "cannot assure" the funding [FY24-25 AR Note 34, p.356; MD&A p.169; Risk Factors p.149; cross-ref business-model/12]. This is a near-dated, contractual claim on the same cash that capex consumes — a real future capital-allocation pressure point, not a current return-of-capital item.

---

#### 5. Capital-Allocation Read

Over FY23–FY26 HCG returned **nothing** to shareholders (no dividend — legally constrained by ₹(4,092)m negative retained earnings — and no buyback ever) and put effectively all of its ₹12,004m four-year CFO, plus borrowings, into capex (~68% of CFO) and goodwill-heavy acquisitions (~33% of CFO; Vizag was 60.7% goodwill), then issued ₹4,250m of new equity in FY26 to repair a balance sheet that serial M&A had levered ~33% higher — so on a **per-share** basis this period created little to no value: reported PAT to owners fell from ₹482m (FY24) to ₹444m (FY25) to ₹138m (FY26) and diluted EPS fell from ₹3.43 to ~₹1.0 even as EBITDA grew, because the spending lands below EBITDA as depreciation and finance cost and one acquired asset (Milann) was impaired ₹319m and then sold to the founder. The **best** use was organic reinvestment into mature centres (which earn ~25% ROCE), though the blend is dragged to 14% by ramping new capacity and falls short of the 20%+ target; the **worst** was goodwill-heavy M&A whose only quantified return (Vizag ~2.9% pre-integration) is unverified against any hurdle and whose Milann leg round-tripped at a write-down to a promoter-linked buyer. The single biggest capital-allocation signal is that **growth here has been bought with debt and equity faster than it has converted to per-share earnings — under the prior owner — and the FY26 deleveraging that looks like discipline was funded by a rights issue, not free cash flow.** Conclusion: **mixed** (skewed value-destructive on the prior-regime M&A leg; the new KKR-era moves — delever, sharpen portfolio, target 20% ROCE — point toward improvement but have under one year of evidence and remain unproven).

---

---

#### Capital Allocation Score

*Higher = better (per-share value creation and capital discipline). Two of six components are scored on partial evidence and flagged; the FY26 figures rest on the audited-results deck, not full statutory statements (confidence 3), and the multi-year record largely predates the current owner [02-018].*

| Component | Score | Max Score | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Organic reinvestment returns | 13 | 25 | Capex is growth-led and runs at/above D&A [02-001]; mature centres earn ~25% ROCE but the blend is 14.0% vs a 20%+ target [02-012], incremental ROIC ~low-teens at best on a distorted base [02-013], and the new-centre cohort was ROCE-negative in FY25 [02-014]. Real assets, real payoff at maturity, but below hurdle and dilutive while ramping. |
| M&A discipline | 6 | 20 | Goodwill-heavy serial M&A: Vizag 60.7% goodwill, group goodwill near-doubled [02-004]; no disclosed ROI/synergy target to verify against [02-005]; the only quantified return is ~2.9% pre-integration; one acquired business (Milann) was impaired ₹319m [02-007] and then sold to a promoter-linked fund [02-006]. Low score reflects unverifiable returns + a conflicted exit, not proven fraud. |
| Buyback discipline | Insufficient Data (scored 10/20 neutral) | 20 | No buyback ever [02-008]; pre-FY26 dilution was trivial/ESOP-only (per-share not eroded by SBC), which is a mild positive, but FY26 was net-dilutive via the rights issue. Intrinsic-value discipline not assessable (valuation module not run) [02-009]. Scored at the neutral mid-point and flagged, not zero (nothing destructive observed) and not high (no accretive repurchase to reward). |
| Dividend sustainability | 7 | 15 | No dividend, payout 0% [02-011]; legally constrained by ₹(4,092)m negative consolidated retained earnings. Defensible for a reinvesting roll-up, but there is no return-of-capital track record to credit and distribution capacity does not yet exist. |
| Balance-sheet prudence | 6 | 10 | Debt rose ~33% on M&A into FY25 (D/E 0.81→1.09, EBITDA/interest 2.74x) [02-015]; the FY26 delever to 1.4x was funded by a ₹4,250m equity raise, not FCF; and ~₹3,318m of bank-dependent minority buy-out puts/forward — ₹1,401.34m already current — compete with capex, with management itself unable to "assure" funding [02-016]. Now within the 2.5x ceiling, but the prudence was equity-bought. |
| Per-share value creation | 3 | 10 | Diluted EPS fell ₹3.43 → ~₹1.0 and PAT to owners ₹482m → ₹138m over FY24–FY26 even as EBITDA grew, with new equity diluting the base [02-017]. Over this window capital allocation created little to no per-share value; the spending landed below EBITDA and one leg earned a negative return. |
| Total | **45** | 100 | Mixed/weak band. Pulled up by real reinvestment that pays off at mature centres and a clean (if equity-funded) FY26 deleveraging; pulled down by goodwill-heavy M&A with unverifiable returns, a promoter-linked impaired-asset exit, zero capital returned, an embedded bank-dependent minority-buyout claim, and a clear **per-share** deterioration. Read as **provisional**: the multi-year record is the prior owner's, and the new KKR-era discipline has under one year of evidence [02-018]. |

**Caps applied:** No MODULE_RULES score cap binds on capital allocation — the governance triage confirms multi-year M&A/buyback/dividend history is available [00_governance-data-triage §5, "No multi-year history: N"], so the "max 65" multi-year-history cap does **not** apply. Buyback discipline is marked Insufficient Data (no buyback occurred; valuation module not run) and scored at a neutral mid-point rather than guessed. FY26 rows carry confidence 3 (results deck, not statutory). The total (45) is unconstrained by a hard cap but explicitly **provisional** pending the FY26 audited Annual Report and 2–4 quarters under the KKR-installed team.

## MG-03 · incentives-and-compensation — Incentives & Compensation — HCG

### Incentives & Compensation — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Indian SEBI-LODR issuer, Ind AS, fiscal year ends 31 March. Reporting currency INR (₹), figures in INR Million unless stated. Report date: 2026-06-01. The compensation-disclosure source for an Indian issuer is the Board's Report + Corporate Governance Report + Section 197(12)/Rule 5 disclosure inside the Annual Report (the DEF 14A-equivalent), not a standalone proxy. Most recent audited remuneration disclosure = FY2024-25 Integrated Annual Report (year ended 31-Mar-2025; Board's Report dated 01-Aug-2025; AGM 25-Sep-2025). The FY26 audited AR is not in the pool.*

**The governing fact for this read (partial-data condition).** Every quantified pay figure in the pool belongs to the *prior, CVC/Aceso-era* leadership — Executive Chairman Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar, Whole-time Director & CEO Meghraj Gore (exited as CEO 30-Jun-2025), and CFO Ruby Ritolia. The executives actually running HCG today — KKR-nominee CEO Dr. Manish Mattoo (in seat 30-Jun-2025) and incoming CFO Sanjeev Kumar Saxena (joining end-May-2026) — have **no disclosed remuneration amount and no disclosed metric set in the pool** [FY24-25 AR, Board's Report §19.4(d), p.79: Mattoo appointed CEO "on such terms and conditions as may be mutually agreed"]. So the *structure and metrics* of HCG's pay framework are assessable from the FY24-25 disclosure; the *magnitude and alignment of the current team's pay* are not. Cross-module inputs used: `management-governance/01_management-and-track-record.md` (team/control context — available), `business-model/11_capital-allocation-governance.md` (turnover/control facts — available), `earnings/06_earnings-quality.md` (non-GAAP/PAT-gap context — available). Per the MODULE_RULES Partial-Data Rule the Incentive-Alignment score is capped at 50 and Overall usefulness at 70 to the extent current-team pay is undisclosed; the score below is set within that cap.

#### 1. Compensation Structure

Reporting currency INR; period FY2024-25 (year ended 31-Mar-2025). Pay components: fixed salary + an annual cash variable component + ESOPs (long-term equity). HCG pays **no commission and no severance**; Promoter/Promoter-group directors receive **no stock options** by policy [FY24-25 AR, CG Report §I(e)/§J, p.125-126].

| Executive | Base (FY25) | Annual Bonus / Variable (FY25) | Long-Term Incentive (FY25 share-based) | Cash vs Equity Mix | Source |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| CEO / WTD — Meghraj Gore (prior; CEO to 30-Jun-2025) | ₹22.05 Mn | ₹11.03 Mn cash variable | ₹12.10 Mn (ESOP fair-value charge) | ~75% cash / ~25% equity (₹33.08 Mn cash vs ₹12.10 Mn SBP) | FY24-25 AR, Annexure 3(a) note 2, p.102; CG Report §J table, p.126 |
| CEO — Dr. Manish Mattoo (current, KKR nominee, from 30-Jun-2025) | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | FY24-25 AR §19.4(d) p.79 ("terms as mutually agreed") — *Insufficient Data* |
| CFO — Ruby Ritolia (FY25 incumbent) | ₹14.50 Mn (cash, incl. variable) | included in ₹14.50 Mn | ₹12.71 Mn (ESOP fair-value charge) | ~53% cash / ~47% equity (₹14.50 Mn cash vs ₹12.71 Mn SBP) | FY24-25 AR, Annexure 3(b) notes, p.103 |
| CFO — Sanjeev Kumar Saxena (incoming, end-May-2026) | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | Q4/FY26 transcript L118-121 (20-May-2026) — *Insufficient Data* |
| (Context) Exec Chairman — Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar (founder; Non-Exec from 30-May-2025) | ₹25 Mn | ₹35 Mn cash variable | None (Promoter — no ESOPs) | 100% cash; variable = 58% of ₹60 Mn total | FY24-25 AR, Annexure 3(a) note 1, p.102; CG Report §J, p.126 |

Notes: (1) The "CEO" line in the Rule 5(1)(b) KMP table shows Gore's cash CEO remuneration at **₹33.08 Mn for both FY24 and FY25** (fixed + variable, excluding the ₹12.10 Mn ESOP charge) [p.102-103]. (2) Independent Directors are paid a **flat ₹2.0 Mn/yr fee** (no sitting fees since FY24, no commission, no options) — approved by postal ballot 25-Jun-2023 [CG Report §J(iv), p.126]. (3) Aceso-nominee directors (Soni, Patel) and KKR-nominee directors received no fee from the company [Annexure 3(a) footnote, p.102]. (4) Variable pay is **annual cash**; the only multi-year/long-term instrument is the ESOP.

#### 2. Performance Metrics

The actual metrics are disclosed in the CG Report's "Variable Remuneration/Compensation" paragraph. They are stated in plain text **without numeric weights** — the weights are set per individual by the Nomination & Remuneration Committee (NRC) and are not published. The full NRC policy lives on the company website (not in the pool) [FY24-25 AR, CG Report §I, p.125].

| Plan | Metric (as disclosed) | Weight | Per-Share/Returns or Size-Based? | Source |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| Annual cash variable (Exec Directors incl. CEO) | **Revenue** | Not disclosed | **Size-based** | FY24-25 AR, Annexure 3 "Variable Remuneration", p.105 (L6498) |
| Annual cash variable | **Profit achievement** (absolute) | Not disclosed | Size/absolute-profit (not per-share) | FY24-25 AR, p.105 |
| Annual cash variable | **Operating-margin achievement** | Not disclosed | Margin (profitability ratio) — not per-share/returns | FY24-25 AR, p.105 |
| Annual cash variable | **"Other strategic goals as decided by the Board"** + individual KRAs/KPIs | Not disclosed | Discretionary / unclassifiable | FY24-25 AR, p.105; CG Report §I(d), p.125 |
| Long-term incentive (ESOP 2014 / ESOS 2021) | Vesting = continued employment (time) **+** "achievement of performance criteria as specified by the NRC at grant" | Not disclosed (per-grant) | Equity instrument (ties pay to share price); underlying performance metric **not disclosed** | FY24-25 AR, ESOP note (i)/(ii), p.97-98 (L5992, L6021) |

**Classification verdict.** Every *named* financial parameter in the variable-pay policy is **absolute / size- or margin-based — revenue, absolute profit, operating margin.** There is **no per-share or returns-based metric (no ROCE, ROIC, EPS, FCF/share, or TSR)** named anywhere in the disclosed criteria, even though management itself has set a public 5-year "20%+ pre-tax ROCE" target [Dec-04-2025 Investor Meet; mgmt-gov/01 §3]. ROCE is a stated *strategic* target but is **not shown to be a pay metric.** The one structural pull toward per-share value is the ESOP: equity grants tie a slice of pay to the share price (≈25% of the prior CEO's package, ≈47% of the prior CFO's). But options are struck at exercise prices of ₹130-200 (ESOS 2021) — at or below market, i.e. **not premium-struck**, so they reward the share price reaching modest levels rather than out-performance, and the performance condition attached to vesting is not disclosed. Net: the *named* incentive design rewards growth and absolute profit/margin, with equity providing partial — but undisclosed-strength — per-share alignment.

**One off-balance-sheet incentive of note.** During FY25 the prior promoter, Aceso (via parent AIHPL), proposed paying **bonuses directly to identified KMPs/employees** "as consideration for... enhancement of shareholder value," with **no financial obligation on the company**; approved by the Board (21-Feb-2025) and by shareholders via postal ballot (results 28-Apr-2025) [FY24-25 AR, Standalone Note 44D, p.293]. This is a sponsor-funded management-incentive/value-creation payout (PE-style), tied to the owner's realized value rather than to a disclosed per-share company metric. Its amount and metrics are **not disclosed**, and the minority vote on it was contested (see §3).

#### 3. Pay vs Performance

| Signal | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| CEO total pay (latest disclosed) | Prior CEO Gore FY25: **₹45.18 Mn** total (₹22.05 fixed + ₹11.03 cash variable + ₹12.10 ESOP); cash-only CEO line ₹33.08 Mn, **flat YoY (0% increase)**. Current CEO (Mattoo) pay **not disclosed**. | FY24-25 AR Annexure 3(a)/(b), p.102-103 |
| Chairman total pay | Founder/Exec Chairman Ajaikumar FY25: **₹60.0 Mn** (₹25 fixed + ₹35 variable), **+22.5% YoY** — the largest single package and the only material managerial increase that year; 58% of it variable. He had no ESOPs (Promoter). | FY24-25 AR Annexure 3(a) note 1, p.102 |
| CFO total pay | Prior CFO Ritolia FY25: **₹14.50 Mn cash + ₹12.71 Mn ESOP = ₹27.21 Mn**. Current CFO (Saxena) pay not disclosed. | FY24-25 AR Annexure 3(b) note 2, p.103 |
| CEO pay vs median employee | Exec Chairman ratio **200:1**; CEO/WTD ratio **150.6:1**; median employee remuneration ₹0.30 Mn, +7.14% YoY; workforce 5,830. Managerial increase (driven by Chairman's +22.5%) outran the 9.63% average employee increase that year. | FY24-25 AR Annexure 3(a)/(c)/(d), p.102-103 |
| Pay vs performance alignment | Pay rose (Chairman +22.5%) and CEO held flat in a year when **reported PAT later fell to ₹138 Mn in FY26** (from ₹444 Mn FY25) and normalized EPS missed consensus in 3 of the last 4 fiscal years [mgmt-gov/01 §4; earnings/06]. Variable pay keyed to revenue/EBITDA — which the company *does* hit — not to the per-share/PAT line it misses, so the design pays out on the metrics that work and is silent on the ones that don't. | FY24-25 AR Annexure 3; mgmt-gov/01 §4; earnings/99 §1 |
| Say-on-pay support (%) | **Promoter-funded KMP bonus resolution (postal ballot, results 28-Apr-2025): only 74.01% in favour, 25.99% against** — and the promoter (Aceso) was recused (net valid votes 1.92 cr vs ~11.86 cr on other items), so this is **~26% of *minority* shareholders voting against the management incentive plan.** ID-remuneration resolution: 96.75% for (3.25% against). ESOP surrender-for-cash option: 99.97% for. ID reappointments (Aug-2024): ~96.1% for. | FY24-25 AR, CG Report §iii(b) voting table, p.142 (L9136-9144); §iii(a), p.141 |
| Peer benchmark | Indian listed-hospital peers (Apollo Hospitals, Max Healthcare, Narayana, Fortis) exist [business-model/08], but **no peer CEO-comp figure is in the pool**, and a reliable web figure was not retrieved for this run. Relative pay magnitude **not assessed** (web search returned only AR links, no dated comp number). | business-model/08; web search 2026-06-01 (no usable figure) |

**Reported vs adjusted note (hygiene).** The pay disclosure uses cash + ESOP fair-value charge; "% increase" lines explicitly exclude share-based payment [Annexure 3(b) footnote, p.103]. The PAT figures used for the performance comparison are reported (not adjusted) — HCG's adjusted-vs-reported PAT gap was +305% in FY26 [earnings/06], which is why a revenue/EBITDA-keyed bonus can pay out while reported per-share earnings deteriorate.

#### 4. Incentive Alignment Read

The named variable-pay metrics reward **revenue, absolute profit, and operating margin** — size and growth, not per-share value — and the disclosed framework contains **no ROCE, EPS, FCF/share, or TSR pay metric**, even though management publicly touts a 20%+ ROCE *target*; the only per-share pull is ESOP equity (≈25-47% of the prior CEO/CFO packages), but those options are struck at-or-below market and their vesting performance condition is undisclosed, so their alignment strength can't be verified. The single biggest signal is twofold and points the same way: pay rose (Chairman +22.5%, 200:1 ratio) on the metrics the company hits while reported PAT collapsed and EPS kept missing, and minority shareholders threw **~26% opposition** at the promoter-funded KMP bonus tied to "shareholder-value enhancement" — the clearest evidence that the pay-design's claim of value alignment is contested by the people it is meant to serve. **Conclusion: mixed, tilted to size-based** — the disclosed cash-bonus metrics are size/absolute-profit-based (no per-share/returns metric), partly offset by equity in the mix; and, decisively, the *current* CEO's and CFO's pay and metrics are **undisclosed**, so true alignment for the team in charge today is **not assessable** (partial-data cap applied).

Out-of-scope note: this report does not value HCG, assign probabilities, or rate the stock — those belong to the valuation module and the master synthesizer.

#### Incentive Alignment Score

*Higher = better. Partial-data cap applied per MODULE_RULES (no current-team / no quantified-weight disclosure): Incentive Alignment capped at max 50. The score below sits within that cap and reflects (a) disclosed metrics being size-based with no per-share/returns metric, (b) undisclosed weights and undisclosed current-team pay, (c) partial equity alignment via ESOPs, and (d) a contested minority say-on-pay vote.*

| Component | Score | Max Score | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Returns / per-share metrics | 3 | 25 | Disclosed variable-pay financial parameters are **revenue, absolute profit, operating margin** — all size/absolute, **no ROCE/ROIC/EPS/FCF-ps/TSR** [03-005, 03-007]. The 20%+ ROCE figure is a stated strategic target, not shown to be a pay metric. Near-floor. |
| Metric clarity and weights | 4 | 20 | Metrics are *named* (better than nothing) but **no weights are published**; NRC sets them per-grant/per-individual and the policy is off-pool (website) [03-006]. "Other strategic goals as decided by the Board" leaves wide discretion. |
| Pay-for-performance alignment | 5 | 20 | Pay rose/held (Chairman +22.5%, 200:1) while reported PAT fell to ₹138 Mn and EPS missed 3 of 4 yrs; bonus keyed to the revenue/EBITDA lines the company hits, not the per-share line it misses [03-009, 03-010]. Equity mix is the only partial offset. |
| Long-term orientation | 9 | 15 | A genuine multi-year ESOP exists (≈25-47% of prior CEO/CFO packages), tying pay to share price; vests over time + perf-condition [03-008]. Marked down because options are at-/below-market (not premium-struck) and the vesting performance metric is undisclosed; bonus itself is annual. |
| Pay magnitude vs peers/performance | 4 | 10 | Magnitude is modest in absolute INR (CEO ~₹45 Mn) and the cash CEO line was flat YoY, but **no peer benchmark is available** to judge relative level [03-014], and the Chairman raise outran staff [03-010]. Partial credit for restraint at the CEO line, capped by missing peer data. |
| Minority-holder alignment | 4 | 10 | A returns-/per-share-blind bonus framework plus a promoter-funded KMP bonus that **~26% of minorities voted against** [03-011, 03-013] points pay at growth/sponsor-value, not clearly at minority per-share value. Routine pay items did pass ~96-100% [03-012], giving partial credit. |
| Total | 29 | 100 | Mixed-to-weak band, set within the partial-data cap (≤50). Size-based disclosed metrics, undisclosed weights, undisclosed current-team pay, and a contested minority vote pull down; a real ESOP and modest CEO cash magnitude pull up. **Provisional** — re-score once FY26 AR discloses the current team's pay and metrics. |

**Cap applied:** No proxy/compensation disclosure for the *current* CEO/CFO and no quantified metric weights → MODULE_RULES Score-Cap "No proxy / compensation disclosure" (Incentive alignment max 50; Overall usefulness max 70). The 29/100 is below the cap and is flagged provisional pending the FY26 audited Annual Report.

## MG-04 · ownership-and-insider-behavior — Ownership & Insider Behavior — HCG

### Ownership & Insider Behavior — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Indian SEBI-LODR issuer, Ind AS, fiscal year ends 31 March. Reporting currency INR (₹). Report date: 2026-06-01. The proxy-equivalent is the AGM Notice + Corporate Governance Report; ownership is the shareholding-pattern filing under SEBI-LODR Reg-31; insider transactions are captured via SAST/PIT disclosures, ESOP allotments, and the SPA transfer — there is no US Form-4-style granular open-market buy/sell log in the pool. Most recent **audited** shareholding disclosure is the FY24-25 Integrated Annual Report (as-of 31-Mar-2025, filed Sep-2025). The most current ownership read is the Capital IQ Public Ownership export (position dates Dec-31-2025), which captures the post-KKR-changeover picture but is a vendor source (confidence 2). A post-changeover FY26 audited annual report / detailed Reg-31(1) shareholding-and-encumbrance pattern is NOT in the pool.*

Scope: this agent maps who owns HCG, whether insider stakes were bought or granted, whether insiders are buying or selling, whether shares are pledged, and who controls the votes. It does not assess compensation (agent 03) or board independence (agent 05), and it does not value the company or rate the stock. Cross-module inputs used and available: `business-model/01_disqualifier-scan.md` (pledging/related-party verdict), `business-model/11_capital-allocation-governance.md` (control/pledge facts), `business-model/12_red-flags-sweep.md` (minority put obligations, RPT context). Upstream in-module input used: `01_management-and-track-record.md` (control history) and `00_governance-data-triage.md` (source map). All were available.

**One fact governs this read:** HCG is a private-equity-controlled company, not a founder/owner-operator one. KKR (via Hector Asia Holdings II Pte. Ltd.) acquired sole control on 30-May-2025 and held **50.84%** at Dec-31-2025 [CIQ Public Ownership Summary, Top Holders, position date Dec-31-2025]. The founder, Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar, retains a pre-IPO promoter stake of **~9.22%** but is now a Non-Executive Chairman with no executive role [CIQ Public Ownership, Dec-31-2025; FY24-25 AR §15.4]. The "skin in the game" here is overwhelmingly a financial sponsor's control block bought for cash, plus a residual founder holding — not management buying with conviction. No insider open-market buying or selling is recorded in the pool, and **no promoter share pledge is disclosed anywhere.**

#### 1. Ownership

Two datasets, reconciled. The **audited FY25 pattern (31-Mar-2025)** predates the KKR changeover and shows CVC/Aceso as promoter. The **CIQ export (Dec-31-2025)** is post-changeover and post-rights-issue, and is the current read. Percentages differ because (a) control passed from CVC to KKR on 30-May-2025 and (b) the FY26 ₹425 cr rights issue enlarged the share base from ~139.4m to ~149.3m shares, diluting each holding's percentage.

| Holder | % Held | Share Class | Bought or Granted? | Source |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| **Controlling owner — Hector Asia Holdings II Pte. Ltd. (KKR)** | **50.84%** (75,909,051 sh) as of Dec-31-2025 | Ordinary equity (₹10 face); **no dual-class / no differential voting rights** | **Bought for cash** — acquired 51.59% from CVC/Aceso under SPA dated 23-Feb-2025, transfer completed 30-May-2025; mandatory 26% open offer at ₹504.41/sh | CIQ Public Ownership, Top Holders, Dec-31-2025; FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §15, p.76-77 |
| **Founder — Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar** | **9.22%** (13,763,027 sh) as of Dec-31-2025; was **10.41%** (14,498,715 sh) at 31-Mar-2025 | Ordinary equity | **Bought / founder pre-IPO promoter stake** — built as founder since inception (CEO 2006-2021), not a compensation grant; % fell on rights-issue dilution and a small share reduction | CIQ Public Ownership, Dec-31-2025; FY24-25 AR §15.3/§15.4, p.[259]/[259-260] |
| Founder family (promoter group) — Asmitha, Aagnika, Bhagya, Anjali Ajaikumar | ~0.46% combined (657,312 sh) at 31-Mar-2025 | Ordinary equity | Family holding (promoter group) | FY24-25 AR §15.4 promoter-group table, p.[259-260] |
| **Other insiders (aggregate, "Individuals/Insiders")** | **9.41%** (14,049,085 sh) as of Dec-31-2025 | Ordinary equity | Predominantly the founder block; remainder includes KMP/ESOP-related individual holdings | CIQ Public Ownership Summary, "Individuals/Insiders", Dec-31-2025 |
| Largest institutional holders | Nippon Life India AMC **5.29%** (7.90m, Sep-30-2025); Tata AMC **3.16%** (4.71m, Sep-30-2025); Motilal Oswal AMC **2.86%** (4.28m, May-04-2026) | Ordinary equity | Open-market institutional | CIQ Public Ownership, Top Holders, Dec-31-2025 |
| Institutions (aggregate, top tier) | **19.66%** (29,356,888 sh) | Ordinary equity | Open-market institutional | CIQ Public Ownership Summary, "Institutions", Dec-31-2025 |
| **Promoter & promoter group (audited)** | **71.22%** at 31-Mar-2025 (CVC/Aceso 60.40% + Ajaikumar 10.41% + family 0.46%) | Ordinary equity | Aceso = CVC's holding co. (bought 2020); founder = pre-IPO; KKR replaced CVC as the controlling promoter from 30-May-2025 | FY24-25 AR, shareholding-pattern chart, p.[~58]; §15.3/§15.4, p.[259-260] |

**Total insider/promoter ownership:** As of the audited FY25 pattern, **71.22%** of equity was held by the Promoter & promoter group (then CVC/Aceso-led). Post-changeover (Dec-31-2025, CIQ vendor data), the controlling KKR block is **50.84%** and the founder/insider block is **~9.22-9.41%**; the FY26 detailed Reg-31 promoter-group total under KKR is **not in the audited pool** — the new promoter group is KKR (Hector Asia + KIA EBT) plus the BSA (Ajaikumar) Promoter Group under a 23-Feb-2025 Promoter Agreement [FY24-25 AR §15, p.77]. **The defining ownership fact is that meaningful "skin in the game" is a PE sponsor's cash-bought control stake, not management buying with personal conviction.** Of the management team actually running HCG today (CEO Mattoo, incoming CFO Saxena — both KKR appointees), no personal equity stake is disclosed in the pool — that is "Insufficient Data" pending the FY26 CG Report.

#### 2. Insider Transactions (last 12 months)

No granular open-market insider buy/sell log (Form-4 equivalent) is in the pool. The transactions below are what the audited filing and the vendor export disclose. The CIQ "Individuals/Insiders" block shows **zero new, increased, decreased, or sold-out individual/insider positions** in the current activity window — the founder/insider block is static.

| Insider | Buy / Sell | Amount | Date | Notes (10b5-1 / nature) | Source |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|
| **CVC / Aceso Company Pte. Ltd. (outgoing controller)** | **Sell** (block) | 71,927,991 sh (≈51.59% of then-capital) to KKR Purchasers | 30-May-2025 | Negotiated block sale under SPA dated 23-Feb-2025; Aceso reclassified Promoter→Public; **not an open-market trade** | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §15, p.76-77 |
| **KKR / Hector Asia Holdings II Pte. Ltd. (Purchaser 1)** | **Buy** (block + open offer) | 71,677,991 sh via SPA; + open offer for up to 37,090,327 sh (26%) at ₹504.41/sh (₹1,870.87 cr) | 30-May-2025 (SPA); open offer Letter-of-Offer 10-Jul-2025, completing thereafter | Acquired **sole control**; cash; **not an open-market accumulation by management** | FY24-25 AR §15, p.76-77 |
| Founder / Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar | **No disclosed buy or sell** | — | — | Shares held flat at 14,498,715 (10.41%) through FY25; CIQ Dec-31-2025 shows 13,763,027 (9.22%) — a small reduction/dilution, **no disclosed open-market sale** | FY24-25 AR §15.3/§15.4; CIQ Dec-31-2025 |
| Employees (ESOP exercises) | **Buy** (option exercise → allotment) | small tranches (e.g., 15,999 sh 09-Nov-2024; 12,638 sh 14-Feb-2025) | FY25 | **Option exercises, not open-market buys** — HCG ESOS 2014 (pre-IPO) + ESOS 2021 | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §16, p.[77]; §43 ESOP, p.[57-58] |

**Net insider activity: Neutral / not a conviction signal.** The only material ownership change in the last 12 months is the **CVC→KKR control transfer** — a negotiated block sale by one financial sponsor to another, plus a mandatory open offer, **not** open-market buying or selling by the people who run the company. The founder did not buy on the changeover (no disclosed open-market purchase to signal conviction in the new chapter) and did not disclose an open-market sale either; his percentage fell mechanically on rights-issue dilution. There is **no individual-insider open-market buying** in the pool to read as conviction, and **no individual-insider open-market selling ahead of weak results** to read as a red flag. The signal is therefore neutral, and is structurally a sponsor-ownership story rather than an insider-behavior story.

#### 3. Pledging & Encumbrance

| Insider | Shares Pledged | % of Their Holding | Source |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Promoter (CVC/Aceso, then KKR/Hector Asia) | None disclosed | 0% disclosed | FY24-25 AR §15.4 (no pledge noted), p.[259-260]; CARO Annexure A clause (ix)(f), p.[224] |
| Founder / Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar | None disclosed | 0% disclosed | FY24-25 AR §15.3/§15.4; business-model/01 disqualifier-scan |

**No promoter or insider share pledging is disclosed.** A word-by-word search of the FY24-25 and FY23-24 annual reports found no "shares pledged" / "encumbered" / "non-disposal of promoter shares" statement against any promoter or insider [confirmed in business-model/01_disqualifier-scan §1, item 2]. CARO Annexure A clause (ix)(f) confirms no loans were raised against pledge of securities held in subsidiaries/JVs [FY24-25 AR, p.[224]]. **Important distinction:** the only "pledge" / "non-disposal" references in the filings are the **Company** pledging or restricting transfer of its **own investments in subsidiaries** as borrowing collateral (e.g., a 20-year non-disposal undertaking on its HCG Manavata Oncology LLP investment; HCG SUN Hospitals LLP transfer restriction; Notes 17/18 "pledge of financial assets") — these are corporate-debt collateral arrangements, **not** promoter share pledges, and do **not** trigger RF-OWN-001/002. No pledging red flag is triggered. Cross-reference: business-model/01_disqualifier-scan confirms the >50% promoter-pledge disqualifier is **N (not triggered)**.

*Data limitation (carried from triage and disqualifier-scan): the FY25 pattern predates the KKR changeover; a post-changeover Reg-31(1) detailed pledge/encumbrance line is not in the pool. The "no pledge" finding is therefore confidence-5 for the FY25 promoter (CVC) and the founder, but **Insufficient Data** for any encumbrance on the KKR/Hector Asia block under its current LBO-style ownership — to be confirmed by the FY26 Reg-31 disclosure.*

#### 4. Control Structure

| Feature | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Dual-class / super-voting** | **None.** "The Company has not issued shares with differential voting rights and sweat equity shares." One ordinary equity class (₹10 face); KKR's control is economic (50.84% of one class), not structural | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §15-16, p.77 |
| **Controlled-company status** | **Yes — controlled by KKR.** Hector Asia Holdings II Pte. Ltd. (Purchaser 1) "acquired sole control over the Company with effect from May 30, 2025"; reclassified Promoter under SEBI-LODR Reg-31A; foreign-owned-and-controlled company under FEMA NDI Rules | FY24-25 AR §13-15, p.76-77 |
| **Shareholder bloc with board rights** | **Yes — KKR + founder Promoter Agreement.** A Promoter Agreement (23-Feb-2025, subsequently amended) between the KKR Purchasers and the **BSA (Ajaikumar) Promoter Group** records "inter-se rights and obligations… as promoters." The prior CVC Investment Agreement (04-Jun-2020) was terminated 30-May-2025. KKR has installed the CEO (Mattoo), incoming CFO (Saxena), and two nominee directors (Mehta, Tanna) | FY24-25 AR §13-15, p.76-77; 01_management-and-track-record §1 |
| **Open offer (minority exit at a set price)** | 26% open offer (up to 37,090,327 sh) at **₹504.41/sh**, ₹1,870.87 cr; Letter of Offer 10-Jul-2025; minority shareholders received an exit option at this price; offer being completed at the date of the FY25 AR | FY24-25 AR §15, p.76-77 |
| **Minority buy-out obligations (control-cost overhang)** | HCG owes ~₹3,318m to non-controlling partners via written put options (₹1,778.0m) + a forward-purchase liability (₹1,540.0m); ₹1,401.34m already in current financial liabilities — a contractual cash claim that competes with capex | business-model/12_red-flags-sweep (FY24-25 AR Note 34, p.[356]; MD&A) |

**Control structure read:** HCG is **controlled, not dispersed.** A single financial sponsor (KKR/Hector Asia, 50.84%) has sole control through a plain-vanilla ordinary-share majority — there is no dual-class or super-voting amplification, which is the cleaner version of control. The minority-protection concern is not vote-rigging but **alignment-of-horizon**: a PE owner runs to an exit clock, and the founder/Non-Executive Chairman shares promoter status under a Promoter Agreement whose detailed inter-se terms (veto rights, exit rights, drag/tag) are not disclosed in the pool (Insufficient Data). Minorities did receive a priced exit (open offer at ₹504.41/sh).

#### 4A. Market Conduct

| Signal | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Insider buys/sells vs subsequent results (timing) | **No individual-insider open-market buy or sell is recorded in the pool**, so trade-timing against results is not assessable. The only large ownership move is the negotiated CVC→KKR block sale (30-May-2025), a control transaction with a disclosed SPA price, not opportunistic insider trading. No insider sale precedes the weak FY26 reported PAT (₹138m). | CIQ Public Ownership, Dec-31-2025 (no individual increased/decreased/sold-out positions); FY24-25 AR §15 |
| Unusual price / volume before announcements | **Not assessable from the pool** — no intraday price/volume series or exchange surveillance record is in the data set. business-model/12_red-flags-sweep surfaced **no** market-conduct (price/volume) flag. | business-model/12_red-flags-sweep |
| Exchange clarification sought for a price move | **None disclosed** in the pool. The only "SEBI" references in FY26 filings are routine Reg-30/Schedule-III cover-letter headings, not actions or clarifications. | business-model/01_disqualifier-scan §1, item 6 |
| Institutional (FII/DII/MF) holding trend; any sharp high-quality-holder exit | **Mixed, no sharp quality exit.** MF holding 9.19% (FY25, 31-Mar-2025). At Dec-31-2025: Nippon Life 5.29%, Tata AMC 3.16% retained; Helios and Trust AMC added new positions; **Motilal Oswal cut its position by 2,829,475 sh** (to 4,276,983, May-04-2026) and a few small funds trimmed (ITI −6,405; CREF sold out 20,633). New institutional money (Sovereign-wealth and hedge-fund holders <5%) appeared post-changeover. **No wholesale high-quality-holder exit.** | CIQ Public Ownership, Institutional Activity + Top Holders Activity, Dec-31-2025 / May-04-2026 |
| **Promoter-linked related-party divestiture (insider as counterparty)** | **Flag — monitor.** The Milann fertility business is being **sold to Inviga Healthcare Fund I, managed by Inviga Investment Advisors Pvt. Ltd., "an entity controlled by Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar, Promoter and Non-Executive Chairman."** EV ₹632m; equity consideration ₹376m (75% / ₹282m upfront). The founder/Chairman is the buyer of a business divested by the company he chairs — a promoter-linked RPT. Signed May-2026; close expected Q1 FY27. | FY26 deck (19-May-2026), "Milann Divestment Update", p.5; FY26 transcript (20-May-2026), prepared remarks |

**Market-conduct read:** No insider open-market selling preceded the weak FY26 bottom line, and no buying preceded good news — there is simply **no individual-insider trading to read**, because ownership change here is a sponsor block transfer, not management dealing. RF-MKT-001 and RF-MKT-002 are **not triggered** (no evidence of timed insider trades; no price/volume anomaly in the pool). The one item warranting monitoring is the **Milann sale to a founder-controlled fund** — a related-party transaction with the Non-Executive Chairman as counterparty; its fairness/pricing is owned by business-model (RPT lens), but as an ownership/control signal it is a conflict to watch, not a triggered market-conduct flag. Trade-timing data is otherwise unavailable.

#### 5. Ownership / Insider Read

Skin in the game is a **financial sponsor's cash-bought control block (KKR/Hector Asia, 50.84%), not management buying with personal conviction** — the CEO and incoming CFO are KKR appointees with no disclosed personal stake, and the founder's residual ~9.22% is a legacy pre-IPO holding that he neither added to nor visibly sold on the changeover, so the insider signal is **neutral, not bullish**. The net insider "activity" of the last year was the negotiated CVC→KKR block transfer (51.59% at a disclosed SPA price plus a 26% open offer at ₹504.41/sh) — a control transaction, not an open-market vote of confidence, and there is no insider open-market buying or selling to read as conviction or as a red flag. The biggest control/pledging risk to minorities is **not pledging (none is disclosed) and not vote-rigging (one ordinary class, no super-voting) but PE-horizon alignment**: a single sponsor controls the votes on an exit clock, the founder shares promoter status under an undisclosed-terms Promoter Agreement, and the Chairman is simultaneously the buyer of the divested Milann business (a promoter-linked RPT to monitor) — with the post-changeover Reg-31 encumbrance line still unverified.

#### Ownership Alignment Score

*Higher = better. The Insider/promoter-ownership component scores HIGH on quantum (a 50.84% controlling block + ~9.22% founder), but the "bought vs granted quality" and "recent insider behavior" components score the CONVICTION dimension, which is weak here because the dominant owner is a PE sponsor (institutional, exit-clock) rather than founders/operators buying personally, and there is no individual-insider open-market buying to read. No partial-data cap binds (ownership and insider data ARE present per triage §5); confidence is reduced where the post-changeover detailed pattern relies on a vendor source.*

| Component | Score | Max Score | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Insider / promoter ownership | 19 | 25 | High quantum: controlling KKR block 50.84% + founder 9.22% + institutions 19.66% [04-001/003/006]; audited promoter group 71.22% at FY25 [04-004]. Scored below max because the dominant block is a financial sponsor's, not operator-owned, and current operating management (CEO/CFO) holds no disclosed personal stake [04-005]. |
| Bought vs granted ownership quality | 11 | 15 | Genuinely bought, not granted: KKR paid cash for 51.59% + open offer [04-002]; founder stake is pre-IPO/founder, not comp [04-003]; ESOP dilution minimal (<0.3%) [04-009]. Scored below max because "bought for cash by a PE fund on an exit clock" is lower-quality alignment than founders/operators buying personally to hold. |
| Recent insider behavior | 11 | 20 | Neutral signal: no individual-insider open-market buying (no conviction) and no insider selling (no red flag) in 12m [04-007]; the only move is a sponsor-to-sponsor block transfer, not open-market dealing [04-008]. Mid-band — neither a positive conviction signal nor a negative one. |
| Pledge / encumbrance risk | 17 | 20 | No promoter/insider pledge disclosed; pledge disqualifier N; the only "pledge" is the Company's own subsidiary-investment collateral, not promoter shares [04-010/011]. Not full marks: the post-changeover KKR-block encumbrance line is unverified (Insufficient Data) [04-012]. |
| Control-structure fairness | 6 | 10 | Clean on structure (one ordinary class, no dual-class/super-voting [04-013]; minorities got a priced open-offer exit at ₹504.41 [04-016]). Marked down for sole PE control on an exit horizon [04-014], an undisclosed-terms KKR+founder Promoter Agreement [04-015], and a founder-as-counterparty RPT (Milann) [04-020]. |
| Institutional-holder quality / trend | 6 | 10 | Diversified institutional float (Nippon 5.29%, Tata 3.16%, Motilal 2.86%); MF 9.19% (FY25); new SWF/HF money post-changeover; no wholesale high-quality exit, but Motilal trimmed −2.83m sh [04-006/019]. Confidence-limited (vendor data, conf 2). |
| **Total** | **70** | **100** | **Strong band, low end.** Pulled up by high promoter ownership genuinely bought for cash, no pledging, and a clean (single-class) control structure with a priced minority exit. Pulled down by the fact that the dominant owner is a PE sponsor (institutional/exit-clock, not operator-owned), no individual-insider conviction buying, sole PE control under an undisclosed-terms Promoter Agreement, and a founder-linked Milann RPT. Net: ownership is aligned in *quantum* but the *conviction* and *minority-horizon* dimensions are mid. |

## MG-05 · board-and-shareholder-rights — Board & Shareholder Rights — HCG

### Board & Shareholder Rights — HCG

*HealthCare Global Enterprises Limited (NSE: HCG / BSE: 539787). Indian SEBI-LODR issuer, Ind AS, FY ends 31 March. Reporting currency INR (₹). Report date: 2026-06-01. The proxy-equivalent is the AGM Notice + Corporate Governance Report (CG Report); takeover/voting features come from the Companies Act 2013 + SEBI LODR + the Articles of Association as summarised in the AR. The only audited board/CG disclosure in the pool is the FY2024-25 Integrated Annual Report (year ended 31-Mar-2025, filed Sep-2025; auditor signed 24-May-2025). The post-KKR-changeover board (from 30-May / 30-Jun-2025) is disclosed in that AR's "subsequent events" sections and the 20-May-2026 Q4/FY26 transcript; a FY26 audited CG Report is NOT in the pool. Where I rely on post-31-Mar-2025 facts I say so.*

Scope: this agent answers whether the board is a real check on management and whether shareholder rights protect minority holders. It does not assess ownership/insider behaviour (agent 04), compensation magnitude/metrics (agent 03), value the company, or rate the stock. Upstream inputs read and used: in-module `01_management-and-track-record.md` and `00_governance-data-triage.md`; cross-module `business-model/01_disqualifier-scan.md` (RPT disqualifier — owned there, referenced here) and `business-model/11_capital-allocation-governance.md`. All were available.

**Governing fact for this read:** HCG is private-equity-controlled, not founder-controlled. KKR's Hector Asia Holdings II Pte Ltd acquired sole control (~51.59%) on 30-May-2025 under an SPA dated 23-Feb-2025, with a parallel **Promoter Agreement** that gives the controlling owner the right to nominate directors and reconstitute every board committee [FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §15, §19.4, p.76-79; CG Report, p.120-127]. The board and all committees were reconstituted on 30-May-2025 around KKR nominees. So the board's independence has to be judged against a control bloc that holds ~51-71% of the votes and explicit nomination rights — the classic minority-shareholder exposure for a PE-controlled listed company.

#### 1. Board Composition

*Two snapshots, because control changed mid-cycle. "As on 31-Mar-2025" is the audited statutory composition; "post-changeover" reflects the 30-May / 30-Jun-2025 reconstitution disclosed in the same AR + FY26 transcript.*

| Feature | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Board size | 8 directors as on 31-Mar-2025 (2 Executive, 2 Non-Exec Non-Independent nominees of Aceso/CVC, 4 Independent). Post-changeover: founder (Non-Exec Chair), Anjali Rossi (Non-Exec), 2 KKR nominees (S. Mehta, A. Tanna), CEO Mattoo (Exec Director), plus 5 Independents (Mathur, Raghavan, Kanakia, Maliwal + Bijou Kurien added 30-Jun-2025) ≈ **10 directors** | FY24-25 AR, CG Report p.120 + §K table p.127; Directors' Report §19.3-19.4 p.78-79; in-module `01` |
| Independent directors (%) | **50% (4 of 8) as on 31-Mar-2025**, all four certified free of material relationships per Companies Act §149(6) / LODR. Post-changeover ≈ 50% (≈5 of ≈10). Meets the SEBI LODR Reg-17 floor (a board with a **non-independent** chair requires ≥50% independent) but does not exceed it | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report p.77; CG Report p.120; declaration of independence §17 (AGM Notice) p.4391 |
| Chair / CEO split? | **Yes, split — but the chair is NOT independent.** Chair = founder Dr. B.S. Ajaikumar (Non-Executive, **Non-Independent** / promoter group, w.e.f. 30-May-2025, term to 30-Jun-2030); CEO = Dr. Manish Mattoo (Executive Director, KKR nominee, w.e.f. 30-Jun-2025). Roles are separated, but a promoter/founder chair is weaker than an independent chair | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §19.4(c) p.78, §21(a) p.79; board profiles p.38; in-module `01` |
| Audit committee independence | **Fully independent-majority and independent-chaired.** Reconstituted 30-May-2025: Geeta Mathur (ID, Chairperson), R. Raghavan (ID), P. Kanakia (ID), Simrun Mehta (KKR nominee, Non-Independent member) — 3 of 4 independent, ID chair. Met 4x in FY25; "all recommendations accepted by the Board." Complies with Companies Act §177 / LODR Reg-18 | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §22(A) p.79-80, §21 reconstitution p.79; CG Report Audit Committee section |
| Nomination & Remuneration committee independence | Reconstituted 30-May-2025: R. Raghavan (ID, Chair), Geeta Mathur (ID), P. Kanakia (ID), Rajiv Maliwal (ID), A. Tanna (KKR-NI), S. Mehta (KKR-NI) — 4 of 6 independent, ID chair. Met 5x in FY25 | FY24-25 AR, §22(C) p.81; reconstitution p.80-81 |
| Nominating function | India has no separate "nominating" committee; the NRC performs director nomination. Per the Promoter Agreement, the controlling owner nominates directors and the NRC recommends them — nomination is **controller-driven**, a structural limit on board self-renewal | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §19.4(b)/(d) p.78; CG Report |
| Stakeholders' Relationship Committee | Reconstituted 30-May-2025: S. Mehta (KKR-NI, Chair), R. Raghavan (ID), A. Tanna (KKR-NI) — chaired by a non-independent KKR nominee; majority non-independent. Met 1x in FY25 | FY24-25 AR, §22(D) p.81 |

Read: the **audit and nomination/remuneration committees are independent-majority and independent-chaired** — the two committees that most protect minority holders are correctly composed and LODR-compliant. The board clears the 50% independence floor but does not exceed it, the chair is a non-independent founder rather than an independent director, and director nomination runs through the controlling owner under the Promoter Agreement. This is standard professional governance constrained by PE control, not an entrenched-insider board — but the independence is at the regulatory minimum, not a margin of safety.

#### 2. Tenure & Refreshment

| Signal | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Average independent-director tenure | Independents appointed/re-appointed: Mathur (since 17-Jun-2021, re-app 5 yrs from Jun-2024), Raghavan (since 12-Aug-2021, re-app 5 yrs from Aug-2024), Kanakia (since 10-Feb-2022, re-app 5 yrs from Feb-2025), Maliwal (since 25-May-2023), Kurien (new, 30-Jun-2025). Average ID tenure ≈ **3-4 years** — not stale; all within first or early-second SEBI 5-year term | FY24-25 AR, §K Director table p.127; Directors' Report §19.2 p.77-78 |
| Refreshment | **Very high.** Two CVC nominee directors (Patel, Soni) resigned 30-May-2025; ID J. Venugopal resigned 09-Nov-2024 (cited "professional and personal commitments," confirmed no other material reason); CEO/WTD Gore's term ended; 2 KKR nominees (Mehta, Tanna) + 1 new ID (Kurien) + new Exec Director/CEO (Mattoo) joined in 2025; founder re-designated Exec→Non-Exec Chair. The board turned over substantially with the control change | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §19.3-19.4 p.78-79; CG Report p.121 |
| Overboarding | **Yes — flag two independents.** As on 31-Mar-2025: **Geeta Mathur held 10 directorships** (incl. Dixon Technologies, Info Edge) and **7 committee memberships (4 chair)**; **Pradip Kanakia held 10 directorships** (incl. ICRA, Sona BLW, Britannia, Camlin Fine Sciences, JM Financial) and **8 committee memberships (4 chair)**. Both sit at the SEBI directorship/committee ceilings. KKR nominee A. Tanna lists ~8 boards (J B Chemicals, Healthium, Rebel Foods, etc.). The AR confirms no director exceeds the 10-committee / 5-chairmanship statutory cap | FY24-25 AR, §K Director table p.127, p.2658-2662 (Kanakia profile); note p.127 (caps met) |
| Attendance | Board met 8x in FY25; max gap <120 days. **Rajiv Maliwal did NOT attend the last AGM** (25-Sep-2024). Committee attendance generally full; Kanakia attended 2 of relevant NRC meetings (joined NRC mid-year, 31-Dec-2024) | FY24-25 AR, §20 p.79; §K table p.127 (AGM attendance); committee tables p.80-81 |
| Board evaluation | Annual performance evaluation of board, committees, chair and individual directors conducted per the SEBI Jan-2019 guidance note; directors "expressed satisfaction." No external/independent evaluator disclosed | FY24-25 AR, §23 Board Evaluation p.81-82 |

Read: tenure is **not stale** (IDs are in their first/early-second terms; board heavily refreshed in 2025). The live signal is **overboarding** — two of the four independents (Mathur, Kanakia) carry 10 directorships each and 7-8 committee seats each, at the regulatory ceiling, which can dilute the attention these two give HCG even though the count is technically compliant. One director (Maliwal) missed the last AGM. These are Medium-severity attention/quality signals, not entrenchment.

#### 3. Related-Party Transactions & Group Leakage

*Figures consolidated unless stated; intra-group transactions (HCG's own subsidiaries/LLPs) eliminate on consolidation and are not leakage. The leakage test is on transactions with **outside** related parties — KMP, the founder family, and entities the founder/KMP control. FY25 consolidated revenue from operations ≈ ₹22,181.4 Mn; total expenses ≈ ₹22,014.7 Mn; consolidated net worth is the relevant base for the materiality matrix.*

| RPT Type | Amount (FY25, ₹ Mn) | % of Revenue | % of PAT | % of Net Worth | Arm's Length? | Source |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Sales to / income from outside related parties (Sada Sarada, HCG Foundation, GHA Academy, etc.) | ≈ 25-30 (e.g., Sada Sarada sale ₹4.92m; HCG Foundation income ₹15.98m; GHA course-marketing ₹19.17m) | <0.15% | n/m (small) | <0.1% | Yes — stated arm's-length, ordinary course | FY24-25 AR, Consol. Note 43 p.371-372; `business-model/11` |
| Purchases from / payments to outside related parties | ≈ 2-20 (e.g., capital asset from Sada Sarada ₹18.20m; purchases from subs eliminate) | <0.1% | n/m | <0.06% | Yes — stated arm's-length | FY24-25 AR, Consol. Note 43 p.372; AOC-2 p.118 |
| Royalty / brand / management fees to promoter group | **0 — none disclosed.** No royalty, brand, trademark, or management fee to the founder/promoter or to KKR | 0% | 0% | 0% | N/A | FY24-25 AR, Note 43/44; word-search of AR (no royalty/brand-fee RPT) |
| Rent / other payments to promoter entities | None material disclosed | <0.1% | n/m | <0.1% | Yes | FY24-25 AR, Consol. Note 43 p.371-372 |
| Outstanding receivables/payables from outside related parties | Tiny: HCG Foundation trade payable ₹0.17m; Sada Sarada other payable ₹nil (FY25); post-employment benefit provisions to KMP (founder ₹3.76m, others <₹2.5m each) | <0.02% | n/m | <0.02% | Yes; unsecured, settled per terms | FY24-25 AR, Consol. Note 43 "Balances outstanding" p.372 |
| Common-control business transfers (intra-group reorganisation) | NCHRI Nagpur oncology slump-sale (BTA, Board-approved 09-Nov-2024); diagnostic/cyclotron transfer to NCHRI ₹1,346.09m; these are **between HCG and its own subsidiaries** | n/a (eliminates) | n/a | n/a | **NOT at fair value** (pooling-of-interests; disclosed, common-control) | FY24-25 AR, AOC-2 p.118; Note 45.1; Std. Note 44 p.288 |

**RPT materiality and leakage read.** Applying the MODULE_RULES RPT thresholds: outside-party RPTs are **<1% of consolidated revenue and a fraction of net worth — Low/Green materiality.** RPTs are recurring (course-marketing, diagnostic-services income, small capital-asset purchases), stated arm's-length, placed before the Audit Committee, with omnibus/yearly approval per LODR Reg-23 [FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §13 p.74; CARO clause (xiii) RPT-compliant, p.224]. They are **not growing faster than revenue** in a way that signals leakage. **There is no royalty, brand, or management fee to the founder or to KKR** — the most common promoter-leakage channel in Indian listcos is absent. **`business-model/01_disqualifier-scan` confirms the >25%-of-revenue RPT hard disqualifier is NOT triggered** (BRSR share-of-RPT-in-sales 0.0012% FY25; consolidated outside RPTs ~1%) — that disqualifier is owned there and I do not re-decide it.

Two honest caveats, neither a leakage flag: (1) the NCHRI Nagpur transfer was explicitly executed "other than at fair value," but it is a **common-control pooling-of-interests** transaction between HCG and its own wholly-owned subsidiary (mandated by Ind AS 103 Appendix C), Board-approved and disclosed — value moves *within* the consolidated group, not out to the promoter [AOC-2 p.118]. (2) The promoter-funded bonus to KMPs (Section 4A) is paid by the promoter, not the Company — no company cash leaks. **Core answer: economic value is not leaking to the promoter/KKR or to the founder family through RPTs.** No RF-RPT-001/002/003 trigger fires.

#### 4. Takeover Defenses & Voting Rights

*India's framework is statutory (Companies Act 2013 + SEBI LODR + SEBI SAST/Takeover Code); HCG's Articles were amended in FY25 (postal ballot 27-Apr-2025) and adopted. US-style poison pills/staggered boards are generally not used; the relevant minority protections are one-share-one-vote, the SAST mandatory open offer, and Companies-Act meeting/consent rights.*

| Feature | Present? | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Poison pill | No | No shareholder-rights plan / poison pill (not a feature of Indian listcos). Takeover defence is statutory, not company-specific | FY24-25 AR; SEBI SAST framework |
| Classified / staggered board | No | Directors are appointed/re-appointed by shareholder vote; ⅓ of non-independent directors retire by rotation each AGM (Anjali Rossi retiring by rotation at the 27th AGM). Independents serve fixed 5-yr terms (statutory), not a defensive stagger | FY24-25 AR, Directors' Report §19.5 p.79; AGM Notice p.166 |
| Dual-class / unequal voting | **No** | Single class of equity, face value ₹10; **"voting rights are determined based on the paid-up value of shares held"** — one-share-one-vote. No DVR/superior-voting shares; no GDR/ADR or outstanding warrants as of FY25 | FY24-25 AR, CG Report "Procedure for Postal Ballot" p.143 (line 9164); item 39 (proportional voting) p.462; §T (no warrants outstanding) p.9460 |
| Majority vs plurality director voting | Majority (statutory) | Director appointments are ordinary resolutions (>50% of votes cast); ID re-appointments are special resolutions (≥75%). No plurality carve-out. SAST: mandatory **26% open offer at ₹504.41/share** was triggered by the KKR acquisition — a minority-exit protection | FY24-25 AR, postal-ballot results p.142; Directors' Report §15 (open offer) p.77 |
| Shareholder ability to call meetings / act by written consent | Statutory | Holders of ≥10% can requisition an EGM (Companies Act §100); postal-ballot / e-voting is available on all resolutions; resolutions need a statutory quorum. No written-consent-in-lieu (that is a US concept). Minority rights to call meetings exist but are diluted by a ~51-71% control bloc | FY24-25 AR, AGM Notice e-voting + quorum p.310, p.904; Companies Act §100 |

Read: **voting rights are intact and clean** — one-share-one-vote, no dual-class, no poison pill, no staggered board, and the SAST 26% open offer gave dissenting minorities an exit at ₹504.41. The genuine limit on minority power is arithmetic, not structural: with the promoter bloc holding ~51-71% of votes, the controlling owner can carry any ordinary resolution and (with founder-group support) most special resolutions, so minority votes are protected in form but cannot outvote control. That is the standard PE-controlled-listco condition, not an abusive takeover-defence structure.

#### 4A. Shareholder Voting & Dilution

| Signal | Detail | Source |
|---|---|---|
| AGM/postal-ballot votes against | Most resolutions passed with **>96% support** (ID re-appointments 96.14-99.93% for; AoA amendment 96.88%; ID-remuneration 96.75%; ESOP-surrender option 99.97%). **One resolution drew 25.99% votes against**: "proposed payment of bonus to identified KMP and employees" (postal ballot 26-Mar-2025, results 28-Apr-2025) — 74.01% for, 25.99% against on a small valid-vote base (1,92,27,825 net e-votes vs ~11.86 cr on other items, i.e., the promoter abstained as an interested party) | FY24-25 AR, CG Report postal-ballot tables p.142 (lines 9119-9145) |
| Substance of the high-against resolution | The bonus was proposed and **funded by the promoter (Aceso/CVC parent AIHPL), with "no financial obligation on the Company"** — a promoter-paid exit reward to KMPs/employees, approved under LODR Reg-26(6). So the 25.99% against is real minority discomfort with a promoter-affiliated pay arrangement, but **no company cash leaks** | FY24-25 AR, Consol. Note 44 Managerial Remuneration p.373 (lines 25419-25427) |
| Proxy-advisor recommendations (ISS / Glass Lewis / IiAS / InGovern) | **Not in pool** — no proxy-advisor report available. Cannot confirm whether the 25.99%-against reflected an IiAS/InGovern "against" recommendation | Pool search — Insufficient Data |
| Institutional opposition | Implied by the 25.99% against on the bonus resolution (institutional/MF holders ≈ MF 9.19% + FPI 2.45% per FY25 pattern); not separately itemised by holder type in the pool | FY24-25 AR shareholding pattern p.64; postal-ballot tables p.142 |
| Dilution history | (a) **Preferential warrants to founder + promoter group in FY21/FY22** at ₹130/share (Aceso 1,15,03,468 warrants; Dr. Ajaikumar 20,00,000 warrants; Bhagya Ajaikumar block) — historic, disclosed, fund-utilisation reported. (b) ESOP dilution minimal (shares 139.12m→139.42m through FY25, <0.3%). (c) **FY26 rights issue ₹425 cr** to cut leverage to 1.4x — pro-rata, so non-dilutive to participating minorities; exact post-issue share count not yet in pool | FY24-25 AR, CG Report §P preferential-allotment utilisation p.142-143 (lines 9358-9425), Note 15.1 share reconciliation p.258; Q4/FY26 transcript (rights issue); `business-model/11` |

**Voting/dilution read.** The 25.99% votes-against on the KMP-bonus resolution clears the MODULE_RULES Red-Flag threshold (>20% against on a remuneration-type resolution) and is logged as **RF-SHR-001 (High)** — it is a genuine minority-protest signal that the comp specialist (agent 03) should weigh. Its bite is softened because the bonus was promoter-funded (no company cash) and the promoter recused itself, so the "against" is on a small interested-party-excluded base. Otherwise voting is supportive (>96% on everything else) and dilution has been benign: a pro-rata FY26 rights issue (not a discounted preferential allotment) and trivial ESOP creep. The historic FY21/FY22 founder/promoter warrants were a disclosed dilution-with-cash-in event, not a flag today.

#### 5. Minority-Shareholder Protection Read

The board is a **real but constrained check on management**: the audit and nomination/remuneration committees are independent-chaired and independent-majority, the chair/CEO roles are split, board independence meets the 50% LODR floor, voting is clean one-share-one-vote with a SAST open-offer exit, and RPTs are immaterial with **no royalty/brand/management-fee leakage to the promoter or founder** — so the core risk that a controlled listco siphons value is not present in the data. What it is *not* is an independent-controlled board with a margin of safety: HCG is ~51-71% controlled, the chair is a non-independent founder, directors are nominated by the controlling owner under a Promoter Agreement that lets it reconstitute every committee, independence sits exactly at the regulatory minimum, and two of the four independents are at the SEBI overboarding ceiling. The single biggest entrenchment/exposure risk for minority holders is **control concentration itself** — with a KKR/founder bloc that can carry any ordinary resolution, minority votes are protected in form but cannot prevail, and the 25.99%-against KMP-bonus vote shows minorities will register dissent that they cannot translate into outcomes (a PE sponsor running the company to its own exit timeline, not a self-dealing promoter). Conclusion: **adequate** minority-shareholder protection — clean rights and committees, no value leakage, but independence only at the floor and real power held by the controlling bloc.

#### Board & Shareholder Rights Score

*Higher = better minority-shareholder protection. Maps to the module's "Shareholder friendliness /100." Partial-data note: the only audited CG Report is FY24-25 (pre/at the changeover); the post-KKR FY26 CG Report and proxy-advisor data are not in the pool, so independence-margin and voting-trend items are scored on FY25 + the disclosed reconstitution and flagged.*

| Component | Score | Max Score | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Board independence | 11 | 20 | 50% independent — meets but does not exceed the LODR floor for a non-independent (founder) chair [05-001]; chair/CEO split but chair is promoter/founder, not independent [05-002]; director nomination is controller-driven under the Promoter Agreement [05-005]. Compliant, not a margin of safety. |
| True independence / tenure / overboarding | 12 | 20 | IDs are SEBI-certified independent and not stale (3-4 yr tenure, first/early-second terms) [05-006]; board heavily refreshed in 2025 [05-009]. Marked down for **overboarding** — two of four IDs (Mathur, Kanakia) at the 10-directorship / 7-8-committee ceiling [05-007] — and one AGM absence [05-008]. |
| Committee quality | 13 | 15 | Audit committee independent-majority + ID-chaired, recommendations accepted by Board [05-003]; NRC independent-majority + ID-chaired [05-004]. Minor: SRC chaired by a non-independent KKR nominee. The two committees that protect minorities are correctly composed. |
| RPT and group-leakage protection | 18 | 20 | Outside-party RPTs <1% of revenue, arm's-length, AC/omnibus-approved [05-010]; **no royalty/brand/management fee to promoter or KKR** [05-011]; >25% disqualifier not triggered [05-013]. Slightly short of full for the disclosed not-at-fair-value common-control NCHRI transfer (intra-group, no external leakage) [05-012]. |
| Shareholder voting rights | 14 | 15 | One-share-one-vote, single class, no DVR/warrants [05-014]; no poison pill or staggered board; SAST 26% open offer gave a minority exit [05-015]. Marked down only for statutory meeting/consent rights being diluted by the control bloc [05-016]. |
| AGM opposition / minority protection | 5 | 10 | **25.99% votes against the KMP-bonus resolution — RF-SHR-001** [05-017]; minorities register dissent they cannot turn into outcomes given ~51-71% control [05-021]. Offset by >96% support on all other resolutions [05-018] and benign, pro-rata FY26 dilution [05-019]. No proxy-advisor data to corroborate [05-020]. |
| Total | **73** | 100 | **Strong-but-constrained / "adequate" minority protection.** Clean voting rights, clean committees, and no RPT leakage pull the score into the low-"strong" band; independence only at the regulatory floor, overboarded independents, a non-independent chair, the 25.99%-against pay vote, and a ~51-71% control bloc keep it from higher. Read alongside the **High** RF-SHR-001 flag. |
